description: The first Theater Expansion for Disposable Heroes and Coffin For Seven Brothers: Infantry and Vehicle Small Unit Skirmish Wargaming in WWII covers the war in the Pacific with the Japanese, the USMC and the Chinese Army.
family: Series: Disposable Heroes Miniature Rules (Iron Ivan Games)

boardgame: Futile Gallantry: The Fight Against Tyranny in the Early Days of World War II, 1939-1940
description:
From the publisher website:

The Fight Against Tyranny in the Early Days of World War II, 1939-1940: Includes the information you need to field French, Belgian, Dutch, Norwegian, Danish, Polish, and Finnish troops. 80 pages of infantry platoons, artillery, vehicles and aircraft. As a bonus, information for the Continuation War Finns is included. Note: Early War Germans are found in Angriff!, Early War British are found in Go Forward Together.

An Early Days expansion for Disposable Heroes and Coffin For Seven Brothers: Infantry and Vehicle Small Unit Skirmish Wargaming in WWII
family: Series: Disposable Heroes Miniature Rules (Iron Ivan Games)

description:
A boardgame based on the Japanese comic strip Urusei Yatsura. Each player plays one of four characters from the comic: Lum, Ataru, Mendo or Shinobu. The players compete to obtain presents.

Components include mapsheet, stand up figures, and 3-d presents.

Hush! is a children's game with no strategy involved, so everyone is equally good!

Each turn, a player turns over one of the hidden tiles. If it is a strawberry, then he or she can take it. If it is a bird, the collected strawberries are "saved" and put aside. If it is a scarecrow, the player must say "HUSH!", and all other players must put back all strawberries they haven't saved yet.

The game ends when all tiles are taken, and the winner is the one with the most strawberries.

Important hint from the rules: If someone has not learned to count yet, the players can stack all strawberries and compare height instead.

Contents:

54 tiles with pictures of strawberries, birds and scarecrows

A simple game in which players must spin a spinner to determine movement.  If a player reaches a space with an outlaw token they spin the spinner again to see whether they "hit" and capture the outlaw (picking up the token) or miss and have to move again.  The first player to capture at least three outlaws and get back to Dodge City wins.

From the publisher:

"Do you like to F*ck? If so, you'll love this f*cking game! Use the spinner to move around the board until one of your markers lands on the bed. Then you spin the spinner and start F*cking in the position the spinner points to."

Components: Board, spinner, four pawns.

From the publisher:

"Porn! is an easy-to-learn strategy card game where you and the other players compete to own the most porn movies. As you build your stash, you also try to prevent other players from building larger collections. You can steal movies by correctly answering a porn trivia question, triple your holdings with the Porn Mogul Card, and earn extra turns by filming a movie. Running out of cards closes the round, but watch out for the Arson! card that can destroy it all."

Aim to attract the most agile ninja into your new Ninja School, supply them with equipment then send them out to attack other ninja schools to maintain your position as the best master.

Players collect ninja in 7 different styles of fighting, each ninja being of different strength. The game ends when one person has played one ninja from five different styles into their school or seven ninjas in total from any style. Points are scored for the value of the ninja and bonuses depending on how many styles are in the school and how many ninja are in each style.

Players may equip their ninja with items to improve their strength, stealth or weapons which are kept hidden until they are attacked or are sent out to attack another players ninja.

From the publisher:

"The crazy game of pot slang! A player rolls the dice, then selects two of the dice and announces a unique two-word pot slang term. This term must make sense to the other players.
Examples are 'Bong Water', 'Pot Head', and 'Grass Brownies'. For 2-5 players."

From the publisher:

"The hilarious drinking game where you and your friends attempt to OUT GROSS each other. Four gruesome categories and over 400 disgusting actions! Ham it up by doing overacted impersonations, name items that fit into vulgar categories, judge your friends in their efforts to gross each other out, and much, much more!!! Packaged in a dog poop shaped tin."

Components: Deck of cards, pad, pencil, custom six-sided die.

"The game of erotic education and sexual satisfaction!"

"You and your lover learn about each other's libidos while traveling around the game board. The woman asks the man erotic questions about how to please her, and the man asks the woman erotic questions about how to please him. Some cards offer general love-making tips and others are more personal to the likes and dislikes of the lover asking the question. The winner enjoys the reward of selecting three cards to interpret and act out during lovemaking."

"Includes: a durable gameboard, 52 Ask Him cards, 52 Ask Her cards, two game markers, a die, and easy-to-follow instructions. Packaged in an attractive game tin!"

The players searches for Noddy and his friends all around Toy Land by finding pieces of their puzzle.

The first player to complete his puzzle and return to the starting square is the winner.

Contents:

Game board
12 pices with Noddy and his friends
4 wooden pawns
4 puzzles
Die

Zombie Wars is a quick tactical game for 2 to 4 players. Each player starts the game with a small horde of zombies. During the game you attack clusters of townspeople, adding those who die to your horde. Your goal is to build a zombie horde that’s large enough to annihilate the hordes of your rivals. If you have the most zombies on the board at the end of the game, you win.

Zombie Wars uses a custom board, tokens, and zombie horde tracker; a bunch of tokens; about 12 six-sided dice; at least two eight-sided dice; and a few ten-, twelve-, and twenty-sided dice. A four-player game takes about 90 minutes to play. Zombie Wars is appropriate for ages 15 and older.

Home Page: http://www.invisible-city.com/play/446/zombie-wars (free rules, board, and pieces)

boardgame: The Comical Game of Mother Hubbard
description: Based on the nursery rhyme.  Be the first player to fill your cupboard with each of four items:  grapes, bread, fish, and a bone!

Mitt aller første kryssordspill (My very first crosswords game) is a game that introduces children to the concept of crosswords. During a turn, the player must try to find as many letters as possible to a given word amongst the hidden letters.

There are two game boards, one for the smallest children with with all letters written down, and one for older children that can spell, with some blank squares.

Contents:

2 game boards
84 letter tiles

Multimillionær (Multi-millionaire) is a Monopoly-like game, but with more advanced economics.

The game includes a stock market, and if one player owns more that 8 (of the 10) stocks of a colour, he or she will get 50% of the income.

Also, there are no prison, only the "Ideal holiday resort", in which the players must pay (progressively more) to stay each round.

There is no income for a completed round, instead there is a dummy pawn traveling around paying rent to the players.

The game ends if all but one player are bankrupt, if one player has 2 million kroner in values, or at a given time.

Månetoppen (Moon Top) is a spin-off from the children's television show "Jul på Månetoppen" (Christmas at Moon Top) by NRK (Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation).

The players must collect as many blueberries as possible before the winter comes.

After finding a blueberry, they can immediately to the start square, or continue searching for blueberry, risking to lose the bucket and all collected berries.

Players
Contents:

Game board
4 pawns
36 månetoppen cards
45 blueberry cards
4 blueberry buckets

Final Argument of Kings is a product of the same circle of players and designers who produced the Squad Leader board game and the Johnny Reb Civil War miniature rules.  Final Argument bears a strong resemblance to the latter but was designed to tune to the warfare of the middle part of the 18th century.  These rules are appropriate for games set from the time of the War of the Polish Succession (1734) to the beginning of the Wars of the French Revolution (1789.)  It is scaled to be appropriate for battle on the European continent.  For 15mm figures, one inch on the table represents 45-50 yards.

Horse and foot units are each four stands and represent infantry battalions and cavalry regiments.  Artillery batteries are single stands as are army, wing, and brigade commanders.  The number of figures per unit is variable with each figure representing 40 soldiers or one section of artillery.  Stand size is variable with the number of figures so that units with more men cover a wider frontage.  (The game would be playable with other basing systems, but the game's basing has a number of subtleties that transmit the tactical feel of the time.  For example, units are longer in march column than they are wide in line as, indeed, they should be.)

Turns follow a set sequence with simultaneous movement and fire of various types.  Each turn represents 20 minutes of time.  Players mark orders for units that are hidden from the opposing player.  Command is an important element but does not dominate the game or become obtrusive.  There is also provision for grand tactical movement so that maneuver and closing to battle may happen quickly without taking too much time nor sacrificing tactical detail.  Combat tends to be bloody with stands taking figure losses as a result.  Morale of units is a key game concept.  Units have a basic morale point that is altered by loss of strength and the local situation faced by the units.  Battlefield events trigger morale checks.  There are no saving roles.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: 1650 more Questions & Answers for use with Trivial Pursuit: Set I, Group I
description:
From the Back of the Box:

1650 more Questions & Answers for use with Trivial Pursuit: Genus Edition...

Happy triviaphiles will recognize the Q&A's in this set from Set I of the award-winning Game of Trivia by the Avalon Hill Game company.

Now - we make these same questions & answers available for devotees of Trivial Pursuit, without having you pay for a complete game.

Here - in this box - are 1,650 additional Q&A's, color-coded for play with Trivial Pursuit (and other similar games of trivia).

And... if 1,650 of them aren't enough, check out the additional groups - labeled 1 thru 4 - to complete the set of 6,600 additional questions and answers.

OMNI Magazine - December '84 - selected Game of Trivia by The Avalon Hill Game Company... "best trivia game of the year, based on its variety of questions and low ratio of arguable answers."

GAMES Magazine - September '84 - editors' choice for GAMES 100 because... "Game of Trivia plays more like a true board game, and there's no reason why you can't use the questions for Trivial Pursuit."

On October 14, 1920, the last major White offensive took place. It would become Wrangel's Kursk, a precursor to that more famous WW2 battle at Kursk but this happened at Kakhovka, a small town astride the Dnepr River.

Unique for it was only the second time in the RCW that a mass tank attack would occur (the first was in the Summer of 1919), fully supported by DH9A bombers and infantry against a very well prepared Red defense consisting of three consecutive trench lines, mines and barb wire. WW1 was in effect "revisited". General Wrangel placed his blind faith in the tanks he had procured from the British, His tankers were well trained. He had even developed a combined arms approach. His forces, though outnumbered by 2 to 1 or more, continued to fight the "good fight" and had busted out of the Crimea in June, sent the Reds fleeing backwards for hundreds of miles with mass tank attacks and the ideal flat land. However, Wrangel was unable to secure the town of Kakhovka. The town had little real tactical value except for the fact the Reds held it. In September, several battles had been fought for it, each time the Whites were repulsed. Oct. 14th would be the last one.

The Reds had created antitank formation consisting of MkV and FT-17 tanks and a unique American weapon, the Maklen 37mm AA gun. True, they only had a few of them , but the weapons ability to penetrate armor at very long distances was a stopper. Artillery units in AT mode also had been created. In manpower, the Reds had overwhelming strength if they did not flee from the tanks as they approached.

The stage was set for the massive tank assault (in RCW terms) and for the final show-down between White and Red.

Game scale is 1 hr turns, 800 yd a hex, company level, individual MkV, Whippet, Ft-17 tanks, Austin Armor Cars, DH9A Aircraft. The system never plays the same as once never knows how many units they can activate.

Inspired by ancient art, Tesserae combines elements of dominoes and jigsaw puzzles to form a game of shapes and patterns. Since it doesn't rely on a square grid, tiles will fit together in interesting and surprising ways. The basic game provides a good blend of strategy and luck, while advanced rules allow for either pure skill games, or ones where luck is more of a factor. Suitable for solitaire, one to five individual players, and several combinations of teams.

The game of Tesserae pits players against one another, meshing colored tiles into the ever-evolving play field, each trying to exhaust their supply of tiles while preventing their opponents from doing the same. Skillful strategy can pay off, but be careful -- things can change on the luck of a draw! This is a game that will stretch your brain.

Designed specifically for play by mail, this is an un-copyrighted multi-player game of snowball fighting.

Players write orders for simultaneous resolution using a simple action point system. Options include moving, collecting snow, several types of throws and a number of specific actions with the board features (conifers, snowmen and a shed).

The game is designed to cover land warfare in Europe 1618-1660.  The emphasis is on continental warfare rather than the English Civil War.  Scale in one inch = 40 yards for 15mm figures, one figure = 50 men and each turn represents 30-45 minutes.

The basic unit is a regiment or brigade sized unit depending on the national force being represented.  These are then organized into Division which are in turn organized into Wings.  Two or more wings is an army.  During play each unit receives a number of actions called Operations depending on a variety of situation factors.  These are then used to execute movement and other tactical operations.

The rules provide diagrams of historical formations, army lists, and scenarios for the battles of Lutzen (1632), Jankow (1645 -- erroneously listed as 1635 in the contents page) and Anfangsthal (1644).  A separate expansion, called for God, King, Pulk and Orta provides additional optional rules as well as additional army lists for Eastern Europe and the English Civil War.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Glow-in-the-Dark Concentration.

Screech uses a plastic owl that tells you the type of "insect" to find by showing the insect in its eyes.  Players search through cards and place the cards in the owl to determine if they have the right card.  (The owl has a flashlight inside the unit to flash the image on the cards to make them glow in the dark).

The Mosquito pieces can be added to a standard Hive set before the start of the game giving each player 12 pieces to play with.

During the game, they are placed in the same way as the other pieces. Once in play, the Mosquito takes on the movement characteristics of any creature it touches at the time, thus changing its characteristics throughout the game.

A Mosquito itself has no movement characteristics, so it gives no movement characteristics to an adjacent Mosquito. Hence a Mosquito cannot move if it touches only a Mosquito.

Exception: If moved as a Beetle on top of the Hive, a Mosquito continues to move as a Beetle until it climbs down from the Hive.

boardgame: Battleground: Crossbows & Catapults – Tower Attack Expansion Pack
description:
Tower Attack is the first major expansion for the Battleground: Crossbows & Catapults.

Expand your Knights vs Orc battles with 36 new pieces.

Publisher: Moose, Melbourne Australia

boardgame: Battleground: Crossbows & Catapults – Twin Attack Armory Pack: Knight Battering Ram vs Orc Triple Catapult
description: Expand your Knights vs Orc Battleground: Crossbows & Catapults battles with this three expansion for the Battleground: Crosbows & Catapults game.

Described in the book Beyond Competition, Space Explorer is a cooperative game where players take turns controlling a space ship going from planet to planet.  Distance and direction is determined by a deck of cards (1-6 in four suits).  The game is a race against time measured in deck reshuffles.

Do-To-Ho Table Hockey is completely different from other table hockey games. The play figures are not fastened to rods, but are mobile on the whole playing field. In their bases just like in the puck there is a ball bearing, so they slide like ice skate runners all over the board. Moving a play figure is done via a short, unique knock against the figure with a small stick as in the case for billiard. The play courses take place alternating, there are 'attack courses', with which the puck may be played by a play figure, and 'position courses' (defense courses as well as offside release courses), with which a play figure only may change position without touching the puck. Puck possession changes, if the aggressor misses the puck or hits an opposing play figure. Do-To-Ho was developed in Germany and published in 1994. Until now (2007) there are about 200 exemplars, all handmade. The antecessor of Do-To-Ho was Stahlkugelhockey (=Iron Ball Hockey), developed by the same author in 1980. For more informations and a Video please look at 
www.tablehockey.de

From the author: Groups is related to Lines of Action, with the differences being that pieces in Groups move only one space, only orthogonal connections count, there are no captures, and the game begins with a drop phase. The game also bears a resemblance to Teeko, with the movement of pieces to attain a configuration, although in Groups a winning configuration can take any form provided all pieces are orthogonally connected. I had been playing it [the game with no jump rule] and found that it eventually bogged down with defensive play. I wanted to speed it up some, so I added a rule that a piece was allowed to jump over another piece to an empty space. I thought this would open the game up more. What I had found is that the game grew immensely more dynamic, and much more offensive, and finished a lot faster. What would go on from 30 turns for a game usually now finished up in under 10. The play balance seems the same now, but the game is a lot faster.

Designer's Note: For individuals who would prefer a more strategic, and less tactical, game eliminate the jump move in the game (see the entry in the variants section).

Online Play


    Super Duper Games

Civilization CHR is an alternate gaming system for "Sid Meier's Civilization" from Eagle Games (2002).
Consider it as a group-up rewrite of the original game still using all of the original components adding new cards and pieces.

The game mechanics are similar to the original one but enhanced and revised to make it a whole new experience for a typical "Civilization" fan.

Civilization CHR goals are:
1) Add variety to game strategies
2) Reduce the "Risk effect"
3) Reduce Luck (introducing balancing rules)
4) Give a chance to catch up on to unlucky players
5) Implement some additional PC game rules
6) Increase realism and players interaction
7) General game speed up (slowed down by new rules) 
8) Better time management in slow phases or deadlocks
9) Having more "suspense" with destabilizing or unpredictable events
10) Increase simultaneous player goals

This boardgame cannot be purchased because it is free, so it has to be downloaded and "printed" from the publisher.  In addition to this, you must own the original "Sid Meier's Civilization" from Eagle Games.

Re-implements:

description:
Described in the book Beyond Competition, Resources is a cooperative game where players take turns collecting resources on the game board.  The object of the game is to keep the distribution of the four resources between the players as even as possible.

Movement on the board is done by jumping when possible  or moving to a spot when not.  Bonus are awarded when all the players have the same number of a particular resources.  Penalty points for a resource are given at the end of the game for each resource another player lags behind the leader.  If the resulting score is positive - everyone wins; if negative - everyone loses.

boardgame: Pinky Lee and the Runaway Frankfurters
description:
A frankfurter is placed on each one of the designated spaces along the track on the gameboard. In turn, each player rolls the die and advances one's own wagon along the track. The first to land on each frankfurter captures it and loads it on one's own wagon. Landing on an opponent forces the opponent to surrender all the owned frankfurters to the frankfurter hiding place at the finish line. The first to arrive at the finish line captures all the frankfurters that are there. The player with the most frankfurters after all have finished is the weiner (pun intended).

Pinky Lee was a Vaudeville comedian who achieved fame as the host of nationally televised children's show.

Players have 3 rolls to try and spell B-R-I-D-G-E using the 6 custom dice.  If a player spells bridge they get 6 points, bridg  scores 3 points and brid  scores 1 point.  All other combinations score nothing.  The first player to 15 points wins.

description:
Loosely based on Chuck Barris' short-lived 1967 game show hosted by Bob Barker, each parent and child begins the game with a pawn on Start. The father reads a question aloud to one of the children and both write down their response. If the answers match, the father scores a card worth 25 points and he advances his pawn the number of spaces indicated on the card. If the answers do not match, the child’s pawn advances one space. The game continues with all players changing roles as questioner and responders. The winner is the player who scores 250 points.

Three of the spaces of the gameboard are marked "toy store". A player gets a chance card whenever landing here. Should a player land on the space indicated by the card held, that player is declared the winner immediately.

The game can be played by one family with the participants playing as individuals, or as a contest between families.

description: Each player represents one of six species of Pacific salmon in their actual round trip from the spawning grounds to ocean, & back again to British Columbia.  Avoid extreme dangers, maintain health, or go extinct.  Rules for a basic and advanced game.

Struggle is a game about life. Each player/team starts with $650. Every time you pass Payday on the board you earn $1,500. You can purchase things like Auto and Life Insurance and 'You're The Boss' cards, make investments and pay bills.

Once players pass Payday three times they will draw a 'Last Will' card which will give them a cash bonus or make them pay a cash penalty. The winning player/team with the most money after counting their cash and investments is the winner.

Beat The Drum is a game for 'Salvationists' as stated on the front box. It is described as a fascinating trivia game for Ages 12 and up

Various questions in the categories of - Salvation Army, General Quiz, Old Testament, and New Testament are asked in levels of Easy, Hard and Master. By answering the Easy, Hard and Master, players advance their marker one, three or five steps respectively.

The winner is the one who reaches GLORY first.

This is a religious-themed trivia game for members of the Latter Day Saints (Mormon) church.  The trivia questions are religious in nature and are asked and answered in a mechanism simulating a simple baseball game. The key to the game is the question and answer booklet, which includes two pages of rules and 53 pages of questions. Each page has six numbered questions and answers. There is a cardboard field inset in the box and plastic pegs to use as runners.

For each "at bat", the batting player rolls a D6. For the first batter, the "pitcher" on the opposing team reads that numbered question from page 1. If the player answers correctly, he receives the result designated for that question. If he answers incorrectly, he is out. Pegs are placed on the field and advanced accordingly. One roll on each page provides a result without a question.

With each succeeding batter, the question moves to the next page of the booklet, allowing a long game before any possibility of a repeated question arises. As per normal baseball rules, three outs ends an inning and the team with most runs scored after nine innings wins. The game seems to expect high scores: there are holes in the scoreboxes for 24 runs by each team.

Many of the questions are very basic: "Who was the real father of Jesus?" (God). Others require more detailed knowledge of the Mormon church: "Name the brother of Joseph Smith whose name begins with the letter H who was one of the Eight Witnesses" (Hyrum), or "Which of the following is not an office in the Melchizedek Priesthood: Elder, Seventy, Teacher, or High Priest?" (Teacher).

description:
A simple game, played with 5 dice and similar in mechanics to Yahtzee, in which players get 3 chances to roll the lowest score possible.

At the beginning of each player's turn, they roll all 5 dice.  Then, they may set aside any number of those dice and roll the remaining dice a second time.  They may again set aside any number of dice and re-roll the remaining dice for a third time.  Dice you set aside previously may be re-rolled if you wish, and you may end your turn at any time if you are happy with your current roll.

Once the rolling is done, your score is the total of all dice, with a few special rules:

1. Fours are free, so you do not add those to the total.
2. If you have four dice showing the same number (except four), you subtract that many from the total.  Five dice of that number does not count.
3. If you roll a straight (all five dice in order sequentially), you subtract 4 from your total.

The game can be played with any number of people, and plays over five rounds, or however many you wish.  At the end of the five rounds, the person with the lowest total wins.

From the rules:  "There are 8 paintings in the game.  Each painting consists of 12 cards.

"Version 1: The aim of the game is to collect cards to make a full painting or paintings.

"Version 2: The aim of the game is to collect pieces to make a painting by memorizing their position on the playing area."

Paintings are by van Brussel, Constable, van Eyck, van Gogh, Ingres, Renoir, Seurat, and Uccello.

From the box:

"In POWARVASION(tm), two warring players battle to capture the other's government or three major cities.  Players alternate moving one of their armed forces pieces until the war is over.  These fighting forces move and capture in any direction up to their maximum range.  Strategy and skill are the keys to victory.  Players set hidden defensive traps, carry out hit and run raids and multiple captures, set up formidable geographical barriers, and launch nuclear attacks.  POWARVASION(tm) is easy to learn, fun to play, and a superior war game challenge!"

The board (with a sample setup, less the hidden traps and presumably cities) is shown on the back of the box.  9x9 square grid, Country Capital (white house), ICBM and Bomber (counters?) look like the King & Queen in chess, flanked by two sets of helicopter, artillery and jet pieces in the knight/bishop/rook configs.  3 tanks and 6 infantry round out the pawn row (tanks at ends and center).  From the board, it looks as if the next two rows you can choose where to set your 3 mountains; single river, lake and forest pieces, as well as two minefields and a missile site, along with between 6-9 "hidden" pieces (one side has 6, the other 9).

The side of the box has a picture of Col. James Jacobson, USAF ret., with a recommendation: "Powarvasion ...is an easy game to play, a thinking man's game of unmatched realism!"

Anyone Can Par is a golf simulation game.

The general rules of golf are followed.  Players tee-off by rolling up to 5 white dice.  The dice are added up and the total is multiplied by 10 to determine the yardage.  To simulate a hook or slice, the black die is rolled to determine which lane the ball lands in.  Shots from sand traps, putts and chip shots are done with special dice that have non-standard number distributions: 



    
         Brown 

         Sand Trap Shot 

          1:2:3:3:3:4 

    
    
         Red 

         Chip Shot

          1:2:2:3:3:4 

    
    
         Green 

         Putt

          1:2:2:2:3:4 

    


Alternately, these shots can be done by taking the appropriate card and following the instructions.  As the player's marker (ball) gets closer to the green, they will wish to roll fewer white dice to ensure they do not overshoot the hole.  Markers landing in hazards suffer penalties.  The course includes 9 holes with water hazards, sand traps, out of bounds areas, and trees.

Rubik's Brain Game is more or less a 2-dimensional Mastermind. As in that game, players take turns setting up a pattern which the other player must deduce, with the player making the deduction in the fewest moves winning the game.

In this game, a pattern is a 3x3 grid with each square being one of 6 colors, looking much like the side of a scrambled Rubik's Cube (the pattern is formed with 9 small cubes). On each turn, the guesser can either ask the setter if a given set of three colors are in a given row or column, or can ask if a given space is a specific color. In the former case the colors present and the colors not present are placed in different sections of the board for tracking purposes.

The object of Trivia Britannica is to get to London via the M25 motorway from wherever you started on the game board.

To reach London, you must have collected tokens from around the board. These are obtained by landing on token cities and answering a question from the category related to that city. On answering a question correctly, the active player can roll the dice and move again.

The standard game requires 3 tokens to get into London, the expert game requires 6.

Home Page: http://www.triviabritannica.com

boardgame: General Manager: The Baseball Business Game
description:
General Manager - The Baseball Business Game produced in 1967 by the H & S Game Co, of Columbia, S.C.

The box is plain green cardboard, with the title  in a yellow label sort of area that also touts  the game as "educational & realistic," "for 2  to 4 players" and notes "New players  may enter the game at any time."

The folded green playing board has a generally *Monopoly* sort of layout, with five franchises per side (named only by city) taking the place of *Monopoly* properties and the white "track" interrupted by "Breaks of the Game" squares,  which also occupy each corner of the "track."  Each of the four sides of the "track" has a  semicircular "siding" featuring smaller squares  for "trades" and minor-league purchases. There  are cards for "player development" and lots of phony cash. (More information to follow)

Game listing comes from a compilation of games created by:

Yahoo Group ---- Baseball Games Group;antique baseball games to modern tabletop sims

More description of the game will be forthcoming from information received from members of the group.

RoCo was developed to answer a challenge, "You can't produce a race game that doesn't rely on dice".  RoCo is a simple game for 2-4 players.  If you can count you can play RoCo.

But can you win?

Collectible Card Game where you aim to dispose of your planet's nuclear waste on another planet, while preventing aliens dumping their waste on yours.  Attacks are made by constructing an elaborate pattern of cards on the table and then announcing an attack based on the total attack scores of all played cards.

Published only in Australia and no longer available.

Wiggul your way through to capture or display!

Wiggul (also previously known as Squiggle) was conceived as an update for the game Kasam. It includes the escort capture style of Kasam but uses a hexagonal board.  An additional aspect of the game is that winning can be by position (getting 5 pieces in a line).

Xephyr is ideally designed for three players, but can be played with any number between 2 and 6.  Balance and co-operation are the ways to win in Xephyr.

Xephyr uses a dice; the latter is unusual for Fleur Designs games but it doesn't mean the element of skill is missing. The whole game is based on your skill of balance; can you get your first piece to its goal whilst keeping your second under control? If you can't then you can lose badly.
Diversion is a simple game in which your move dictates where your opponent can move.  Do you go for speed or sending your opponent in circles?

Qiddle is a redesign of The Royal Game of Ur, with a design goal of adding meaning to the spaces while altering the layout of the spaces.  It pits player against player against player against player. Two or four compete but there are limited safe spaces and your resources won't allow you to keep to safe havens.
Five is derived from an ancient Indian game like Ludo. Unlike all other games from this source, Five can be played without dice; then it turns into a game of pure strategy.

Guide is a game of tactics for two players and extends some of the gameplay of draughts (checkers).  It is played with draughts pieces on a Laita board (irregular 4x5 diamond).

Instead of taking pieces from the board, as in draughts, captured pieces are stacked beneath the attacking piece and can be rescued later when their attacker in turn is captured.

Gold Rush is a game of alertness and fast reactions. The idea is to collect 5 identical cards and then grab a gold nugget.

This game comes with a board and specific places to put the gold nuggets. There are 62 cards with seven for each of the eight minerals (Gold, Diamond, Emerald, Silver, Platinum, Ruby, Copper and Iron). There are also six cards for Fool's Gold.

Avoid reaching the 'Out' square (at the end of the board) by successfully giving answers that are within chosen categories.

Ex- If a category is 'State Capitals' each player must name a state capital in a clockwise rotation until a player cannot think of an answer or duplicates an answer already given.

The winner is the player closest to start when a player reaches the 'Out' square.

Odds and Evens is a gas rationing board game that was created in 1979 at the height of the gasoline crisis.

Game components include 'Odds' and 'Evens' cards which aid you in moving around the board and collecting 'Ration Coupons', 'Gas Cards' and 'Credit Cards'.

It's time to eat! Lindy the Lunch Lady is cooking up all sorts of fun in her kitchen … like anchovy milkshakes and mashed potato pizza! Her wild menu is sure to have you giggling as you match cards together to create four-course meals. Once you've collected a five-meal feast for Lindy's cafeteria, you win!

Lunch Munch is portable, easy to play, and features an electronic Lunch Lady announcer for a card game unlike any you've played before.

What's in the box?
Includes electronic Squawkbox and 88 illustrated Lunch Munch cards. Requires three AAA batteries (included).

Move the puck to your advantage by playing your hand of offensive and defensive players, goalies and star players". Contents include gameboard, instructions, a deck of cards and five "pucks".

boardgame: White Eagles: A Panzer Grenadier Supplement Book

There are 40 new scenarios depicting both sides on the attack, battles of cavalry against cavalry, and even a tank battle or two. Plus of course background articles on the armies and weapons involved. This module is not playable by itself, but requires ownership of Eastern Front and Road to Berlin to enjoy all of the scenarios. White Eagles includes 165 die-cut and mounted playing pieces. Almost all of them represent Polish forces: tanks, cavalry, armored cars, infantry, machine gun, mortar, anti-tank, artillery and of course leaders. The book's format is the same as our very popular Sinister Forces and Fronte Russo books.

On a player's turn, the player rolls 2d6, with doubles giving you extra turns.  When you land on a square you get options to change your position on the board, such as buying/selling oil wells or opening/closing oil pipelines.

The winner is the first player to deliver a specified number of oil barrels to the Refinery, usually 15 or 25.

The M25 is a light party-ish game revolving around the circular M25 Motorway.  On their turn, players pick up a card and do what it says - anything from a simple move to a full-blown charade, with drawings and lip reading possibilities.

By doing the action correctly, the player then gets to move further around the M25.  The aim of the game is to be the first to reach central London and find a parking space, which is done by rolling a 6 on a six-sided die.

Tzerar has proclaimed his domain be called Urthe, where he rules with a ruthless hand.  Wars have raged for centuries to try to regain the lands.  Technomancers and Mages have been wiped out in separate wars that only solidified Tzerar’s power.  Gems have lost almost all value and the few that live, just try to survive on what they find.  The great City of Elidor has been long forgotten, as Tzerar’s castle now rests in its place.  The world, that was known by all, would surely be lost if not for one thing … none of this happened yet.

Welcome to the land of Ruined Urthe.  The expansion, to the Djarhun fantasy board game, can send Heroes on a journey 1,000 years into the future of Djarhun.  Explore the land that will come to be … if only you do not get to the Book of Avrakar in time. description:
From the manufacturer:

Battle Animals is a game of strategy, much like chess, where each card has its own strengths and weaknesses.  Learning how and when to use each card is the key to the game.

Similar to Blokus, players lay down colored tiles on a field. Tiles must connect "via a straight line drawn from any of the corners of their last tile, but the line must be in continuous contact with tiles of the same color to be valid."

A collection of several different sports-themed games (e.g. baseball, basketball, football, tennis, golf, soccer, bowling) with simple wooden boards and pegs for score keeping and a pair of dice for each game.

Packaging is a round metal tin.

Most games require players to take simple actions that correspond to the different possibilities of dice roll from 2 to 12.
The players tries to land their MARBLIX as close to the center of the play area as possible while also attempting to knock away an opponent's well-placed MARBLIX, just like curling.

Batailles de l'Ancien Regime 1740-1763 (BAR) is, despite its French title, a set of miniatures rules in English designed to simulate European land warfare in the mid-18th century.  The players supply the figures at any desired scale.  The hallmark of these rules is the highly detailed and historically meticulous system for portraying the various types of military organization during this period.  Units consist of variable numbers of figures and constitute infantry battalions, cavalry regiment and artillery batteries.

Despite its deliberately "Old School" design (i.e., large-sized units, figure removal, historically accurate rules on movement and changes of facing), the rules use modern mechanics and so do not require the writing of orders.  Existing miniature armies need not be re-based to use this rules set.  The rules book is self-published but has been produced to a high standard of quality.

*  72 pages including the color cover
    * Quick Ref. Charts for large/small games, miniatures and/or tables.
    * If you know the 18th Century, you can play right away with just the Quick Reference Charts.
family: History: War of the Austrian Succession

description:
From the Website...

"Monster Quest is a fun and exciting game, with nine years of play testing by fantasy gamers. If you like dungeons and a dragon or two than you will love a game of Monster Quest. The game doesn't take days or weeks to prepare it's ready to go in minutes right out of the box. Monster Quest is a mythological board game that leads a player through a magical land of action and adventure. Monster Quest has 226 spaces on the board and 450 cards.  There are Spell cards, Wisdom cards, Map cards, Monster cards, and even Property cards that can be bought during the game.  The cards are one of the ways that a player develops their character.  Also, the cards give the player the ability to move about the land of Monster Quest rather than relying solely on the chance of the dice.

There are 12 different parts to the game-board, a main trail that connects all of the different parts of the game-board together, a Town Adventure, a Dungeon, six Inner trails, and three Outer trails.

Each player has a pegboard to keep track of their Hit Points (HP), Armor Class (AC), Platinum Pieces (PP), Gold Pieces (GP), Silver Pieces (SP), Jewels, and the Experience (Exp.) that a player will gain during the game.

Monster Quest uses two sets of dice, two six-sided and two eight-sided.  The dice are used to Battle Monsters, win treasure, and move about the land of Monster Quest.

A game of Monster Quest can last anywhere from three to five hours depending on the level of difficulty you wish to play.  Monster Quest can be played by two to four players.  There are six jewels in the game protected by monsters; a player must collect all six jewels to win the game.  Monster Quest is a whole world on one board where strategy is key.

Beyond the City Gate treasure awaits in dark Caves, along Wooded Trails through the Emerald Forest on Shipyard Docks across the Black Sea to thirst in the Desert wandering the Foothills to the Mountains of Doom on your way to the King?s Castle to save the Princess.  BEWARE of the Dungeon on your quest young squire and if you have what it takes the jewels can be yours."

Titeuf is a well known animated series comic character in France. In this game you help him and his gang passing a day in  school. You will have to master hilarious challenges.

Beside 220 cards and a dice there will be also a mini ball launcher and a basket to throw the ball in.

Produced in 1974 by the "Educational Products Inc. Division of Westinghouse Learning Corporation". The contribution of "Mark Coker, Productivity Management Consultant" to the development of the game is acknowledged.

"The Productivity Management Game presents real life simulations of situations and activities leading to the improvement of productivity in a variety of businesses and industries. Improved productivity contributes to profitability and lowered consumer costs.

Game Objective: to undertake three productivity improvement projects for an organization and to complete the projects on or ahead of schedule, with anticipated or greater savings for the organization." [preamble to the rules]

Extrapolated from a Games Gone By article. Game is based a lot on CANDYLAND. Board is 13 X 20 and features places like Lollypop Land, Candy Cane Lane and Gumdrop Islands. The game included 4 wooden pawns, a candycane spinner. Object of the game was to make your way through the maze and finish at the candy store. First one there won the game.

From the same company who made the famous Tru-Action Football game (The one with all of the men with plastic legs that when you turned on the power "ran" all over the place. This electric action game features 4 Sprinter racing figures. There are two different kinds of tracks on the field (board). One features the regular running events like the 100 yard dash, the 220, the 440 and the mile run. A separate track was used to run the low hurdles. The low hurdle track had bumps on it so that at least in theory the runners actually jumped over those low hurdles as they came down to the finish line.
Card game involving bidding and trick taking.  Card set includes four colors with numbers 1 through 15 plus a variety of special cards which invoke unusual rules.

A simple roll-and-move game wherein players travel through Switzerland, trying to be the first to complete the route.  There are a few additional random elements, including cash (which players must produce at various points along the path) as well as a few talent contests (such as singing the first verse of a song, and moving a number of spaces depending upon how well one has sung) or answering some trivia questions about the current town.
Players attempt to blow as many varmints into their opponent's goal as possible in this air powered game.
Players take turns rolling small wooden balls down a sloped gameboard.  The gameboard features holes of different point values.  Players try to get their balls into the various holes to score points.
Dice Baseball game using 3 D6 dice to determine the outcome of each pitch.  The game board included 10 wooden pegs for recording base runners, outs, balls and strikes.

boardgame: The Peculiar Game of My Wife and I
description:
From the National Museum of Play Online Collection:
"One of the most popular children's games of the late 19th century was called "Peter Coddles and his trip to New York," or some variation of that title. The games true origins have been lost but it is thought that it was first developed by McLoughlin Brothers in New York, though nearly every competitor produced one or more versions. Some, such as this version, bore completely different titles but followed the same method for play. It is a very simple word game best played with a large group of children, so it lent itself to social gatherings, parties, or holiday functions. The game consists of cards, usually small printed strips, with word groups printed, usually nouns. A leader reads the 'story' supplied with the game and when he/she encounters a blank, the first player to the left supplies the words from the card drawn. The play advances around the table, with nonsensical humor increasing with each blank in the story. Versions range from simple to deluxe--with quite complicated printed stories supplied. The game relates to other word games of its time such as Anagrams and to contemporary word games such as Boggle and Mad Libs."

boardgame: Mythes et Légendes: Le jeu des Univers Fantasy et SF
description:
Players in Mythes et Légendes  tell a story about a hero with a pre-determind happy ending. Each turn, players add a card to the storyline and add to the story.

If the story makes no sense, other players can vote against the story and the storyteller loses his turn.

When all conditions for the happy ending are met, the first player playing a card with a black marker can tell the ending and win the game.

All cards in the game use artwork used in novels published by Pocket.

There are a few special ¨magic¨ cards that can change the course of the story.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Drums of War Along the Mohawk
description:
These rules are designed to simulate land combat in North America during the French and Indian Wars, 1744-1766.  The rules are geared to lower intensity warfare from skirmish level to brigade-sized actions.  They may also be extended to the frontier warfare of the previous century as well as the era of the American War of Independence.

The style of the rules are "Old School" in the sense of relatively high figure density with considerable attention to actual unit organization.  Extensive notes and information are provided in the rules book on units such as Scottish Highlanders, American Rangers, Canadian Militia, and Indian Allies among others.  The rules also include such "chrome" elements as "How to Portray an Eighteenth Century Leader" (Chapter 4) as well as rules for colonial sieges.

The rules set concludes with the design for a campaign game set during the struggle for Lake George in New York state.  An area map is also provided.

boardgame: To the Banners:  Rules for Pike & Shot 1500-1700
description:
These rules are designed for fast play during an era of European land warfare across two centuries.  They strive for simplicity while providing a feeling for the nature of battle during this time.

The rules are tuned to enable simulation of smaller scale actions rather than the large-scale battles that are more commonly the focus of miniatures rules but were more infrequent than the types of actions (raids, ambushes, assaults and patrols) these rules are suitable for.

Given this scale of focus, the rules are designed for play with 25mm figures but may be easily re-scaled for 15mm.  Rather than offer army lists as such, these rules instead provide lists of characteristics of various unit types (e.g., gendarmes, landsknechts, various nationalities of shot and pike) by period:  Tudor Wars, Italian Wars, Thirty Years War, English Civil Wars, and Louis XIV.  Along with these lists, there is a system for generating armies based on unit cost.  A representative scenario is provided for each of the five periods of the lists.
family: History: War of the League of Cambrai

description:
Ridiculous Vanity is a miniatures rules set that simulates European politics and land warfare during the age of Louis XIV.  It is really two games in one.  The first is a campaign game where the players are national leaders engaging in politics and moving forces across a map of Europe divided into areas with differing economic and strategic value.  The second is a set of miniatures rules for simulating battle from 1660 to 1715.  The rules may be used exclusively of the campaign system and vice versa.

The rules do not require re-basing from other rules systems.  The units are standardized with infantry battalions consisting of 12 castings, two cavalry squadrons are 4 castings each.  The number of castings per stand varies with foot and cavalry types.  Armies are organized into commands and orders are issued to commands and groups.

The book includes several campaign scenarios set for different periods beginning in 1660, 1680, 1690 and 1701.  There is also one battle scenario for the Battle of Utrecht, 1676.
family: History: War of the Spanish Succession

description:
Killer Katanas II is the revised version of a set of miniatures rules designed to simulate warfare in Japan during the 16th and 17th centuries.  The designer has also been a consultant on several recent board games on samurai warfare and renaissance era Japan.

The rules support two different scales of battle.  In the small scale a base of figures represent 120-150 foot or 60-75 horse.  The larger scale enables simulation of the biggest battle of the era.  Each base represents 240-300 infantry or 120-150 cavalry.

The rules cover all the unit types of the era including Ronin and Warrior Monks.  Further, the author includes nearly two dozen classical Japanese army deployment patterns.  Besides the rules, the booklet contains extensive lists of leaders and their ratings as well as army lists, ten historical battle scenarios and a scenario generator.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Criminal Mouse is an Italian game (written in part by prisoners) about being in prison in Italy. Players are incarcerated rats living their daily lives. Although an educational game, it is not recommended for children under 14 years.

Winner of "Archimedes 2004" special mention for its high social value.
Players construct a vertical track using plastic pieces then marbles down the track.

[From the box description]

Collecting mushrooms is fun! The one who finds the most mushrooms is a real lucky gnome.

There are red, blue, and yellow mushrooms hidden under 33 panels of tufts of grass. The players hop from tuft to tuft with three colored goblins and uncover the panels again and again. The player who can remember the the most colors finds most mushrooms and wins.
Roll and move game where players round up rustlers.  Based on the CBS TV show "Johnny Ringo" staring Don Durant.
Roll and move rodeo game based on the ABC TV show "Stoney Burke" staring Jack Lord.

This game's theme is the Mormon emigration from Nauvoo to the Valley of the Great Salt Lake. The game includes a “pioneer odometer” spinner, game pieces (covered wagons and pioneers on foot), and 150 quiz cards.  Game play takes the pioneers on the Mormon trek through Iowa, Winter Quarters, and past obstacles like buffalo stampedes, Indians, skunks, cliffs etc. The Game of Emigration was locally produced in Utah with limited production and distribution.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

description: Know Your Gospel is a LDS (Mormon) trivia game for families or small church groups with questions focusing on scripture, church doctrine, and church history.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

description: This is a religious themed game where players answer trivia/knowledge questions about Jesus Christ and his teachings in order to advance on the board and earn the status of a "Disciple."  There are various levels of questions so that adults and children can play together
This is a religious (LDS/Mormon) trivia game using material from the Book of Mormon which was marketed for family or small church groups.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

description: Mormon Sports Trivia is very similar to the game Trivial Pursuit, except all questions deal with LDS history,  sports, and Mormon athletes. This game was published as a follow up to the success of LDS(Mormon)game "Jots and Tittles" and even uses a Jots and Tittles gameboard. There are six trivia categories: 1) Play on the Field, 2) Action on the Court, 3) Competition in the Arena, 4) Figures and Personalities, 5) Great Moments and Achievements, and 6) Miscellaneous.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

description:
Free, downloadable, educational board and card game designed for students, Grades 5 through 7, that teaches the fundamentals of paleontology -- digging in the field and researching about what you find.

From the website:

"You're a paleontologist, searching the world for pre-historic remnants and evidence of past life. As you move through the landscape you'll need to dig for discoveries (in the dig spaces) and research for information on what you dig up in your adventures (research spaces.) Your goal is to set out from the University in search of evidence of past life and return after you match 5 Dig Cards with their correct Research Card. Be the first to make it back to the University with your matches and you become the Paleontology Professor and game winner."

Game files (available as one pdf file and broken up into several pdf files) freely available on website.

Webpage also includes the game's design process and game variants.

Home Page: http://www.soozzone.com/paleotopia.htm
A simple roll and move game where players collect markers as the circle the board.  Interestly, the flip side of the gameboard features a stock car racing game.

A hardly known Tom Schoeps game. Each player tries controls a donkey and tries to get his oranges into town first (because the earlier you arrive the better the prices are...).
On your turn you can move forward which costs one food. Distance is 1-6 depending on the space you are starting from. Second possibility is to move one space backwards which gets you one food. Each player also gets a set of tactical cards that allow to manipulate the distance a bit. 
Games reminds a bit of Hare and Tortoise, but has got enough elements to be an 'independent' game.

boardgame: The Game of Terry and the Pirates
description: The Game of Terry and the Pirates was produced by the Whitman Co. in 1937. Special dice let Terry, Pat, Connie, Dale, Old Pops and the Bandits to try and move around the board in an attempt to collect the treasures. Playing pieces included 4 wooden markers for the characters and 4 wooden scoring markers. The playing board had different things that could happen to any of the players in the game. Players start in Shanghai and attempt to collect the treasure and leave the board.

Treasure Trek is a family trivia game aimed at members of the LDS (Mormon)faith.  As players roll and move around board, they encounter Book of Mormon events, doctrines, and sites.  Questions contained in each trek are from that particular area of the scriptures.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

boardgame: Tennis Shoes Among the Nephites: The Game
description: A roll of the die takes players into Book of Mormon lands.  Players answer gospel questions to collect Gold Plates.  Special questions for players ages eight and under.  Theme based on the popular series of books by Chris Heimerdinger.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

description:
Rails of Europe is an expansion for Railways of the World. Glenn Drover, one of the two designers of the original game, has designed a European setting for the expansion, which uses the original game's pieces, tiles, money, trains, shares and rules, along with a new map and card deck. There are some rules changed and added for the expansion to reflect the new setting of the expansion.

Due to the complicated corporate history of Eagle Games, this expansion was unusual - it was originally an official, yet unlicensed expansion of Railroad Tycoon. It was official, since the designer of the original game also designed this expansion and it was to be published by a company that is a descendant (of sorts) of Eagle Games.  The expansion is unlicensed because it never bore the Railroad Tycoon name, which was licensed from Sid Meier's computer game. Its status as an expansion of Railways of the World is much more official, although it existed before the base game of Railways of the World was published.

description: Geography based boardgame where the players buy and sell US States.
Board game simulating an ant farm as player use tweezers to move the small lifelike plastic ants throughout the underground colony.
A face building game where players use plastic pieces to create funny looking faces for the game characters.
Labeled "the hide and seek game" it's nothing more than a roll and move game.

Fast paced game in which players bet chips and then spin a wheel trying to fill up slots on a card.

Game includes a heavy-duty plastic bidding tray with an enclosed spinner. 4 cards for players to keep track of numbers called. And bidding chips.

Ages 8 and up.

With elements of games like Celebrity, Time's Up! and Taboo, Identity Crisis is a team-based game where you compete to guess famous identities based on the clues given by your team mates. In the first round you give verbal clues, in the second round you give one-word clues (to the same celebrities used in round one). You also have "Crisis" cards, which indicate that players from either team can guess on this turn. The game includes a timer to limit the clue givers to one minute.

From The Box

Players compete to identify the famous and the infamous, the real and the fictional, during two rounds of play.

In the Identity round, use as many words as you like to describe the identity. In the Crisis round, use only one word! Can you describe someone in one word? It's a challenge...but that's all part of the Crisis!

Engage in a little 'identity theft' and steal points from the other team.

boardgame: Santa Anna Rules: Warfare in Mexico – 1820-1870: Volume 1 – Tactical System
description:
These rules simulate land warfare in Mexico, 1820-1870.  The rules permit simulation of all the many conflict during this time including the Mexican-American War and the Maximillian War.

Units are infantry battalions, cavalry regiments and artillery batteries.  The rules give a playable game while providing a remarkable feel for the warfare of this time and place.  Unit morale is critical.  Casualties are indicated by figure loss but there are also losses to straggling.

The rules also provide two scenarios for the Mexican-American War (Buena Vista and Churubusco) as well as for the battle of Puebla in 1862 and for San Jacinto during the War for Texas Independence.
family: History: Latin American Wars of Independence

boardgame: General-at-Sea: Wargames Rules and Scenarios for Fleet Actions, 1640-1721
description:
General-at-Sea is a set of rules and scenarios for fleet actions, 1630-1721.  They were mainly designed for 1/2400 and 1/3000 models although 1/1200 may also be used.  The rules use a multi-ship system that permits large actions to be fought to conclusion in good time.

The rules also include scenarios for the Anglo-Dutch wars as well as for the later periods of the War of the League of Augsburg and the War of the Spanish Succession.

family: History: War of the Spanish Succession

boardgame: Captain-General: Wargames Rules and Army Guides for Land Battles, 1660-1721
description:
These rules simulate land warfare in Europe during the period 1660-1721.  One centimeter represents about 20 yards.  Units represent infantry battalions, cavalry regiments and artillery batteries.

Units are made up of stands with variable number of figures and units are also made up of variable numbers of stands depending on unit type.  Foot units are differentiated by tactical doctrine:  platoon firing, shock, rank firing, skirmish, dragoons, etc.  There are similar differences among the horse units.

The rules come complete with a very large set of army lists of various nations from the different period covered by the rules.

family: History: War of the Spanish Succession

description:
Might and Reason is designed to provide a fast-play set of rules that will simulate large-scale land warfare during the period of the War of the Austrian Succession and the Seven Years War.  They allow players to game the larger battles of the period to a conclusion in just a few hours time.

Units are made up of multiple stands of miniature figures that represent units of brigade size.  The rules are consistent with any basing system. There are also rules for pre-battle scouting and post-battle pursuit.

The rules set also includes two complete campaign systems:  an all-European grand campaign as well as a campaign tool kit for creating one-year campaigns within a given theater.

Home Page: http://www.sammustafa.com/mr.html
family: History: War of the Austrian Succession

boardgame: Undercover: The Game of Secret Agents
description: Spy themed game has players trying to find an escape route while avoiding enemy agents and hazards.  Players use special "Infra-scope" goggles to help find and use underground supporters as they try to escape.

A pencil and paper game in which the first player to form an EL(six different numbers in a line with no more than one right angle turn) wins.  A quick playing game that still contains a good deal of strategy.

boardgame: A Game About The United Nations
description: Players compete to fill seats on the U.N. security council.  Combines political theme with monopoly-like roll and move mechanics.

"The Game of Space Tag"  This is a nuclear/space themed version of Battleship.

From the rules .....

Blasto is a battle between two space fleets.  Regular Blasto is played by two players.  Three may play, however, if two game boxes are used.  See note under variations at the end.  The object of the game is for each player to locate and destroy his opponents' fleet.  The player who accomplishes this first is the winner.  Occasionally there will be a tie game.
Simple wild-west themed spin and move game.  Game includes 12x12 cardboard game board, spinner & 5 wooden discs for pawns.
Simple roll and move game that has the theme of Doc Holliday going after the "bandits."

A set collection game in which players' melds earn them money.  Cash can be used to draw more cards and to neutralize or steal opponents' melds.  Players may extend or protect their own melds.  Most money at the end wins.

The game is played with two or three ordinary decks of playing cards.

This game can be played in various formations. One game is played like Memory with players turning over tiles to find matching numbers. Another game has players rolling dice and selecting tiles that match the total and then filling up a player mat.

Translation of description from speldatabase.be
Six large boards each contain 10 drawings. Each time the sequence of numbers from 1 to 10 visually presented. Each card has its own theme. All 60 illustrations are also at much separate tickets.

The rulebook suggests many different ways to use this material.

As a warm-up, each player gets a playmat. The legkaartjes be dealt on the table and each player is trying to quickly collect ten cards.

Later, a lottery game played which also drawing upon the memory. The legkaartjes now lie face down in the middle. On his turn, each player now looking for tickets that fit with the player's mat.

The box also contains two regular dice. That you can use the right cards to collect. After looking dice each player a card with the same figure.

Each player who wins all ten cards can collect.

The illustrations are today but what about old fashioned charm because of their simple charm. Young children look really illustrations bearing the requisite number image is. The many game modes make this game more than once can be used.

Apparently a different game from the same-titled Erstes Rechnen.
Laita is a game of tactics played on an irregular 4x5 diamond board.  It can be played by two, three or four individual players, or by four players in a partnership game.

Four Square (4²) is a tactical game that can expand from one board to many, and where the playing area can wax and wane and twist and turn.

It is available as 2 or 4 boards, with the unique option of add-on boards.   A total of 6 different boards are available.

Chiaroscuro is an advanced version of Fleur Designs' game "t'other" for 2, 3 or 4 players. Most of Fleur's games have simple rules - the interest is in the tactics - and although Chiaroscuro's rules are, like the others, short they are more complex and need the ultimate in concentration.

Your task is simple if you've placed your pieces well.  But can you keep track of your opponents?

t'other is a simple strategy game and was designed with a modern Wars of the Roses in mind. It's not possible to explain without giving the full rules but those not from either side of the Pennines will be relieved to know that even southerners and foreigners will be capable of playing the game and no phrase book is required!

[From publisher:] The visitors of the Beach Stadium all look agog with expectation at Twiddle Turtle: Will she be the best again at rolling, skittling, and playing boule in the 2222 Olympics? A potentially record-breaking game collection with game board printed on both sides.

Can be combined with: Schildi Schildkröte

Spin, count and match! Children build counting skills for numbers 1-10 while learning about insects. Spin the numbered bug wheel.  The first player to cover three leaves with bugs in a row, wins!

Includes four laminated game cards with fun bug facts on the back, spinner and 36 markers. For 2-4 players.

Lunge, dodge and attack in a head-to-head battle between two mighty foes.

Tail controllers enable full control over the action
Built-in sensors keep score
Powerful roaring sound effects
Articulated dinosaur action
Realistic prehistoric landscape
Step back in time as dueling dinos roar, snarl and battle each other. Just grab the tail controller and challenge a friend to see which dinosaur rules.

"The classic game of Bingo has a new twist with Dora and all of her friends.  Kids and adults will enjoy this Bingo Adventure!"

Dora Bingo is classic bingo, but without the numbers and letters.  Instead, colors and pictures of the Dora characters identify each spot, and a spinner is used to call out the spaces.  Red Swiper, blue Boots, orange Dora, etc.  Simple enough for four-year-olds.  Game includes four cards and a spinner, and can be stretched to five players if one calls out the spots while the other four play.

Note that this game includes small plastic disks to cover the spaces, which could pose a choking hazard to very small children -- clearly marked on the package.

Ages 4 and up

boardgame: Sesame Street Preschool Games for Growing-Shapes
description:
From the box: "Each GAMES FOR GROWING game set contains a variety of game materials that are used in various combinations depending on the particular game played.
Games for Growing-Shapes has instructions for 8 possible games.
Game 1 Shape Pair Game, object is to collect the most pairs of shape cards before opponents.
Game 2 Remember-A-Shape Game, object is to collect most pairs of shapes by remembering where they are. 
Game 3 Spin-A-Shape Game. object is to get rid of your cards by matching the spinner.
Game 4 Fish for Shapes Game, object is to collect the most pairs of shape cards by going "fishing."
Game 5 Shape Rummy, object is to get rid of your cards in your hand before any other players by grouping shapes into sets. 
Game 6 The Funny Train Game, object is to complete a train of three of the same shapes or five different shapes.
Game 7 Sherlocks's Great Shape Mystery Game, object is to collect the most cards by matching mystery cards with cards in your hand.
Game 8 Mumford's Hiding Shapes Game, object is to Collect the most cards that make pictures by finding the hidden shapes in the picture.

World Bank: The Game casts players as governments of developing countries. The goal of the game is to acquire resources of various kinds, dealing with debt, foreign aid, multinational cartels and natural events in the process.

While the game involves rolling and moving around a circular board, there are a number of subtleties; players may trade amongst themselves, establish cartels for uncommon resources, and even engage in wars.
Krikos uses (circular) Byzantine Chess board, colours it as modern chess, uses modern chess pieces and moves and an additional set of new rules.

Hex Agon is an abstract strategy game that can be played for different ways, including versions that allow for solitaire play and for up to six players to play.  Mechanics involve racing of pieces, capturing of pieces, and placement strategy.
Simple roll and move game. Game came with a game board, 4 Blue Horse pegs, 4 Green Horse pegs, 4 Red Horse Pegs and 4 Yellow Horse pegs and a die for movement

boardgame: Show Business: The New Game from Emile Littler
description:
The Littlers - Emile, Prince & their sister Blanche were at one time the most powerful figures in the theatrical world of the West End & Provincial theater, with almost a total monopoly in producing musicals & pantomime in the UK.

This game was designed by Emile & was intended to give an interesting insight into the numerous hazards to the pursuit of stardom.

Players, starting from the Village Choir, would throw the dice & proceed along the track towards the School of Dramatic Art obeying the various directions on the way.

The correct number required must be thrown to finish on Stardom & the first player to reach here wins the game.

Amongst others Emile Littler was responsible for putting the young Radio & Variety star, Julie Andrews, into his pantomimes as the Princess in 'Aladdin' & this game is a delightfully produced & highly collectible period piece.

Renaissance Warfare was third in the SAGA series of rulebooks for period warfare.  It is a set of miniatures rules simulating land warfare in Europe and Asia from 1494 to 1690.  It may be used with any scale of figures.  There are extensive rules for organization but players may use figures based in the standard DBx format with little problem.

The rules contain rich detail on warfare in this period of many unit types while still providing a playable game that will see a result in a few hours.  There are 49 army lists that themselves provide a rich source of information and cover the important armies of Asia as well as those of Europe.  There are also lists for relatively minor powers such as two lists for Ireland and one for the Knights of St. John.
family: History: Classical Age of the Ottoman Empire
History: War of the League of Cambrai

description:
Cossack Wars is a set of miniatures rules designed to simulate small-scale warfare between cossacks of the 17th and 18th century.  It is a skirmish-type wargame with individual figures representing single cossacks.  They are given various personalized characteristics.

The rules are simple and perhaps somewhat reduced to produce quick play.  The rule book also contains 13 prepared scenarios of various sizes of which the largest would require 180 figures (360 figures to also represent dismounted cossacks) and the smallest 30 (60).

boardgame: Husaria: Wargames Rules and Army Lists for Eastern Europe from 1558 to 1699
description:
Husaria is a set of miniatures rules designed to simulate land warfare in Eastern Europe during the period 1558-1699.  Given the subject matter, there are many rule elements designed to convey the unique context of war in the East.

Each figure represents 60-100 men.  The units are portrayed by stands of figures and represent what in the West during this period would have been brigade-sized groupings.  There is a clever interactive system for players to build armies from detailed army lists for all the varied armies of this period:  Polish Commonwealth, Cossacks, Tatars, Ottomans, Muscovites, Lithuanian, Swedes, etc.

There is also a very large list of leaders with micro bios as well as a listing of important battles from the period.
family: History: Classical Age of the Ottoman Empire

The game is actually a collection of several games, each with its own deck of cards and instructions.  Most all of the games involve matching pictures on the cards with pictures on the board.  In one game, "Busy City" for instance, one person selects a card, and then pantomimes the image or activity on the card for other players to guess.

He describes not only the troop types, weapons and tactics of the period, but also shows how to begin amassing armies of wargame figures which are balanced in their composition, and how to use these same armies to win ancient battles on the tabletop using the Wargames Research Group Ancient Rules.

description:
Black Hole Explorer is an interesting children's game about building a rocket to travel to and study a black hole. The game is designed to be light-hearted, educational and fun.

The game is played in three steps:

First construct your ship using what funds you have available -- $10 million to $60 million. Your ship must have probes, radiation shielding, gravitational strength and power to drive your spaceship.

And, don't forget to name your spaceship!

Next, travel to the black hole and enter into a low enough orbit with it to launch your probes.

There are spaces around the black hole to draw Event cards that may help or hinder your mission.

Finally, escape the black hole's gravity (which is harder than getting there), return to Earth and study the data from your probes. Did you gather enough information to win the Nobel Prize? The first player back gets to roll for the chance first. But is he or she fails, then the next finisher gets the chance.

The game has competitive and collaborative playing options, and also information for playing the game with large groups.

Basic Cowboys and Indians game from Crunchy Frog, part of the 'Big Damn' series of games.  Includes cardboard cut-out figures, but can also be played with plastic Cowboy & Indian figures.  Simple combat mechanic results in a quick, bloody battle; Basic game doesn't take terrain into effect so movement is based on an included 'ruler' which determines how far you can move.  5 pages of rules(including the optional rules), plus 5 scenarios.

"Wargame Tactics" will be welcomed by enthusiasts old and new, by Generals good, bad and downright indifferent. For it demonstrates that victory on the wargames table can be achieved (and usually is) by the player who adheres to the tactics of the period in question and to the basic principles of the art of war. It therefore follows that the player who studies the military background of his particular period will not only derive more enjoyment from his hobby, he will also win more battles.

Free print-and-play game to teach children about the problems facing sea turtle survival.

Each player starts with 10 turtle tokens, and the game can end when any player increases his population to 50 tokens, or when after a set number of turns, the player who has the largest population. If a player runs out of turtle tokens, he is out of the game.

There are 4 variable conditions that help or hinder population growth. Each time a six is rolled on the dice, spin the spinner to see if these conditions (human interaction, climate, predators, and food availability) flip-flop from high to low or from low to high:

As players move around the board, cards may be drawn to collect or lose turtle tokens, change conditions, and gain tokens by answering trivia questions.

As players complete one life cycle and cross the start space, their turtle population increases based on the current population.

If players arrive at the same space at the same time, the turtles tussle producing more (baby) turtles for both players.

boardgame: The Oxford History of Board Games
description:
"The Oxford History of Board Games investigates the principles of board games throughout the ages and across the world, exploring the fascinating similarities and differences that give each its unique appeal, and drawing out the significance of game-playing as a central part of human experience - as vital to a culture as its music, dance, and tales."

"Beautifully illustrated, and with diagrams to show the finer points of the games, this is a fascinating and accessible guide to a richly rewarding subject"
