boardgame: Overlord: D-Day and the Beachhead Battles
description:
OVERLORD: D-Day and the Beachhead Battles is an expansion module for The Killing Ground: Campaign for Normandy, July-August 1944 that covers the period from June 6, to July 5, 1944. This 30 game-turn module links directly to The Killing Ground with an additional map, playing pieces and rules. It may also be played as a single map, stand alone game. In either case, the original The Killing Ground game is required to use this module.

OVERLORD: D-Day and the Beachhead Battles is divided into three stages, each with a different sequence of play. 
1. D-Day: June 6, 1944
2. OVERLORD: The Build-up
3. The Killing Ground

D-Day: June 6th, 1944 sequence of play is used only on game-turn one. OVERLORD: The Build-up sequence of play starts on turn 2 and ends the Game-turn in which both Allied nationalities have completed their depots. The Killing Ground: Campaign for Normandy, July-August 1944 sequence of play starts the turn after the depots are completed. The rules from the original Killing Ground game are used throughout the Build-up except were noted. The rules for D-Day and OVERLORD are in addition to and supersede the standard Killing Ground rules until the end of the Build-up. 
  
The OVERLORD expansion module includes the following:

     24 page OVERLORD Rules Booklet
     D-Day Turn 1 Sequence of Play Booklet
     2nd edition Killing Ground Rules Booklet
     North map section ; 22" x 34" (2 sided)
     280 back-printed, die cut counters (1/2 inch)
     Game Turn Record Track
     Sequence of Play Card (2 sided)
     Breakdown Battalion recombination guide
     German Set-up card
     D-Day and Build-up Charts and Tables card
     Combat Manual Pamphlet
     Guide to Replacement Counters
     Allied Tracks and Tables Single Map Supplement Card
     CRT Card


Most units are regiments; armor and other assets are battalions. At one day per turn and 2.5 km a hex.

description:
Spyhunting is a 2-player strategy game whereby one player acts as the hunter and one player is hunted a.k.a. the spy.

This game can be played with standard equipment (checkerboard) from the average gamer collection in addition to some non-standard dice.

The spy’s objective is to travel from one corner to the opposite corner and back without being “shot.”  The hunter’s objective is to “shoot” the spy before he or she accomplishes his or her objective.

This game was a Top 11 finalist in the 2002 Board Game Design Competition called "Simultaneous Action Design" awarded by About.Com.

Odds and Evens is a 2-player strategy game that can be played with components found in the average gamer collection.

The object of the game is to be the first player to move all of your pieces to the opponent's home row.

Players take turns rolling two six-sided dice and moving pawns on a standard checkerboard.  A unique rule to the game completely eliminates any advantage earned of one player rolling better dice combinations.

From the Rules:  THE LUCK O' THE IRISH is the game for Kings of Ireland, Leprechauns, Fairies, and the Little Folk.  It is played by means of pieces being moved around the playing board, Ireland, according to throw of the dice.

The players set off from Dublin taking any road they wish and on their journey around Ireland must collect a Pot of Gold, a Harp, a Shamrock, a Shillelagh, and an Emerald.  The first player to return to Dublin with all five items is the winner.

On their journey player will visit the historical cities of Ireland , cross the River Shannon, find their Pot of Gold at the end of the Rainbow, ride in the Jaunting Car and encounter that mischievous elf of Irish folk-lore the Leprechaun.

But beware there are pitfalls of which the most ferocious is the Banshee.

To win you will need "THE LUCK O' THE IRISH"

You arrive at Rattlesnake Valley where you build a saloon with your last dollars.
With Rattlesnake City you will discover an exalted life, reconstructed cities, big ranches, old gold mines and a run-down rail way.
You will loot the banks, winning (perhaps) a game of poker and, most of all, you will not save up your bullets.

Mr. Boneyhead is late for work.  He just lives down the road from his office.  It'd be all so easy except for the pesky pigeon who has taken such a dislike to him.

As the pigeon you must drive Mr. Boneyhead off the street's end.  Use any of the tricks that your cards may allow you or... well... use what nature gave you in the end.

As Mr. Boneyhead you must make it to work period.  Use your umbrella and whatever the street offers up to you in order to get there.  And if you're of a nasty frame of mind... pluck that pigeon's feathers for him.  Take enough from his tail and he'll leave you alone while the other birds laugh at his fashion faux pas.

Zombie Basketball...

Enjoy the horror, the humour and the blood and guts of a game played by brave young men and shambling undead zombies.

Play against a friend or by yourself.

Try the game and claim some fame.

Solitaire dice game in which you get to throw the dice exactly 30 times.  If you are efficient with your early tosses, you can use your saved throws to improve your score.
A two-player Rummy variant where certain discards force cards in the draw deck to be exposed.

Flash is a card game for two to five players.  Play is much like Rummy, but the emphasis is on sequences.  In fact, the player with the longest sequence at the end of the hand is the only player to score.  Melds of three and four of a kind are useful, however; they are immediately replaced by a like number of cards from either the stock or the discard pile.

Crossway is played with a Go set.  White's goal is to connect the left and right edges of the board with a contiguous sequence of stones, and Black's goal is to connect the top and bottom edges.  Unlike Go, you can connect diagonally, so there are eight directions of connectivity in Crossway.

You can’t crosscut, so if your opponent already has connected two stones diagonally adjacent, you’re not allowed to place a stone that would create two mutually crossing adjacent diagonals.

Draws and ties cannot occur in Crossway.

Palisade is played with a Go set.  Points available to Player 1 are interspersed with points available to Player 2 in a checkerboard pattern.  When you form a loop which surrounds enemy stones, you remove the enemy stones from the board.  Neither player may place stones into a loop.  A player owns the perimeter of his loop as well as all of the points contained within the loop.  When all of the board points, both occupied and unoccupied are owned by the players, the game is concluded and the player who owns the majority of points wins.

Rule sheet:
    http://www.marksteeregames.com/Palisade_rules.pdf

Online Play

From The Intro - What is a BackRound? A BackRound is a word pronounced backwards. For example: the BackRound for 'table' is 'leb-bait'. Some BackRounds are made up of actual words like 'eat-is' (which is the word 'city', pronounced backwards).

The first player to collect 5 cards (if team play you must collect 10 cards) wins the game.

From the Intro - The Winery Game is designed to be played by 2 to 6 players, each representing a different winery. The object is to generate the most profits before eight of the twelve markets are filled to capacity. This is done by buying grapes, bottling them into wine cases and selling the wine to the markets spaced around the board.

Word game which uses 40 octagonal tiles with letters on them.  Players draw and play a tile at a time to form words.  In addition to forming words by adding to the existing layout, one or more lines of letters can be removed and replaced to form a word as well, although each word may only be scored once throughout the game. Although ostensibly about battles between tanks, this game is really an abstract strategy game with some superficial similarities to checkers.  The game is played between two players who respectively move their tank pieces on an grid of squares in an effort to trap and destroy the other tanks.  While the game has some interesting rules for movement and capture, the components are rather low budget--small cardboard circles for the tanks and a fold out paper map.

This is a football (soccer) trivia game.  The game includes a plastic ball with electronics which operates as a timer and a noisemaker.  Teams of players compete to answer questions to be the first to score seven goals and win the match.  Red and yellow penalty cards can be given to players on the opposing team.

This is a roll and move game using the theme of students attending the very exclusive Eton College in England.  Players begin with four "colour cards" representing a different facet of life at Eton - artistic, sporting, social and academic. They must then work their way round a stylised map of the college, getting rid of all their cards to become the winner.  A variety of penalties can be imposed for bad behavior, including a "faux pas" for things such as hitting a teacher with a snowball, or allowing one's mother to turn up at school in an embarrassing hat.

The game was invented by an Eton housemaster and his assistant, both old boys of the school themselves.

description:
Sixteen is an original card game for two players, ages 8 and older. It is easy to learn (about 5 minutes) and takes about 15 minutes to play, though players will find themselves wanting to play again and again before they put the deck away.

Each player is dealt three cards. The remaining cards are placed face-down in the middle of the table as the draw pile. The cards are numbered 0-6. There are two of every card, but only one zero card for each suit. There are also two wild cards. Players take turns, picking a card, and then adding a card to a fanned-out, face-up play pile.

There are two ways to win a set: when the face-up cards total 16, or when the last three cards in the win pile are all the same color or number. So, you have to pay a lot of attention to what's in the pile, as well as what's in your hand, as well as make some educated guessing about what could very possibly be in you-know-who's hand. If the play pile goes over 16, a "bust" is declared and the other player gets the win. The player with the most sets when all cards have been played wins the round.

All of this results in some rather delicious strategic implications. The game can become quite competitive, but the elegance of the game, and the element of luck, keep the competition focused, and light-hearted.

The instructions describe four optional play variations, one of which extends the game play to younger children (perhaps as young as 6), but the game plays best with the recommended ages.

This is a soccer game using the team and players of Leeds United.  The game focuses on the placement of players on the board and uses a combination of die rolling and card drawing to establish player movement and goal scoring. In addition to winning matches, players also focus on accumulating the largest amount of money.

NRL (the National Rugby League of Australia) present Full On NRL. The object (as in proper rugby) is to score as many tries and conversions as possible, moving your thirteen players around the board using the Direction Dice. Each of the league's 16 teams are represented in the game as you play the Telstra Premiership or just for fun.

Players draw a card and list as many answers as possible in 60 seconds, receiving one point for every answer.  Categories are similar to those on Family Feud, such as, "Things you eat raw."  Cards offer three levels of difficulty.

Wings for the Baron is a game of designing and manufacturing World War I aeroplanes.  It uses cards to represent the various aeroplane technnologies and random events of the period.

The mechanics are simple: each turn, the players secretly choose two actions to perform: Build (to construct factories), Spy (to acquire other players' technologies, Design (to construct a new type of aeroplane), Research (to get more cards), or Bank (to protect assets from inflation).  Since selection is simultaneous and the actions are quick to resolve there is very little downtime.

After resolving actions, the players are awarded points based on the quality of their aeroplane design and their industrial capacity; then a War Status card is drawn to randomly affect the progress of the war. The game ends when the war does, so timing and efficiently using your actions are important factors in victory.

The game typically lasts about 30 minutes.

Wings for the Baron is available from the designer at the game's web site.  Compared to the playtest version, previously distributed for free, this desktop-published game has rules edited for clarity, ten pages of historical notes, more easily distinguished colors, and fully-illustrated cards.

boardgame: Under the Black Flag: Swashbuckling Action on the High Seas
description:
Under the Black Flag is the first supplement to the Gloire: Swashbuckling Adventure in the Age of Kings rules, and it covers swashbuckling pirate adventure in the Caribbean in the golden age of the buccaneers.

The book includes rules for creating campaigns of linked games, which will allow players to advance their model’s skills and abilities. Educated Guess - Drawing game. Divide everybody up into two teams (there are rules for 3 players). Players alternate as the DRAWER and try to get their team to guess what they are drawing.

The board is made up of 5 Greek Letter - Pi, Delta, Theta, Sigma and Omega. Depending on which Greek Letter your pawn is on determines the 'Type Of' clue to be drawn.

Players compete to provide the correct historical date of events, and then use number tiles to construct that date in a sort of crossword/scrabble type arrangement.  The game's components are cards with the history questions on them and cardboard tiles with numbers on them.

A children's game which uses the theme of a hoop toss at a carnival.  This boxed game includes two rows of cardboard stand-ups featuring toys and items of the period and four rubber rings.

boardgame: Charge! Or How to Play War Games
description:
Originally published in 1967 in Britain, Charge! is one of two foundation texts establishing the modern miniatures war game hobby.  Many of those who play miniatures began with this book and almost all existing rules systems can trace their lineage back to this book.

The book is a detailed building up of rules for playing a war game.  It contains detailed discussion of the rationale behind these rules, grounding them in military theory and history.  The book contains two sets of rules:  an elementary set for playing simple games and an advanced set of rules to provide a richer simulation.  It also contains one scenario, a mythical 18th century battle, which is played out step by step in the book.

These rules define the "Old School" of war gaming: individually based figures in large units with written orders to sub-commanders, etc.  Despite its age, there are groups of gamers who continue to base their encounters on the spirit of this book if not its strict letter.

description:
The game consists of a base with eight large plastic tower sections which are built up into a leaning tower.  Each tower segment has 16 columns molded on the side and these are aligned before the game starts.

The simplest game consists of rolling two dice to find the tower segment to be rotated and how many columns it is to be rotated, either clockwise or counter-clockwise.  The player who causes the tower to collapse loses the game.

In the more advanced version, players may choose which tower segment to rotate and by how much - between 1 and 3 columns - and the player scores 1 point for each column rotation.

In the final version of the game, players chose a tower element for the player on the left to use and all players are involved in constructing the tower before rotation begins.

(Note: The English language rules don't state that you cannot reverse the last move made.  This is compulsory to make the game playable.  For the simplest game, if the same tower segment is chosen, then it must be rotated in the same direction as the last move.  Alternately, you can use a House Rule that states that the towers can only be rotated clockwise.)

description: An innovative tactical rule set based on the 30 Years War.

Miniatures rules for the Colonial Period (late 19th Century).


Rampant Colonialism! was designed to recreate colonial engagements both real and fictional with miniature figures.  
While these rules were written with 15mm figures in mind, there are just as well used with 20mm and 25mm figures. 
The period covered by these rules includes the French and Indian Wars through the late 19th Century, and even beyond into the early 20th Century for such conflicts as the Boxer Rebellion, and the African and Middle Eastern theaters of World War I.

Rampant Colonialism can be played with two or more players, and in fact makes a great multi-player game.  
Each side is organized into a number of units, and optional command and control rules have been provided to maintain a chain of command for issuing and receiving orders.  
Individual models are also used, and include officers/leaders, NCO's, sharp shooters and heroic individuals.  
During a turn, a unit may move, fire and/or engage in melee with the enemy.  
All combat is resolved with the use of a ten-sided die.

The weapon chart covers everything from black powder muzzle-loaders through magazine-fed rifles using smokeless powder.  
There are also rules for cannon (both muzzle and breach loading) and machine guns.

The lists in the book include:

     The French & Indian Wars
     The Asante War, 1874
     Second Afghan War, 1879
     Zulu War, 1879
     First Boer War, 1881
     Sudan, 1885
     Boxer Rebellion, 1900
     WWI in the Middle East and Africa, 1914-1918


Best of all, the rules are simple, and they play quickly.

If playing Rampant Colonialism doesn't cut down on the "rules lawyering" syndrome in your gaming group, we'll eat our pith helmets!

–Description from publisher

Starship is a game of miniature battles fought between giant capital ships and their smaller escorts versus their enemies in the cold depths of space. Combat is intense and deadly, capturing the feel of starship combat in anime and science-fiction films.

The world of Starship! Is not one of hard-science, instead portraying the sci-fi we have come to know and love as portrayed on television and the movies. We have given the laws of physics and space a cursory nod, but eventually led them behind for the more enticing subjects of fun and playability. If you have any interest in the future and battling it out amongst the stars, then we know you will find Starship! An immensely enjoyable game.

description:
From the manufacturer:

We all have a friend like George, who is as cheap as the day is long. In this game, each of the players is having lunch with this "George," with the goal of contributing NOTHING to his lunch tab. Playing is simple:

Players, in turn, choose a question by value/difficulty. Answer the question right - transfer a portion of your contribution to another player (i.e., the value of the question). Answer the question wrong - another players transfers the value of the question to you. The first one to Nothing wins.

From the publisher:
Storm the walls of Rome, lay siege to Jerusalem!

Siege and Conquest is a rules expansion and source book for Warhammer Ancient Battles.

Throughout history, Sieges have decided the fates of nations. From the earliest Biblical times through the Roman period, the Crusades, the 100 Years War and into the age of gunpowder, peoples defended their walls from invaders looking for Conquest.

Cities have held out against invaders for years by stubborn resistance, others have fallen purely at the hand of a traitor or exchange of coin.

Featured within this book are the following:

SIEGE
A historical overview and full rules for siege warfare through the Biblical, Classical and Medieval Eras.

The siege section of this book allows you to recreate a full siege campaign using a series of 10 scenarios, or as many wargamers prefer, the option to just move to the final deciding assault!

By using the WAB points system, the besieger can create openings in the wall or undermine the fortification, whilst the besieged can attempt to shore up their beleaguered defences!

This will allow you to complete a siege game in a couple of hours.

CONQUEST
Scenarios and pitched battle variants as well as inspirational material for using terrain and weather in games, ideas for multiplayer games and developing your own campaigns of Siege and Conquest.

Also included are exciting new rules for fighting in settlements such as villages and cities.

These add a new level of tactical play on the tabletop in your games of Warhammer Ancient Battles.

A full colour section is included showing a range of fortifications across the ages, armies deployed for battle and explanations of the rules in action!

Besiege Constantinople, command Viking Raiders or defend the city of Troy.

When the enemy are at the gates and your walls are crumbling, will your army hold?

Expands

    Warhammer Ancient Battles

From the publisher:
The Age of Arthur – Warfare in the British Dark Ages 400 AD – 800 AD is a source book for Warhammer Ancient Battles.

This exciting period of British history saw the foundations of England, Scotland and Wales laid down by the warring Saxons, Romano-Britons, Welsh, Irish and Picts.

Arthur himself may have led British resistance against the Saxons, but this supplement also focuses on the other great warlords of the age – including Cadwallon of Gwynedd, Vortigern of the Britons, and Ecgfrith of Northumbria – and their warbands.

This source book contains a suggested historical overview and chronology of the period, as well as 19 detailed army lists featuring:

The Romano-British Civitates – including the armies of Vortigern, Ambrosius and the Bretons. 
The British and Welsh Kingdoms – including the kingdoms of Gododdin, Gwynedd, Strathclyde and Dumnonia. 
The Saxons – including Saxon raiders as well as the kingdoms of the Heptarchy. 
The Franks – the armies of the early Merovingians. 
The Picts – including the Northern and Southern Picts, and the enigmatic Attecotti. 
The Scots-Irish – including Irish raiders and the kingdoms of Dalriada and Ireland. 
There is also a section on wargaming Arthur in history for those who wish to use the great hero in their armies! Accompanying each of the army lists are suggestions for collecting, painting and wargaming with the various armies that fought in Dark Age Briton.

Other resources include a full colour section portraying the armies of the time and a wealth of illustrations and maps.

This book contains 15 exciting scenarios and two full campaigns – Mount Badon and the Raiding Season – to help you recreate the atmosphere of early medieval warfare – The Age of Arthur.

AGE OF ARTHUR ERRATA
Despite the best endeavours of many people, a production gremlin created a couple of minor typos which sneaked through into printing. They are both in the Saxon section.

1. Page 50 - Saxon army list. The Saxon Gedriht (hearthguard) should be limited to 0-1 unit, like most of the other comitatus units in the book.

2. Page 53 - the Army of Ecgfrith, King of Northumbria. As printed, poor King Ecgfrith has a Ld of 2 and appears to cost only 10 points. This mighty Saxon warrior was, of course, somewhat more charismatic than this, and should have an Ld of 9 (as per the Saxon Cyning profile on page 49) and cost 210 points!

Expands:

    Warhammer Ancient Battles
family: Theme: King Arthur / The Knights of the Round Table / Camelot

The theme of the game is the story of Three Little Pigs and the Big Bad Wolf; the game consists of four player tokens, the board and a die. The players draw lots over who is the Wolf.
The players roll the die and move their tokens according to the roll. The player who reaches the goal first is the winner. However, the Wolf must capture all three Pigs in order to win. In case he is ahead of all three Pigs, the Wolf, and he alone, may move backwards.
If the Wolf arrives at a circle where a Pig is resting, he has captured the Pig and that player must remain there. If another Pig arrives at this circle, the captured Pig is released. He may also gain his own freedom by rolling a six.
If a Pig arrives at a circle where the Wolf is resting, the latter must remain there for two turns.
In addition to these basic rules, there are several special event circles that have their own rules for the Pigs and the Wolf.

Here's a fast-moving fun game for people of all ages. It's a great party game because any number of people can play. Or you can challenge just one person to a duel with the dice. In the place of 1's on the dice are smiling pigs.

Everybody antes one chip into the pot. You roll the dice as many times as you want to earn 100 points, but rolling a piggy ends your turn and rolling two piggies takes away all the points accumulated up to that point in the game (at which point everyone else goes "oink oink" to show how greedy you were). The player then has to ante another chip into the pot.

Whoever gets to 100 points gets all the chips in the pot and is the winner for that round.

(Note that despite the involvement of Peter Marshall and the use of "Celebrity" in the title, Celebrity Piggy was never actually a game show.)

(This game was a one-of-a-kind gift for Nate (Cavedog_pdx on BGG) designed and assembled by his regular gaming group. He had no idea what 14 of his friends were up to!)

"Before bridge there was whist, a much simpler card game to learn but nonetheless a challenging game of strategy for four players.  Now there's Open Whist, a modern variation that two, three, or four players can enjoy."

Open Whist was originally designed so that two or three players could enjoy whist. In the two-player variant the players have two hands each. They are playing both the role of themselves and their "partner" in standard Whist. The main difference is that all of the cards are played face-up in a pattern that only allows certain cards to be played at any given point in time.

Many ways to play - the basic game for kids of all ages or the advanced game for a greater "cubic" challenge!"

The game uses custom dice and the board and box are laser-cut and laser-etched wood.

Master Nexus is a connection of words, be it lexicon, geography, history, or famous people. Each player spells out words by placing pegs in holes under the corresponding letters. Each player in turn tries to form a word of one or more letters starting with the last letter used by the previous player. The first player scoring 150 points wins the game. No player should take more than one minute to form a word.

The game consists of a peg-board with 324 lettered holes in seemingly random order, and 324 pegs. The words are not connected as in Scrabble; the players simply fill up available letters on the board, and they cannot create words with, say, T if all Ts have been used. (Not counting the final letter of the last player's word.)

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

Monopoly with Utah themed spaces and cards.

This is a board game published in 1996 to celebrate Delft's 750 years as a cultural city. It appears to be published/supported by a host of Dutch business, particularly those based in Delft. One of the businesses is Jumbo games. Several forewords from the various companies involved in the game are found in the rules book.

The board is a 12 X 12 square grid superimposed on a map of Delft. The object of the game is to place tiles on the board, with the coordinates determined by rolling two dice (with opportunities for two re-rolls). When a square of four tiles is on the board, it is considered completed and is turned over to show an 'object worth seeing'. Rolling doubles allows one to roll a special die, which could result in an extra turn, being allowed to place two tiles anywhere on the board, or missing the next turn.

The game ends when someone has completed five objects worth seeing. Scoring points is based on where the tiles in completed objects lie. The player with the most points wins.

description:
Over the years, there have been numerous attempts to develop rules for three dimensional (3D) chess using  three standard 8 by 8 chess boards (3x8x8), however, many of these games suffered from major problems that distracted the players from the essential characteristics of traditional chess. Some of these 3D chess variations created new chess pieces while other 3D rules imposed mandatory or unusual moves. In addition, many of these "rules" were generally incomplete causing inconsistent interpretations. None of these game variations offered a truly playable chess game.

Because of the lack of playable rules, the Millennium 3D Chess rules were written with the objective of extending the traditional chess game into a multilevel environment without distorting the basic game. To this end, Millennium 3D Chess has not "created" new chess rules, but instead extended the traditional rules to allow for multi-board play. Other than the concept of moving between chess boards (levels), all traditional two (2D) chess rules apply.

From the publisher:
Warfare in the Spanish Reconquista: 900-1250 AD

Rodrigo Diaz, better known as El Cid, is one of the most famous warriors of the Middle Ages. His deeds are legendary and his adventures in a world of Moors and Christians, castillos and caballeros, provide ideal territory for games of Warhammer Ancient Battles.

This source book contains an historical overview and detailed chronologies outlining the important events and battles of the time.

This book will give you 8 detailed lists featuring:


     Armies of Christian Spain - The early Christian Kingdoms, the Age of El Cid and the Reconquista. 
     Armies of Al-Andalus - The Caliphate of Cordoba, the Taifa Kings and the Later Taifas. 
     Armies of the African Invaders - The Almoravids and the Almohads. 
     Aliados y Mercenarios - Allies and Mercenaries available for hire to complement your armies. 


The age in which El Cid lived is one of the most colourful in military history and the armies are spectacular and varied and this is portrayed in a 16-page color section.

Accompanying each of the army lists are detailed notes on wargaming tactics, and collecting and painting the various armies that fought in Medieval Spain.

The book also contains two new scenarios for games of Warhammer Ancient Battles, as well as a campaign system allowing you to hire El Cid himself!

Expands:

From the publisher:
Alexander the Great is one of the most famous names in history, and he led his armies to the ends of the earth and carved out an empire. Upon his death, the empire he conquered split apart within a few years.

This source book contains a detailed historical overview of the period outlining the important events and battles of Alexander’s Age up until his death.

Featured within this book are detailed army lists for Alexander and his enemies including:


     Rise of Macedon - Army lists allowing you to recreate the Macedonian armies of Alexander and his father, Philip. 
     The End of Persia - Army lists allowing you to recreate the Persian army of Darius III and his Satrapal generals. 
     The Indian army - Army lists allowing you to recreate the Indian army of Porus as well as other Indian army variations. 
     Greek Mercenaries and Barbarian army lists from the period are also included, and these can fight either for, or against, Alexander. 


Accompanying each of the army lists are detailed notes on Warhammer Ancient Battles wargames tactics, and collecting & painting the armies of this defining period in history. Also included are a series of detailed Orders of Battle allowing you to follow in Philip’s and Alexander’s footsteps and recreate some of their famous encounters and campaigns:


     The Battle of Chaeronea 338 BC 
     The Battle of Granicus. 334 BC 
     The Battle of Gaugamela 331 BC 
     The Battle of Hydaspes. 326 BC 


Alexander the Great: Rise of Macedon 359 - 323 BC also contains a 16-page full-color section portraying the armies of the time and a wealth of illustrations and maps.

    Warhammer Ancient Battles

From the publisher:
In the West, Rome may have fallen, but in the East, Byzantium ensured the Roman values and spirit endured for another 1000 years.

This 128-page sourcebook contains an historical overview of the period and covers events from 527 to 1204 A.D. During this period we see the rise of importance of the cavalryman on the field of battle and this book reflects that development as Byzantium repeatedly struggled and triumphed.

Featured within this book are detailed army lists including:


     Early Byzantines 527-660 AD - including the armies of Justinian and Heraclius. 
     Germanic Kingdoms 442-800 AD - including the armies of the Ostrogoths and Lombards. 
     Sassanid Persians 226-649 AD - including armies for the early and later Sassanid period. 
     Steppe Nomads 558-1200 AD - including the armies of the Magyars and Penchenegs. 
     Thematic Byzantines 660- 1042 AD - including the armies of the Isaurian and Macedonian dynasties. 
     Bulgars and Slavs 643- 1018 AD - including the armies of the early and later Bulgars. 
     Early Caliphates 632- 1073 AD - including the armies of the Arab Conquest and Abbasids. 
     The Rus 750-1250 AD - including the armies of the Early Pagan and Great Rus. 
     Later Byzantines1042-1185 AD - including the armies of the Komnenian dynasty. 


Using these 9 lists and appendixes you can create over 35 different variations of armies from the Byzantine period. Also included are some Orders of Battle for some famous encounters from the period including:


     The Battle of Dara 530 AD. 
     The Battle of Yarmuk 636 AD. 
     The Battle of Dorostolon 971 AD. 
    The Battle of Manizkert 1071 AD. 


The book also contains a 16-page full-color section portraying the armies of the time and a wealth of illustrations and color maps.

From the publisher:
Hannibal Barca was the most famous general of his age. At the Start of the 2nd Punic War, against all odds, he marched his army over two mountain ranges and took on the Roman Republic; the mightiest empire of the ancient world. He won famous victory after famous victory and campaigned in his enemies homeland for 15 years. Not for half a millennia would Rome be challenged by an external threat of the same magnitude.

This source book contains a complete historical overview of the period taking in the three wars that Rome fought with the North African City state of Carthage (264 BC to 148 BC) with a description of the forces involved.

Featured with in this book are detailed army lists for all the key protagonists:


     Punic Army (Wars 1-3) 
     Republican Roman Army 
     Spanish 
     Numidians 
     Early Gauls 
     Ligurians 


Also included are maps and diagrams of key engagements such as Cannae, Lake Trasimene, and Zama the last battle of the 2nd Punic war.

The 112-page sourcebook also contains pages of color photographs depicting the armies of this period.

From the publisher:
The Art of War: The Chinese approach to warfare 2205 BCE – 280 CE is a source book for Warhammer Ancient Battles.

China has the longest continuous civilization in history and was finally unified by the First Emperor in 221 BCE. The Warring States period was over, and Qin victorious! Yet only 16 years later, the Qin Dynasty was in turmoil, the Emperor dead and all that would remain of his legacy would be the silent warriors of Xian- the Terracotta army.

This source book contains an historical overview that covers the principle military events and developments of Chinese culture from small bands of tribal warriors right up to the mighty Imperial armies that dominated Asia.

Featured within this book are detailed army lists including:


     Shang Dynasty 
     Zhou Dynasty 
     Warring States 
     Eastern Steppe Nomads 
     Han Dynasty 
     Red Eyebrow Rebels 
     Yellow Scarves Religious Cultists 
     Three Kingdoms 


Using these army lists and variants within them you can create over 34 different varieties of armies from early Chinese history!

Other resources included are scenarios, battles, a campaign system and Ruses for use in your games, as well as advice for modelling Chinese armies and terrain in full colour.

The Art of War also contains a full colour section portraying the armies of the time and a wealth of illustrations and maps.

description: This is an educational game that uses sets of cards to provide eight different card games for three learning levels aimed at teaching and reinforcing colors to children.

A boardgame version of "I Spy".  One player secretly selects a card which represents one of the characters pictured on the game board.

One by one the remaining players must try to guess the first player's secret character.  Players may only ask questions which may be answered "yes" or "no".  Each question or incorrect guess at the secret costs a player one of his ten chips.

The player who correctly guesses the secret identity collects the card from the first player.  The role of first player now passes to the next player to the left.

Once everyone has had a chance to be first player the game ends.  The game can also be played two or more rounds, depending on how long a game is desired.  The winner is the person who has the most cards.

boardgame: Magic Tent Game with Mystery Treasure Hunt
description: It's a story, a game, and an adventure! Hank, Stella and Cody were camping one night in their grandfather's mysterious tent. When they woke up the next morning, they were in another part of the world! Can you solve the mystery of the Magic Tent and bring them home? Young adventurers climb into the full-size, indoor play tent and begin reading the story. The story contains hints to finding clues that parents have hidden around the house. The mystery and clues are provided in the Mystery Treasure Hunt, Vol. 1 Alaska, which is included with this set. After reading each hint, the kids climb out of the tent and race to find the hidden clue. When they have collected all the clues, they'll fit them together to reveal a secret message. It's an active and imaginative game for groups or individuals. The high-quality tent makes a colorful, sturdy play structure. It stands nearly 5 feet tall and 4 feet across, with stay-open tabs and mesh ventilation panels. Made from sturdy, flame-retardant, 70 denier nylon. Comes with 48" x 58" Nylon Tent, 15 Tent Poles, Mystery Treasure Hunt and Illustrated Instructions. For 1 or More Players, Ages 7 and up.

boardgame: The Magic Tent Expansion Pack: Mystery Treasure Hunt Vol. 2 – Australia
description: Designed for use with The Magic Tent, this second volume brings a brand new story and an all-new adventure full of mystery and imagination. Hank, Stella, and Cody camped out one night in their grandfather's mysterious old tent. When they awoke the next morning, they were in another part of the world. It's up to you to solve the mystery and help bring them home again! This time the adventure takes place in Australia. As kids read the story inside their own Magic Tent (not included), parents will hide clues around the house. During key points in the story, the kids will read a hint, climb out of the tent, and race to find the hidden clues. When they do, they'll put them together to reveal a secret message that helps them to solve the mystery! It's an exciting activity for groups or individual kids. It combines reading, imagination, problem-solving, active play - and it's just plain fun! This kit comes with a 58 page adventure book, 7 secret clues, and a smart card with instructions and additional information for parents. For use with The Magic Tent - sold separately. 1 or More Players, Ages 7 and up.

Five scenarios are also included: Alma, Isandhlwana, Gravelotte-St. Privat, Khartoum and Little Big Horn.

The advanced version of Taliesin Starter Game, Taliesin Master Game is played on 3 circular boards, one on top of the other.  There are nine different types of player piece, in gold or silver, each with unique movement.  The object of the game is to capture the opponent's Life Force piece.

The core of the 3 boards is a "black hole" and this is the means of transferring pieces between boards.

Taliesin (Starter Game) is played on a round board with 19 pieces on each side and is very similar to chess, with soldiers moving one space, centurions moving like rooks, Taliesin moving in all directions and capturing the opponent's Life Force being the object of the game.  There are nine different types of piece, but several move in the same way as others; White Goddess and Shogun are essentially the same pieces, as are King Arthur, Henge and Centurion.

There is also Taliesin Master Game, which has 3 boards with additional rules.

description:
From the instructions:
Player #1 represents the investigators who have witnessed strange murders on the Miskatonic University Campus and now seek to find and destroy the cause of these murders before greater evil befalls Arkham. The evil is the spawn in human form, Wilbur Whately [sic]. To win, the investigator player must find and destroy him before or after he transforms into the spawn.
Player #2 represents the spawn and the cultists that worship and protect him. This player must eliminate all professor, police, reporter, and detective counters from play. At any point that he achieves this goal, player #2 wins the game. In the process of this, player #2 attempts to have Wilbur kill enough people to gain the energy he needs to transform into the spawn. Once Wilbur is the spawn, it will be much easier for him to attack and win.
Wilbur must transform into the spawn by the end of the last full moon phase on the turn track or his opportunity will pass until next year. Should he fail to transform into the spawn by the end of this phase, the game ends in a stalemate.

A tile laying game where you are building a maze and trying to move your pawn to an exit while trapping everyone else. There are several types of tiles (e.g., thugs who you can fight to take cards from your opponents; medics who can heal you; mole people, who help move you around to different parts of the maze; etc.) that make up the maze. There is also an action deck that can move you or your opponents around the maze, or reconfigure the maze. The first person out of the maze wins!

Another Electric game from Jim Prentice. This one is called JIM PRENTICE Electric Fire Fighters. I have little information about the game except that by pushing a trigger device you respond to the call for help fighting a fire.

This is a fairly basic spin and move game where players compete to be the first to complete an airplane race around the world.  The large square metal board is a world map with a racing course plotted on it.  The board has 2 spinners on it, which players use to control their airplane movement.  Various other spaces/map coordinates have other bonus and hazard effects on the race.

This game is very similar to "Operation," in that it is a physical dexterity game where the players play the role of a surgeon operating on a cartoon-like character.  In this version, players use a fork like tool to carefully remove various pieces of the patient without causing him to shake and wake up.

Franklin, Bear, Fox, and Snail are on their way home from school, and the teacher has given each an exciting challenge: Try to fill score cards with things they have found on the way home!

Players search for the treasure chips that match their score card. The winner is the player who is the first to find all the chips in his set. Versions are included for beginning and advanced players.  No reading required.

Franklin’s Treasure Hunt Game is intended to help players to develop skills of visual discrimination, sorting and classifying, as well as important social skills: taking turns, following rules, sharing, and respecting others.

Billed as the "Computerized Action Game," Quarterback used a combination of offensive/defensive cards selected by the players combined with a small mechanical rolling piece to determine the outcome of football plays.  Licensed by the NFL Players association, Quarterback invited players to play against each other as one of 16 NFL quarterbacks of the period.

Paartie - Plauderspiel für Partner is a cardgame for adults. Players use cards to try and gain gems. You win gems by knowing what your partner thinks, feels, wants. A game for relationships.

Ages 18 and up. Released: Essen 2007.

UNRELEASED GAME

    
    
         Note that all details of this game are preliminary. This game has not been released, and since its announced release date has long since passed, this game might never be released. Ave Caesar: Kurs 1 (Course 1) will include a double-sided board with two new tracks, six horsecarts, six coins and 144 cards. The art is by Eckhard Freytag and Ingrid Berner.

Expands:

    Ave Caesar

The Game of Football produced by Parker Brothers in 1880 features a picture of young boys playing a game that looks a bit like sandlot football. The game comes with a number of colored pieces that represent players and by jumping over players each team tries to score goals.

As a version of late-1800s football, this game would be completely different from the same-named Game of Football from 1948, as the rules of the physical game developed significantly in the intervening 70 years.

boardgame: Warhammer: Chariot Wars – A Supplement for Ancient Battles
description:
From the publisher:
Seven detailed lists cover the greatest empires of the ancient Middle East - 2500 BC to 500 BC: the age of chariot warfare.

Each list covers one of the following great empires and nations, including allies and opponents, historical background information, and descriptions of troop types:


     Sumerian & Akkadian. 
     New Kingdom Egyptian. 
     Hammurabic Babylonia Empire. 
     Hittite Empire. 
     Mycenaean Greek and Trojan Armies. 
     The Israelites. 
     Assyrian Empire (including Babylonians). 


Chariot Wars is also a source book of historical information for each army list, including detailed time lines of historical wars and campaigns which make use of controversial new research. There are also notes on tactics, painting, military organization, and color plates.

Expands:

    Warhammer Ancient Battles
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: History: Wars in the Ancient Near East

boardgame: Warhammer: Armies of Antiquity – A Supplement for Ancient Battles
description:
From the publisher:
Armies of Antiquity contains army lists for use with the Warhammer Ancient Battles rules. The lists cover all of the most famous armies of the ancient world, from the chariot armies of ancient Egypt through to armies of crusading knights.

New rules to use in your Warhammer Ancient Battles games. 
22 new army lists, including:
Sumerian, Egyptian, Hittite, Trojan Wars, Assyrian, Persian, Ancient Greek, Alexander & His Successors, Ancient Indian, Republican Roman, Carthaginian, Late Roman, Cataphract, Byzantine, The Nomad Hordes, The Saracens, Saxon, Viking, Norman, Crusader, Chinese, and Samurai.

Special tribal variations which can be used with the Barbarian army list included in Warhammer Ancient Battles.

Please note: Armies of Antiquity is now out of print, but many of the lists are available in the new edition (version 1.5) of the Warhammer Ancient Battles rulebook and more detailed lists in the many sourcebooks made available since this book's release.

Expands:

    Warhammer Ancient Battles

boardgame: Legends of the Old West: The Alamo – Victory or Death
description:
From the publisher:
This lavish book deals with one of the most infamous battles in the Old West when the Mexican army fought Texans at the Alamo mission in San Antonio. Inside you¹ll find new rules, army lists and scenarios so you can take your battles to a whole new scale, creating sweeping military actions to recreate the Texas War for Independence.

Relive the battle of the Alamo. Change history at San Jacinto. Hire David Crockett, James Bowie, or even Generalissimo Santa Anna to join your cause.

The fate of a nation rests in your hands.

Expands:

    Legends of the Old West


Expanded by:

    Legends of the Old West: Showdown which includes rules errata

The title translates as tattletale or motormouth.  A trivia party game for 2-8 people, it may have been first published February 1994 Spiel im Heft Spielzeit (Playtime Magazine).  See designer's website http://www.burkhardt-spiele.de/Quasselstrippe.htm for a copy to print and play (in German).  You will want to use die of different colors.  It looks as though you roll the dice and have to name a plant, a politician, whatever to move forward and the name has to begin with the letter on the trail from start to finish.
Ages 8 and up

This 200-page book is a "best of" White Dwarf.

From the publisher:
This book is a collection of the best of White Dwarf Warhammer 40,000 articles.  It provides you with a complete update on the forces and technologies of the Warhammer Universe.  The Warhammer 40,000 Compendium contains the complete army lists for: Space Marines, Imperial Guard, Squats, and Harlequins together with their histories and backgrounds.  In addition, it includes the new rules for Dreadnoughts, Robots, and other fighting machines together with all of the revised rules for vehicles.

Expands:

     Warhammer 40,000: Rogue Trader

Das Lieblingsspiel Von Adam Riese (the Favorite Game of Adam Riese) is a simple self-published wooden abstract arithmetic game for two that plays in 5 to 10 minutes, and that comes in a pouch.

Each player (light and dark) plays on the same off-set grid of three rows up and three rows down. Each row has a start value of ten and four spaces to fill, except one space marked (+), resulting in fourteen spaces in all to fill. Each player has 10 stones to place: four square value stones (4, 3, 2, and 1), three round arithmetic symbol stones (the minus sign, the division sign, and the multiplication sign), and 3 tall round scoring markers (two in their own colour and one in their opponent's). Players take turns to place one stone of their own choice from their own supply onto the appropriate spaces. After all stones are placed, the scores are determined. Each player scores all the rows that are marked by the tall scoring stones of their color (one of which is placed by the opponent), and the score for each row is the outcome of the simple formula that players have created with the stones. Whoever gets the most points overall is the winner.

Adam Riese, in the title, refers to the great German mathematician Adam Ries, better known as Adam Riese (1492-1559), whose work contributed to the phasing out of the impractical Roman numerals in favour of the Arabic numerals that we currently use, and who made it his life's ambition to bring the basics of mathematics and algebra to all people. For that reason, he wrote his science books in German, rather than in Latin, the language of the scientific elite, and was one of the first to do so. The German phrase "nach Adam Riese" (according to Adam Riese) is still used as an expression in the German language to emphasize the obvious correctness of a result of simple arithmetic.

"An enlightening and non-competitive game for Christians from different backgrounds to explore and share their beliefs" (From box cover).

Players move their pieces, answer questions and vote on Christian discussion topics to gain game segments.  Game continues until all have 6 segments; there are no winners or losers.

Published on behalf of the Churches in Great Britain and Ireland.

boardgame: Du bist der Größte, kleiner Rabe
description:
You are the Biggest, Little Raven! All the forest friends of little Raven race from all edges of the forest to little Raven's nest in the meadow.  Each team has three animals and little Raven is the judge. The first team member to reach the center gets a piece of the cake from little Raven in its nest.  Naturally he or she takes the largest piece.  And once the pieces are distributed, little Raven determines which crew won.
An exciting game for color learning.
Contents: game board, 12 play figures, 1 little Raven, 1 Raven nest, 8 pieces of cake of different sizes, 2 color cubes, and rules.
Ages 4 and up.
The winner becomes the Postmaster by returning to the Post Office after delivering all their mail.  Plan mail routes which can be altered by draw cards.
family: Theme: Mail / Stamps / The Post Office

description:
The Bomb is Ticking!: Your knowledge can save the city.
A self-published game created by Burkhardt and the senior class of Schubart-Realschule, Geislingen an der Steige, Germany.  It centers abound the city of Geislingen as threatened by a bomb.  Players need to know the answers to general and city-specific questions.  With each wrong answer a figure advances forward to detonate the bomb.  Conversely, if three questions are answered correctly, the players receive one of six puzzle pieces.  When completed, the pieces combine to make a pair of scissors that can cut the fuse in time and thus save all the players and their city.  This game may be the genesis of Burkhardt's DEUTSCHLAND - Finden Sie Minden (the map game for 3-5 players published by Kosmos).

A game as varied as one day at the beach.  The sun shines, the sunscreen disappears, and the boombox blares.  Appears to be a companion game to Freche Mädchen - Liebe, Jungs & Mädchenträume, also by Kosmos for the same gender and age group, based on the FRECHE MÄDCHEN FRECHE BÜCHER or IMPUDENT GIRLS IMPUDENT BOOKS.
Designer's site says it is for girls Ages 10 - 14.
http://www.burkhardt-spiele.de/Meine%20Ludographie.2.htm

The earliest recorded hunt game in Europe, Catch the Hare was mentioned in Libro de los Juegos (Book of Games) commissioned by Alfonso X in 1283.  It is played on an alquerque board, with one side taking the part of a hare and the other taking 12 hunters.  The hunters must trap the hare so that it cannot move; the hare must kill enough hunters to make their task impossible.
family: Traditional Games: Alquerque / Fanorona (Indian War Games)

description: Billed as "The True American Teen Game" and "The Game That All Girls Love to Play," this was a game marketed for preteen and teenage girls.  The game is a competition between players to achieve the highest level of "popularity."  The game had an interesting mechanism for determining whether a girl scored popularity: a small, pink, plastic phone—the Automatic Answer-Phone*

A children's memory game with a theme based on the children's picture book by Janelle Cannon. Stellaluna loves her baby bird friends, but hates those icky bugs they have to eat in Mother Bird's nest! She needs to find her own bat family again and feast on yummy mangoes. Help Stellaluna learn the skills she needs to be a bat again.

Players take turns turning over memory chips. If it is a helper chip, they place it the sky path and move Stellaluna one space forward. If it is not, they turn it back over again and leave it in place. When Stellaluna reaches home, all players win! There is a more advanced ruleset for older players.

This game is intended to teach young players to develop memory and story-telling skills as well as important social skills: taking turns, following rules, sharing and respecting others.

The zoo animals are jostling to get inside the holiday express. But not all the animals will fit! Already the first ones tumble off the wagon! Who with a bit of skill will get their animals inside the train? A pushing game for 2 to 4 skillful players.

boardgame: Die Wilden Hühner und die Liebe
description: Game tie-in to the German 2007 film of the same name based on the book by the writer of "Inkheart", Cornelia Funke.  The title translates as "Wild Chickens and Love."  The description at Amazon.de makes it sound like a romantic polygon of Sprotte, Melanie, Frieda, Wilma, Trude, and Maik. The players try to collect the correct pairs.  Contents: 5 collecting maps, 35 cards, 4 three wooden meeples, and 49 wild chicken markers.  For Ages 8 - 13.

Players take turns being either Finger Dinger Man or Super Slow Man.Depending on the card drawn, Finger Dinger Man is released down a ramp towards Super Slow Man. Buttons are pushed to fake out your opponent by both players. The winner gets a card. Object:To win the most Finger Dinger Man cards.

Not to be confused with the Lingo games which are played with pegs, cards or scrabble-like tiles.

The 25 letter dice are placed at random into the spaces of the grid. The 12 scoring pegs are placed in the holes in the corners.

Players then take turns to think of words beginning with the letters showing on the tops of the dice in the grid & each time a player names an acceptable word they remove the appropriate die from the grid & keep it (for scoring at the end of the game).

The winner is the player with the highest number of points !

boardgame: The Game of Categories, Answers and Fun
description: The object of the game is to fill in the spaces of the game sheet with answers that start with a designated letter and are associated with a specific category within the allotted time. This is very much like the game Scattergories that came a year before it.

A cardgame where players use collections of cards to attack and defend, themed around castle sieges.  The first player to successfully attack three times wins.  Designed for a 2002 competition to create a game based on siege attacks.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Age of Steam Expansion: Montréal Métro
description:
1962: The city of Montreal's population booms to over 1 million people, and the city stands on the verge of international recognition. By the conclusion of the next decade, Montreal will host both the World's Fair, and the Summer Olympics. In the present, Mayor Jean Drapeau recognizes the immediate need for mass transit, a subway system.

This Age of Steam expansion puts players in the role of government planners, building the Metro, and transporting passengers on the island of Montreal. Unique features present in this map include neutral track (Government Links) and a new split level Locomotive, to take advantage of that track. Montreal was designed with the explicit, and sole goal of supporting the 3 player scale, and has several changes to enhance the play for that number of players. It is intended for experienced Age of Steam players.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
The Transformers Card Game consists of four games using a 36-card deck containing 18 Autobots and 18 Decepticons. The following are the four games that can be played and a brief summaryof each:

1.)The Transformers Truth Game:  The object is to get rid of all your card by placing two, three or four cards on the table and declaring that they are all the same numbered card.  If any opponent thinks you are bluffing then  he declares this.  The player than shows the cards.  If the player lied than he takes all the cards.  However, if he was telling the truth then the opponent takes all the cards.

2.)Robot Battle: This game is played like the game "War" where you want to be the one with all the cards at the end.

3.)The Transformers Brainpower: This is a matching game where all cards are placed face down on the table and then two cards are flipped.  If they are the same, then the player takes the pair and goes again.  However, if the cards are different then the players turn is over. The player with the greatest number of pairs wins.

4.)The Transformers Shout: In this game, each player flips over cards one at a time.  When a card is duplicated, the first person to spot this and yell "transformers" gets all the cards on then table.  This continues until one player has no cards.  At that time, the player with the most cards is the winner.

How well do you know your US History?

In this game, one player reads from a list of numbered clues on the back of a card while others try to guess the correct answer. You score five points for guessing after one clue and it declines to one point if all five clues are read. The last clue is a picture of the place, event or person in question. Players continue taking turns until all cards have been read. Score is kept on included scorekeeper. The player with the most points at the end of the game wins.

How well can you identify wild animals?

In this game, one player reads from a list of numbered clues on the back of a card while others try to guess the correct answer. You score five points for guessing after one clue and it declines to one point if all five clues are read. The last clue is a picture of the animal in question. Players continue taking turns until all cards have been read. Score is kept on included scorekeeper. The player with the most points at the end of the game wins.

boardgame: Helmsman: The Game of Table Top Sailing
description: Helmsman The Game of Table Top Sailing Made in England by Minimodels LTD. The object of this game is for two or more players to complete the sailing course on the board by fair sailing and superior skill thereby beating your opponents.

Beat the Bookie published by Chad Valley/Barclay Toys from London in 1965. Game features a four foot long mechanical track, a game betting board and 5 metal "horses". Turning a crank on the game board moves the horses down the track and eventually  produces a winning horse.

This 1980 game from Real Life Games from Oklahoma City, Oklahoma is really just a football gambling game. You use real life football games and then bet on which team will win and cover the spread. You are playing with play money. The game tells you to add up your total wins and loses and then either laugh or cry. Game comes with a supply of play money and a weekly score sheet to mark down how you are doing.

A children's game (Ages 4 - 7) in which players move around the board collecting cards representing the cycle of nature (the seasons).  In the process they learn the days of the week and months of the year.  A dexterity element occurs when one player must drop the four-seasons card and another player must quickly catch it.

boardgame: Mickey Mantle: Roger Maris Official Home Run Derby Game
description: The baseball game is played by throwing Styrofoam balls at prongs contained within the target areas.  Object was to have balls stick to the board in the HOME RUN area. The game comes with 3 Styrofoam balls and a small instruction sheet.

Appears to be a companion card game to Freche Mädchen - Liebe, Jungs & Mädchenträume, also by Kosmos for the same gender and age group, based on the Sassy Girls, Sassy Books series.  http://www.frechemaedchen.de/
The “Song for Tom” is set in the world of showbiz and music competitions.  The players all have a crush on bitter-sweet Tom.  Each player tries to form "girl groups" to win the contest first with a foursome, then a trio, and finally as a duo of musician cards.  The first to complete a group receives a lucky stone as a reward. It takes three stones to win but heart-breaker Tom can appear at anytime to change the line-up.

boardgame: Official Mickey Mantle Baseball Action Game
description: From Key Man; The box depicts a great picture of Mickey Mantle ready to swing the bat. The plastic toy is Approximately 7" long. Batter swings, and hits ball off standing tee when plunger is pushed.

Win-A-Card Board Game was put out by Milton Bradley in 1969 and came with 1968 Topps Baseball cards. Football and Non Sports cards were also included. The cards came in clear cellophane packs.  The game box reads "Contains a generous supply.", probably between 50-70 baseball cards, and various amount of the other sports, and non sports cards. The 1968 Topps cards that were included with the game are of a different variation to the regular issued Topps cards. The backs of the cards are a bright yellow color as opposed to orange backs. Game included a spinner which was used to determine if trades of cards between players took place.

Children's game (Ages 4 - 7) in which players move their fantasy character (fairy, dwarf, giant or wizard) through the Charm Forest looking for treasures.  Each treasure is of a particular quantity (4 stars, 6 jewels, etc.).  The player must correctly reveal the right number treasure in order to advance towards the castle.  The first player to reach the castle wins.
Children's game (Ages 4 - 7) in which players must help the good knight Kunibert catch the evil Zahlenteufel ("number devil") by solving simple math problems.

Freche Mädchen - Süße Jungs & Abenteuer: another in the 
Freche Mädchen Freche Bücher or Sassy Girls, Sassy Books-related game series by Komos.  This one is story-telling teen party game about their first teacher or the first boy with whom they were in love. Afterwards the player investigates the cover stories of the others.

boardgame: Jim Prentice Electric Whiz Raceway Game
description: Jim Prentice Electric Whiz Raceway Game. Another Electric Game from Jim Prentice. This time the push button electronic connections determine which cars move on their trip around the track trying to win the race.

Children's game (Ages 6 - 9) in which players take turns using the catapult to shoot cardboard tokens ("rocks") into the castle.  If it lands in a section with a closed door, the player must correctly answer a "mystery" which tests their reading comprehension skills.  Answering correctly awards the player the treasure in that room.  The player with the most treasure wins.

"Syllable King Castle" is a children's game (Ages 5 - 8) in which players must speak one-word answers to simple questions in order to get past doors on the game board.  In addition to knowing the right answer, players must also indicate the syllables in the answer word by knocking on a door with a wooden stick.  The first player to reach the top of the castle wins.

This is from the "Little Raven" game series based on the children's book series by author Nele Moost and illustrator Annet Rudolph. Alles Meins! is a children's game (Ages 4 - 12) in which players must keep track of the locations of different animals as they move around the game board in order to win tokens.  It features "Small Raven": a popular character in German children's literature.

The little crow steals whatever toy is in front of him, but when he has taken all the toys from his friends, he realizes that this is not nice and gives all the toys back.  This is a move and collect game.

Officially an expansion that works with either Ticket to Ride or Ticket to Ride: Europe, Ticket to Ride: Switzerland requires the train pieces and train cards from the above mentioned original games to work. It comes with a new board, 46 new destination cards and a set of rules (in several languages) to create a new game. The Ticket to Ride: Switzerland map and rules first appeared as an exclusive map for the Ticket to Ride computer game. In addition to being specifically designed for a smaller numbers of players the map has several new features. Locomotive cards are used exclusively for tunnels (which work as they do in Ticket to Ride: Europe). Some destination cards have an end point inside Switzerland and other possible ends in adjacent countries (the value of the completed card is based on the farthest possible connection completed). There are also destination cards for connections between adjacent countries.

Although not officially sanctioned, it works fine with Ticket to Ride: Nordic Countries also. Due to Ticket to Ride: Märklin's different distribution of train cards, Ticket to Ride: Switzerland is not considered to be compatible.

Part of Ticket to Ride series.

Re-implemented / Re-printed in:

    Ticket to Ride Map Collection 2: India & Switzerland

Not to be confused with The Spiders Web from Otero Games.

Guide the spider around his web by tilting the board to control his scurrying movements. But beware ! If the spider passes over a hole he may stop in his tracks.

A tantalizing game teaching dexterity & co-ordination for any number of players from six years up !
Players spin a spinner to move their ship around a series of islands, racing to be the first to reach the home base.

The game box shows a long Zeppelin air ship named ZZ1 and has the subtitle "Air Game". The two part board shows three cities inside large circles, Berlin, Paris and New York from left to right. Connecting the cities are curved, color paths, demarcated into spaces by crossing lines. The zeppelin pieces are made of metal. There are also cards showing zeppelins. The game was made to tie-in with the then new Transatlantic dirigible service

Based on a German television series of the same name around the time. Players were travelers parachuting into various adventurous locales such as making an accuracy jump, saving another skydiver by bringing him a parachute in freefall, firefighting as smoke jumpers, a demo jump before air force officers, incidentially saving one of them from drowning and finally saving some lost children.

boardgame: A Game of Insights: For Men Only
description: This is a psychological-themed party game for men intended to have competitors explore their feelings, opinions, and awareness on variety of topics and personality areas.  The game uses themes drawing from both Eastern and Western traditions of philosophy, etc.  The game includes: 78 cards with illustrations, Playing board, Insights Into Action pad, and Guide to Keywords.

Seattleopoly is a Monopoly-style game which uses the theme of the city of Seattle, Washington (USA).  Spaces on the gameboard represent well-known attractions, locations, and entities in Seattle.  This game comes with: Playing Board, Surprise Cards, Treasure Cards, 2 Dice, Playing pieces, 25 Branch Offices, 15 Skyscrapers, Rules, and Money.  The game was produced in connection with Seattle area charities.

"Up and Down" was originally published around 1920 by J.W.Spear & Sons.  In 2006 Kosmos reprinted the game, including reproductions of the detailed, painted metal playing figures that were in the original game.

The game itself is a simple roll-and-move style game with squares on a circus background.

This is a trivia game with over 1000 questions divided into five categories: Humanity, Earth, History, Technology, and Nature.  Players move around the board trying to correctly answer questions so that they can earn chips from each area.  The chips are used to surpass obstacles along the way, and players can rearrange the obstacles on the game board.  The first player to reach the last square on the board wins.

There are several aspects of the game that make it family-friendly.  The first is that questions can be answered in multiple-choice style so that players can try to deduce the answer rather than having to know the fact directly.  The second is that there is a catch-up mechanism which boosts the player in last place so that they can compete more effectively.

From the back of the box:

"Imagine strolling down Christmas Tree Lane...  Prancing through Reindeer Pass...  Gliding down Bobsled Run...  Taking a jaunt across Jingle Bell Boulevard.  All this and more await you as you journey along the pathway to the North Pole!

Santa has sent his elves into town on last minute errands.  Each player must help his elf return to the North Pole, while enjoying holiday activities and collecting gift tokens in his own red gift sack."

This is a roll and move game where the object of the game is to collect the most gift tokens while moving to the end of the board.  You have Instructional Spaces (where you complete a task to get tokens), Pictured Spaces (where you get a token), Location Spaces (where you get tokens), and Blank Spaces (where nothing happens).  The person who gets the most gift tokens wins the game, and in case of a tie, the person who reached the last space first wins.

Galaxy Xpress is a fun to play abstract card game for age 8+. Player, as the galaxy tour guide, will fly a luxury cruiser and take his tour to three designated planets for sightseeing. If the time is allowed, he will be more than happy to take the tour to sightseeing the natural phenomenon that occurs only once in every one millennium. During the trip, player may take the shortcut through space portal but be most beware of the Xnergy waste and the evil pirate. At the end of the game, the player with the most victory point wins.

Ages 8 and up

Player: 2 to 6

Time: 30 minutes

Contents (98 cards):
12 Planets
4 Events: 1 phenomenon, 2 portals and 1 pirate.
66 Xnergy in 6 colors
6 Tour Schedules
6 Luxury Cruisers
3 Phenomenon Tour Receipts
1 Comet. Starting player card

Creating the Galaxy:
In the middle of the table, randomly place 12 planet cards in a circular clock formation; 1 planet at every hour position.  The circular formation is the ‘galaxy’.  Then place four event cards next to 12, 3, 6 and 9 o’clock planets; phenomenon next to 12, pirate next to 6, and two portals next to 3 and 9.  Event cards must be placed outside of the galaxy.

Goal of the Game:
When one player arrives home after a successful round trip, game continues until the round ends, and player with highest victory points wins.

From The Publisher:

Be the first captain in sail your ship trough the icebergs. A funny game for the whole family
family: History: The Sinking of the Titanic

boardgame: HeroCard Orc Wars: Sorceress Expansion Deck
description:
Sorceress Expansion Deck

As the Elven Nation reels under the Orc advance, a cry rings out for heroes.  From ancient libraries hidden in the trees comes a Sorceress and her mystical companion.  They pledge the full force of their arcane arts to the destruction of the Orc tide!

The Sorceress is a HeroCard expansion deck for HeroCard Orc Wars.  The Sorceress can compete in any HeroCard game or can be combined with other decks to create a customized character.

Contains 33 character cards and 1 figure

boardgame: HeroCard Orc Wars: Ranger Expansion Deck
description:
Ranger Expansion Deck

As destruction looms over the Elven Nation, a cry rings out for heroes to defend it.   From the depths of the verdant forest a Ranger emerges.  He pledges his twin blades and huntsman's prowess to fight the Orc scourge and carve a path to victory.

The Ranger is a HeroCard expansion deck for HeroCard Orc Wars.  The Ranger can compete in any HeroCard game or can be combined with other decks to create a customized character.

Contains 33 character cards and 1 figure

Kosmopoliya is a Russian board game about space exploration, economy and warfare. The goal of the game is to gain a cosmic monopoly and be the last player on the board.

The game board shows a circle of planets. It is players task to start the economical development on the planets by establishing space ports, mines and factories. Players can do investments on several planets. Players also need to secure their economy by investing into space ships that can protect their convoys. Thus the game is about the balance of maintaining and moving of the their space ship fleet and at the same time developing the economy of planets to get more money. All attacks on planets are solved by throwing dice.

Zvezda game number for Kosmopoliya is 8608.

Mykerinos: The Nile

This Mykerinos add-on was made available at Essen 2007. It includes 6 new tiles and 4 tokens. The players will sail on the Nile and meet a new character, governor Greenwish.

Now also available in Ystari Box (Rio Grande Games edition only)
family: Theme: Fine Art and Art Museums

description:
Card game based on the popular SCI-FI show Farscape. The object of the game is to have the highest score by the time the series (game) ends. There are Character cards, event cards, character exit cards and play change cards.

Joolushko Tunay Hovalis - Interion Student         
Utu-Noranti Pralatong - TraskanHerbologist           
Stark - Banik Strykera               
Jothee - Luxan Hybrid                  
Moya - Leviathan
Talyn - Leviathan Hybrid              
Lieutenant Braca - PeaceKeeper Officer         
Commandant Mele-On Grayza - PeaceKeeper Officer

Events are drawn from the event deck and have differing effects. When a characters Physical Health or Mental Health has been reduced to zero that character is out of the game. Their are also events that change rules of the game. (reverse direction, reduce hand size, play all cards on a turn not just one, hand size is increased from 5 to 7 and eventually the end of season card which ends the game.)

From Games of Art, Miro is a game based on the artwork of Joan Miró.

The game board consists of a single curved line that loops over itself many times.  Players color segments of this line to color in. When an area is enclosed, the player who acted scores points based on the number of enclosing segments he or she owns.

From the publisher: "Angelina can’t wait to be a ballerina and dance in the big performance! But everything takes time and practice. Before she dances on center stage, she needs to learn her dance steps and collect all the necessary items to be a prima Ballerina!"

While learning and performing steps along the way, players try to collect all the items necessary to take ballet classes for the big performance at the Royal Theater. The game features a dual number/color spinner so children who can't count yet can still play.

Monopoly type game designed for Alumni, students and friends of Penn State University, which uses locations, traditions, and features of the university and its athletic programs in place of the traditional Monopoly spaces and cards.
publishers: Late for the Sky Production Company

description: A Pittsburgh Steelers Collector's edition of Monopoly, Steeler fans can buy, sell,and trade the blackand gold's greatest players and team assets, including 4 coveted Super Bowls. 6 Collectible Pewter Tokens: Quarterback, Linebacker, football, cheerleader, and a Steeler's helmet and Whistle.

From the Publishers' website
"Scourge! is the first game in our line of Make-Your-Own games. When you receive your book cut it out and assemble it yourself and your ready to play. In the game af Scourge! you take on the role of a cult member bent on taking control after the mysterious death of your leader. Build an army of street thugs and demons and amass a library of spells to defeat your enemies while battling in the crowded alleys of a Victorian Metropolis."
