description:
Board game that lets you play the market for big profit.  Build you're investments cautiously or trade you're way to quick gains.  Watch you're cash levels, you don't want to go bankrupt!

Be prepared to face some serious ups and downs on you're way to prosperity.  The most sensible strategy may win but so might the craziest.

Each player controls a car that's a car. Players try to guide their cars on the track using the radio station 'Radio Route' that transmits information about weather and track conditions using three different wavelengths. Then there still is the police helicopter that could stop one of the player's cars. The goal of the game is to reach the finish by using the best   Radio Guidage.

Game of Golf that uses a "golf bag": a confined plastic shooter with two spaces: one for Driver and putter and the second one for fairway shots. The game is played on a giant mapboard that illustrate an 18 holes course complete with hazards and greens all divided onto spaces and dots. You use the shooter to try and obtain the result of a shot you want and you move your golf ball around the course.
Action dexterity game where the players shoot balls towards a goal "ala Gnip Gnop" but attempt to get four in a row of their color either vertically, horizontally or diagonally "ala connect four".
Player's spin a spinner and move along a track.  Along the way they collect "Hoedown Coupons".  Player who collect the most coupons at the end wins.
A children's game where the players spin a spinner and be the first to collect the gold off of their player card.  Ages 4 - 8 listed on the box.

StoryTellers is a storytelling game for 3 to 5 players, Ages 10 and up

Setup of the game is very quick. Every player gets a Hat Card, 5 (random) Craft Cards, and 3 Bravo Chips. The Heckle Cards are placed in the middle of the playing area, and the StoryTimer is handed to the player who is going first.

The game starts with the first player reading aloud one of the 53 included storylines. There are three phases to play:

Phase I: StoryTelling
The player holding the StoryTimer creates (speaking out loud) the next part of the story (typically a sentence or two). Play moves around the table, with each player creating and speaking a new addition to the story. During this time, if a StoryTeller uses one of the elements on your Craft cards, you place it next to your Hat Card. Bravo Chips can also be tossed to StoryTellers who do a particularly good job.

Phase II: Discussion
Anyone who played a Craft Card points it out to the others. If no one object, it goes on to that player's Hat Card and will be a point at the end of the game.

Phase III: Recitation
The player holding the StoryTimer gives a Recitation of the story so far. Other players can toss Bravo Chips for a Recitation well done. The StoryTimer is passed to the left, everyone draws back up to 5 Craft Cards, and we go back to Phase I.

At the end of the game, players vote to assign awards (most creative, most hilarious, most surprising, most dramatic). These go into the recipients' hats.

The winner is the player with the most points in his or her hat.

A couple notes about the cards are in order. Craft Cards contain elements of the StoryTellers Craft, which means that players score points by either using or recognizing good storytelling. There are also Heckle Cards which are common to everyone and can be tossed to stop things from getting out of hand.

Home Page: http://www.liveoakgames.com/storytellers

Civil War Battles is a miniatures system in a book of 136 pages of which 46 pages are battle rules. It contains 12 historical scenarios and a scenario generator.

CWB requires a lot of dice rolling and games last about 7 turns.

Economic-financial game, from the famous serial TV.
The players must fulfill an economic objective in order to win by moving on two boards (City of Dallas map and a map of world).

boardgame: Games to Play: Board and Table Games for All the Family
description:
From the back cover

A fascinating collection of over 130 of the world's most entertaining board and table games, both ancient and modern. Twenty games are beautifully reproduced in their original form complete with instructions, tactics and rules for all the family to play actually on the pages of the book.

Games of position and race, war games and word games, chess and dominoes, mancala and fortune-telling games -- all are included in this superb compendium. Wei-Chi from China, Seega from Egypt, Burmese Chess, Conglak from Indonesia, Hnefatafl from Scandinavia are among the less familiar but games of Ludo, Bagatelle and Blackjack are here too.

More than the games themselves, a wealth of historical information and the opportunity to discover an extraordinary variety of age-old games make this book invaluable to the serious collector of games as well as the games enthusiast.

ISBN-13: 9780718129934
ISBN-10: 0718129938

This is a collector's item of Marvel Comics with 6 games, an electronic dice and colored fiches. Press the button and the numbers will light up.
Board: 60x40 cm

The book has 6 games:

- Spiderman
- De X-mannen
- Wolverine
- Fantastic Four
- De Vergelders
- The Incredible Hulk

Number fun for preschool children.

My First Picture Dominoes helps preschool children learn to count, recognize numbers, and practice hand-eye co-ordination.


     An exciting game for 1-4 players, Ages 3 - 5.
     Reinforces observation and  matching skills as included in the Early Years' Curriculum.
     Developed in association with early learning consultants.


Contents:


     28 sturdy domino cards featuring numerals and photographic images
     Easy-to-follow instruction sheet with helpful note to parents and teachers

From the introduction to the rules:
"Policeman Paul has a lot of work to do today as quite a number of crooks are hanging around town. Studying the "personal description" given by the dice in the tumbler, you can help Paul track down the crooks in a thrilling chase. Who will be the best assistant policeman and the quickest at catching the right crook?"

This is a game in Haba's range of tumble-dice games, which include dice inside a sealed tumbler with a clear plastic dome. This game has 3 six-sided dice, two of which are essentially D3s, with 3 different faces repeated once. There is a die with dots (1, 2 or 3 dots), a colour die (yellow for the beach, green for the park and blue for the town) and a crook die (Policeman Paul, a chubby crook, a woman crook [on 2 faces] or a crook with a loot bag[on 2 faces]). Besides the dice and the rules, there's a large wooden piece representing Policeman Paul and a deck of 27 cards, showing all possible combinations with crooks of the three dice.

The 27 cards are placed face up on the table around the Policeman Paul piece. A player shakes the tumbler. Depending on what the dice in the tumbler show, players try to claim the corresponding cards on the table. If the crook die shows the Policeman Paul face, the other dice are ignored and players try to grab the Policeman Paul piece - the successful player gets to choose any card on the table and place it in front of himself. If the crook die shows a crook, the players try to cover with their hand the card showing the corresponding number (on the die with dots) of that kind of crook in the venue specified (by the colour die). Players can only use one hand and the player who successfully covers the correct card with his hand gets to place the card in front of him. Players who wrongly grab Policeman Paul or cover the wrong card can make no further attempt to claim the right card that turn. Cards in front of players can be captured by other players if the same combination of dice get rolled again.

The first player to claim five crook cards wins.

As a game, Talecraft aims to promote creative thinking through story creation. As a story-creation tool, Talecraft aims to help writers generate ideas for stories, and help combat Mental Block.

Talecraft provides the different Genres and Archetypes as cards. Since the setting is dependent on the genre of the story, Talecraft leaves the creation of the time and place to the imagination of the player. For the plot, Talecraft uses the Key cards, where a player may either be given plots, or keywords that would govern the story. Players may be required to add more characters to his cards. In this event, the player’s story would be more character-driven than plot-driven.

A player of Talecraft has to create a story using the cards he had picked. The limit the picked cards set to a player prompts the use of the player’s resourcefulness; and the need to fit all the cards into a story stretches his imagination.

It is not a requirement to appoint a winner when playing Talecraft. Since the aim of the game is to make stories, and the basis of a good story often depends on the judge's personal taste, Talecraft is often played without declaring winners. Should a winner be required, however, here is how it is done:

Each player will have a cup before him. After the player tells his story, all the other players will drop coins in the storyteller’s cup, depending on its impact on them. If a player thinks the storyteller’s story was not well made (like if there are a lot of loose ends, or if the events of the story are too scattered), the player does not drop any coin into the storyteller’s cup. If the player thinks the story is acceptable but has little impact on him, he drops a coin in the cup. If the story manages to amuse the player, he drops two coins. And if the story awes him, he drops three coins. At the end of the game, or rounds of game, the players tally the scores. The player with the most coins wins.

Another way to decide a winner for Talecraft is to appoint a non-player as a judge who would decide who the winner is to be.

Despite its English name People Game is a German Word Game for at least 2 players or teams.

The cards show each uncommon or unusual German words with their explanation or definition. A sand timer of about 3 mins running time allows the players to do the following:
They read one term and the definition and are allowed to write two definitions of their own. When the time is up then they offer between 3 and 5 points to their adversaries. They read those 3 definitions and have to guess which one is correct.
If they get it right it's them who advance on the track otherwise it's the active players.
This continues until one team reaches the end of the track.

The game consists of the board, 420 cards, 8 standard pawns, 1 pad, 4 pencils, the sand timer and of course the rules.

From the Manufacturer:

The fast-paced, high-stakes dice game with a Vendetta! Unleash your inner risk-taker and earn huge rewards, or reversals. Play, prey, and maybe pray...but watch out for the Vendetta, the ultimate dirty trick that lets you make new enemies at twice your normal speed. We would tell you more, but then we'd have to.. well.. you know. Game comes with playing mat, dice, and cups. Ages 8 and up, for 2 or more players.

From Hasbro:

Grab your girlfriends and take center stage in this fun, interactive game! Spin the wheel to answer a question, tell a secret or perform a silly stunt – just like Hannah Montana would do! Ask each other fun questions and see what you know about the Hannah Montana show. Will you tell the truth or complete a dare? Your friends are the judges, so have a blast! Score the most points and be the winner as you discover cool stuff about your friends!

Game includes plastic game tray, cardboard wheel assembly with 2-piece spinner knob, 3 anchor pegs, 3 twist locks, 96 Hannah Montana secret cards and instructions.
For 2-4 players.

Ages 8 and up.

Käpt'n Blaubär Sieben Weltmeere Kartenspiel-Sammlung contains 7 simple card games for children.

The game contains German language rules, 32 cards and 30 life belt shaped counters.

It is a promotional free gift game from Deutsche Postbank AG.

(from the rules)
Isn’t it fun to blow the crap out of your friends while playing video games? Don’t you get a sense of smug self-satisfaction? Now get that feeling without getting motion sickness or eye-strain. Fragapalooza is a miniatures game of multi-player, shoot-‘em-up, video game-style action for 2 to 10 players.

The basic setting of Fragapalooza is your standard sci-fi shooter like Halo or Unreal. Players frag one-another on a board strewn with deadly weapons and fantastic power-ups. Change the names of the weapons, throw down some appropriate terrain and you can turn the game into WWII action, a western, spy thriller, etc.

Edit: The second edition of this game is now named "Frag-O-Rama".

Trick taking partnership game for 2,4 or 6 players. Half the deck are "American" cards with either Eagle or Liberty Bell symbols.  The half are "English" with either a Lion or a Crown symbol.  The highest cards are the King George and George Washington cards.

Part of Stoll & Edwards' "Two-Games-in-One" series, six games that each included a 16" x 16" board with a simple children's game on one side, usually involving familiar storybook characters, and a checkerboard on the other. Implements and rules were included for one game along with "a full set of checker men" (as a Stoll & Edwards catalog put it) for the other.

In this case, the "A" game is a simple spin-and-move race through the episodes of Lewis Carroll's Alice's Adventures in Wonderland (1865), including the Pool of Tears, the Caucus Race, the Mad Tea Party, and the Queen's Croquet Grounds. Occasional hazard spaces actually tend toward rewarding players, but occasionally they can force players to move back a few spaces or lose one or two turns.

The original Stoll & Edwards edition has a game board printed in three colors (black, orange and green). In their next incarnation as Stoll & Einson, the publisher reissued the game a decade later under their "Playjoy" brand, with board art utilizing a wider variety of colors and some illustrations revised to more strongly resemble Sir John Tenniel's famous book illustrations.

The game uses color coded letter tiles, each letter is a different color.

In the game play all players try and add letter parts to the table that help form a valid word.

The first player to get rid of all their tiles wins.

There are also several other variations of the Alphabet game, from spelling words out to types of spelling "relays".

This is a trivia game about knowledge of geography.
The map shows the northern hemisphere of our earth and above the 6 categories of questions: Continent, Surface area, Capital City, Inhabitants, Currency and Miscellaneous.

Each question card consists of 5 questions. Every player has to  put between 3 and 5 chips on the corresponding categories. This indicates he claims to know the answer.
Correct answers allow him to change 4 chips into a knowledge marker, false answers will make lose him a chip.
Goal of the game is to get 3 knowledge markers in 3 different continents.

There are catastrophic cards that threat players losing their knowledge markers, but they can save it by answering additional questions from special card pile.

The counters represent companies, institutions, leisure facilities or restaurants. The players collect and exchange during the play process these counters, in order to settle it in as valuable a combinations on the play plan as possible. But they receive points, which are noted on the memo pad. Who collected most points at the end of the play or after several plays, wins.

This childrens game consists of a set of cardboard cutouts of glasses. Children each take a 'glass' and take turns to roll a dice (different colour on each side). Rolling different colours allows children to fill up their 'glass' with different coloured fruit pieces. First to complete their knickerbocker glory wins.

"Avilude: or game of birds"

Players attempt to collect sets of Birds from the deck of 64 cards.  The deck contains 64 cards, divided in 8 groups.  Each group contains 4 bird cards showing pictures of birds and 4 corresponding description cards.  Several game variations are presented in the 8 page rules booklet.

The 8 groups include: wading, pigeon tribe, honey eaters, web-footed, birds of prey, running birds, and singing birds.

boardgame: Game of Baa Baa Black Sheep
description:
In this Sheep themed game that is similar to the card game Casino, players attempt to collect pairs of matching cards.

Each card, in addition to the illustration on it, has a letter that shows which cards they form pairs with.

Dice based horse racing game that features pari-mutual style betting pools.  Any number of players may play. Dice are rolled and the horses are moved up one space for each time that their number appears on the dice. A Colored multiplier die is rolled with the regular die and each horse whose number appears on the white dice is moved ahead as many places as appears on the colored die.

Players spin a large spinner all collect the points shown.  When a player has collected 100 points they get an tile with a piece of the apple on it.  The first player to complete an apple wins.  A pure luck game.

Described in the book Calculate!, Run for President is a representation of the Electoral College system for selecting a President of the United States.

Play makes use of a calculator - floating point division is used at the start to seed the game.  Beyond that point, the process could also be done simply by hand (multiplication by a single digit).  The first three digits of the resulting number  gives the state and number of votes cast in that state.

When all states have at least one vote cast in them, the game ends.  The player with the highest number of votes cast in the state wins the state and scores points equal to the electoral votes the state has.

Described in the book Calculate!, Target Number is a game where players are using math operations trying to get the calculator to display a number in the range of 10 to 20.

The game is played on a track that is a large loop.  Each player uses the numbers on the track in order.  For example, if the calculator displayed 196 and the next number was 512, followed by 70 one could add 512 to number giving 708 and then divide by 70 which would give 10.1142... This number is in the range and would score  points for the player who would then pass the calculator to the next player who would continue on the track.  Play ends when all the numbers in the loop are have been used.

Published in Japan, but sold briefly as a ziplock in the US at the Origins convention, this is a wargame of the crucial October battles at Guadalcanal.  In fact, the game's subtitle is "Bloody October."

Game includes a Masahiro Yamazaki map of Henderson Field and the surrounding area done in a somewhat stylized manner.  There are 264 counters including several surface ships and planes.  The Japanese rules are twenty pages of 8.5 x 11, done in a sepia tone. including some advertisements for the Japanese edition of Command magazine.  The English rules are sixteen black and white xeroxed pages, also 8.5 x 11 unbound, although two sided and perfectly readable.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: Spy game about searching a red mercury substance. Every player controls secret agents from CIA, KGB, Mossad or Jihad organization. They explore laboratory, attack each others and try to capture a red mercury substance.
Players control their spaceships and travel in the Solar System. They accomplish tasks and earn cash. TransSolar contains an unconventional moving system.

A 2 player game where players move pieces across a game board by following instructions on cards.

Game includes instructions, a checkered board, 32 wooden playing pieces, and 65 instruction cards.

Doomsday America is a lighthearted apocalypse themed board and card game. The USA is divided up into 9 zones. Players have population counters in these zones. Players use disaster cards to eliminate their opponents' counters. Plagues, Pestilence, Locusts, Comets, Invasions, Zombies, it's all there. Score Victory points each turn for the zones you control. Depopulate 2 Zones to end the game. Free rule set available at:  http://www.angelfire.com/games2/warpspawn/DDA.html

Use your skills to land the marbles in the holes and earn the most points. Comes with 12 marbles in 4 colors.

Place the metal tray on the floor or table top.
Each player chooses three of the same colored marbles.

Each player takes a turn at rolling their marbles into the tray.
You can only roll each marble once. If you miss the tray you can not roll again.
try to land the marbles in the holes with the highest point values.

If you knock loose one of the marbles already in the tray you lose that marble's point score. After all three marbles have been rolled add up your total score. 
The person with the highest score is the winner!

ALTERNATE VERSION:
Each player rolls one marble at a time trying to knock out the other player's marble's.
After each player rolls their three marbles the player with the highest score is the WINNER!

A cute Eastern German track cycling game. It features a 60 field track, six riders, two wooden dice, and the rules to play 6 different races: Sprint, Elimination, Pursuit, a 10 lap race, point race & the 6 days race.

Radfahrerspiel is a cycling bases race game produced in East Germany in 1955. It has simple roll & moves mechanics and the goal is to reach the finish first by completing a track as soon as possible. On that track the players also have to pass a railway crossover. Three cars have to be moved, too, and can be used to block other players.

From the rules:

"These rules were made to give players a simple set of rules for quick-play conventions games, giving a general feel for the period. The French campaigns in Algeria, Morocco and the Sahara were hard-fought affairs and lasted many decades.  Reflecting that reality, these are no “form square and bash the natives” kind of rules: the peoples of North Africa had a warrior tradition going back to the time of the Romans. The Arabs, Berbers, and Tuareg were fierce warriors to be reckoned with and respected for their skill and courage. These rules are meant to capture some of that spirit."

Described in the book Calculate!, Invasion is a game where players try to capture regions forming a path to the opponent's capital.

Each region has a number on it, increasing in value the closer to the capital.  Play alternates between attacking and defending.  The attacker selects a region in the opponent's country that borders either the common border or one of the previously captured regions.  The attacker and the defender each secretly select a set of contiguous numbers from their grid of numbers.  The attacker selects from three to six and the defender selects one to three numbers.  The product of the attackers numbers is divided by the product of the defenders numbers.  If the resulting value is larger than the region number, the attacker colors the  region in.  If the  value is lower,  the defender colors the region in.  Once a region is colored in, it may not change hands again.  Numbers used in attack or defense may not be used again either.

A player wins when the other capital is captured or when he seals off all paths to his own capital.  If a player does not have three contiguous numbers on his grid, he may no longer attack.  If neither player may attack, then the winner is determined by comparing the sum of the regions that each would need to take including capturing the capital - the lower sum wins.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
This 140-page hardcover (a soft cover version was also published) has rules for a whole new way of playing Warhammer Fantasy Battle (3rd Edition) and Warhammer 40K (Rogue Trader) focused on sieging a castle.  New rules for things like siege engines and dropping items, say boiling oil; a new flow for play; and a system for buying castle defences within the scope of building Fantasy and 40K armies are all presented.

The end papers are the reference sheets needed to play the game.  Also included are two counter sheets with items one might need around a castle, like rocks and sacks of gunpowder.  There are also diagrams that can be copied which allow the defender to secretly lay out his or her defences before the game starts.  Then, when the time is ripe, the defenders pop up and are shown by placing their models onto the castle

Like any Games Workshop book, there is plenty of eye candy. This book was released at the same time as Games Workshop's Might Fortress castle model set.

Expansion for:

    Warhammer Fantasy Battle (Third Edition)
    Warhammer 40,000: Rogue Trader

French cycling game that was also released in a German edition. The game is presented in a big (42x33cm) plastic suitcase that contains all components. It features 12 cyclist riders (they are 6 cm high, which is rather big) in 6 different colours, thus 2 riders per team. As in most racing games, the goal is to reach the finish first with your riders.

BigBalls represents an advanced development of EinStein würfelt nicht! and consists of similarly short rules. The neutral balls however do not allow to use the same successful strategies as in EinStein würfelt nicht!.

The wooden gameboard is triangular with 28 fields.
Each player gets a color (black or white) with six stones each. In addition there are six neutral marbles, called Big Balls in the third corner.

When it's your turn you may roll a dice and move an according numbered stone of yours upwards or sideways or you move one of the BigBalls downwards.
You may beat (eliminate) own stones as this enables you to move faster. But this also bears the risk running out of stones that will cause losing the game.
Winner of the game is the player who reaches first the corner of the BigBalls with one of his own stones.
The aim of the game is to proceed slowly on the way to hell and to be the last player outside the hell.

Glossotechnia is a card game in which the players collaboratively create a new language, and attempt to translate certain challenge sentences into this game-language. It combines competitive and cooperative elements, and can be used in teaching certain concepts in linguistics.

http://jimhenry.conlang.org/conlang/glossotechnia.html
A simple and extremely rare roll-and-move children's game in which the point is to get out of the woods.  The cover features a girl in a red riding hood.

This game is much like Run Yourself Ragged/Snafu. This one requires batteries.

Instructions- Place a silver ball into the area in the upper left marked 'start'. Turn the auto timer clockwise to the start position and off you go.
A - Go around twirling posts
B - Through the first orange gate
C - Onto the topsy turvy steps, to the top, then down the other side
D - Through the next gate
E - Fing you way through the 'blind alley'
F - And through the treacherous tunnel
G - Open the last pair of closed gates
H - Under the arch
I - Into the 'whirling well' and out the end of the maze

From the instructions:

"The purpose of this game is for enjoyment, self-improvement, and fundamental education of golfing rules and regulations.  TOUR GOLF(TM) is designed to be played as you would normally play a game of golf.  The winner is the player who has the lowest total strokes at the completion of 18 holes of golf."

The game includes the following:
2 white d6 with pips
1 white sand wedge d6 numbered 1,2,2,3,3,4
1 green putting d6 numbered 1,2,2,2,2,3
4 golf tees for use as pawns
1 22"x28" board
1 pad containing rules and scorecards

The game box offers to let you "play the famous golf courses you've always dreamed of playing--in your home!" and promises that "other golf courses will be available in the future."

Die Friedensfahrt (aka "Course de la Paix") was the most important cycling race behind the iron curtain. It started in 1948 (and is still running: in 2006 it was the 58th edition, though the importance of the race is much lesser, and it is not run by national teams anymore). Of course, the race needed a companion board game! The game can be played with a maximum of 6 players and features twelve cards with portraits of the winners of the first 12 editions of the race and the name of the 12 stages. First card features two riders since the first edition of the Friedensfahrt was split in two legs, so there were two winners. The board shows a track from Prague via Warszawa to Berlin. He who scores best over all the stages wins the game.

French cycling game appeared at the end of the '80s. What makes it special is that it is about female cycling. It's played with 2 dice, tactic cards, mountain cards, sprint cards and rouleur cards. Each players controls 2 cyclists. The player who scores best in the overall classification wins the Tour Féminin.

Mind Ninja is a simple abstract strategy game for 2 players.

It is unusual among board games in that the players themselves invent the win condition when the game starts, in a way that ensures both that equally-skilled players have equal chances to win, and that ties are impossible. This feature leads to great variety and depth of play.

The game is also a generalization of the the game of Hex, which means that the set of all possible ways that a game of Hex can play out is a subset of the set of all the possible ways a game of Mind Ninja can play out.  Mind Ninja is a generalization of many other games as well, including Go-moku (5-in-a-row), Havannah, One-Capture Go, Y, Poly-Y, and *Star, among others.

It can be played with stones of two (or more) colors on any tiled surface. It can also be played with paper and colored markers. A version of the rules is available here.

Emperor of China is a card game simulating the rise and fall of dynastic empires throughout the turbulent history of China. The winner of the current hand becomes the new Emperor and gains Victory points. The other players are Rebels and Barbarians looking to gain power next time.

Conquests of Charlemagne is a card game depicting the exploits of Charles the Great, King of the Franks, 768-814 AD. Players carefully play cards to be the first to conquer 10 regions and gain Victory points while preventing their opponents' from doing the same.

This is a beautiful old spin-and-move racing game from Milton Bradley that was first published in 1873, clearly to latch on to the fame of Jules Verne's novel Around the World in 80 Days, published in the same year. Unlike the voyage of Phineas Fogg, the game's track is strictly by sea.

Both the game equipment and rules are more complex than usual for a Victorian spin-and-move. The key difference is that winning does not mean who completing the 93-space spiral track the fastest, but doing so in the fewest days. In addition to spaces that move players forward and back, many of them apply time penalties. Each player has a card that tracks the number of days taken - one per pip on the five-space teetotum and one more for each penalty day. With absolutely perfect spins, it is possible to complete the journey in just 76 days, but this is highly unlikely!

Each player card has one metal arrow on dial that counts up to 50 days, and another on a smaller quarter-circle dial that tracks multiples of 50 to a pessimistic maximum of 450. On the back of each card is a "Record Tablet" with a space for the days taken for each stage from New York to: Equator, Rio de Janeiro, Cape Horn, Equator, San Francisco, Sandwich Islands, Hong Kong, Anjer, Mauritius, Cape of Good Hope, St. Helena, Equator and back to New York. The 1873 edition includes a global map showing the voyage track that is formed into a sleeve wrapped around the player cards.

Barbarian Hordes is a card and dice game depicting the clash of Empires and Barbarian Nations in Europe circa 400 AD. Simple system of raising armies and conquering your opponents' territories. Be you Roman, Hun, or Goth, crush your foes and take all that you see by force.

This is an adult game that uses dice two sets of dice, five decks of cards and a color-coded spinner. You turn over a card  from the Foreplay deck and read it aloud. Then turn over a card from the Passion deck and read it. The former explains what you'll both be doing in the current "round of play" and the latter explains what's next.

"You and your lover may want to spend time familiarizing yourselves with the Passion Card, because it may affect the strategies you use while carrying out Foreplay."
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Battle of the Sexes: Bedroom Edition
description:
This is the adult version of the game battle of the sexes. The object is for foe two teams (Boys vs Girls) to get across the board by answering gender based risque and revealing questions.

A fun party game if you have the right mix for the teams.

The cardgame consists of a booklet with 100 questions and answers, 36 cards showing monarchs and 100 small cards with answers.
Any number of persons may play at this game in the following manner: a President must be appointed, who is to see if the questions are correctly answered: the president distributes about six or eight counters to each player, the remainder to be left in the box, which will serve for a pool. He then deals the answers round, an equal number to each player; after this is done, he shuffles the pack of Heads, and placing the whole pack face downwards on the table, desires one of the players to take the uppermost and read aloud the name of the king, when he was born, died, etc. The president then asks the question from this little book, connected with the card turned up and whoever plays the proper answer, receives one counter from the pool; but if the answer is wrong, one counter must be paid to the pool. The next player then takes a Head and the same rule must be observed till all of them are read. When a player has forfeited all his counters, his remaining cards must be given to the player on his right hand. Should the pool become empty, each player must contribute one counter.

The game is decided in two ways, either by all but one player having forfeited their counters, or by all the heads having been turned up, in which case the remaining players are to reckon their counters, and he who has the greatest number wins the game. The winner takes the contents of the pool, and claims the office of president in the next game.

THE GAME OF THE GAMUT a Musical Gift for the Young - By John Betts, 115 Strand, London (his address 1845 - 1874) - The game consists of a wooden box with sliding lid containing thirty-six playing cards each showing a music book with chord on it and trumpet with number to top. One player calls out a number and the person holding the card - replies with the word spelled out by bass and treble notes - A good way of teaching children to read music - counters are won or lost depending on the outcome. The game is dated by the Jigsaws advertised inside the lid which seem to have been produced c1845 (ref Linda Hannas) as Betts himself dated very few items.

Japanese card game, which looks similar to UNO, but is a set collection/hand management game.  Domingo is spelled with the "m" turned on one end resembling a three as the game is about collecting triplets (three of a kind.)

From the patent description:
"A multiple player word forming game is disclosed. The game uses a board, a cup and a plurality of cards. The cards are randomly transferred to and from the players. The players use the cards to form words. In the best mode, a game board defines a flat surface bounded by a peripheral wall upon which the cup freely travels about. A player begins a turn by spinning the cup, which eventually lands upon a card transfer space. The card transfer space dictates whether the player either draws or passes cards. The player transfers cards either from the deck or to a discard pile or to or from another player during a turn. Then the player attempts to spell a word by selecting cards in his hand and laying them face-up in front of him. Opponents may challenge the player's word. Any player who transfers all of his cards during a set becomes empty-handed and may not challenge words. The number of points earned by each player is dependent upon their performance both in forming words and in manipulating the number of cards in their hand. Preferably, a chance card multiplies the number of points a player can get. The set ending player's total score is doubled if the player ended the set with the chance card. Other player's scores are multiplied by zero if they held the chance card when an opponent ended the set."

In English, players use a cup shaped spinner that will move around the 5x5 board to determine card movement between the players. Players have to build words from their available cards and score points displayed on the cards.

A childrens game, or rather rules for three different games. The box contains the rules and a set of tiles. Each tile shows a word with a picture to illustrate it. The tiles are in groups of 4 and all tiles within a group rhyme.

The three games are basically variants of memory or pairs though in one of the games children are encouraged to make silly sentences out of the words they are collecting.
The goal is to bring one pawn to the center of the maze, using the chips like bridges or barriers.

A small twist on a classic children's game, (though it is often enjoyed by adults).  In addition to the classic pop-o-matic die, Stitch (the Alien from the Disney movie Lilo and Stitch) is trapped in the bubble, adding the possibility of a "free 6".  The counters are shaped to look like the security forces on the galactic command ship.  Sold at Disney theme parks and Disney stores, it makes waiting in line much more fun!
This simple children's game was hand-colored!  Featured in the Liman Collection of board games at the New York Historical Society.

The goal of Allianz is to beat all pieces of the other players by jumping over them with your own pieces or to conquer all enemy castles. Everyone plays on one's own but alliances can be formed that prohibit two players from attacking each other during a certain number of turns.
Players try to roll 9 times 9 without rolling 9 cats. The 1 on each die is replaced with a cat.
Discover the city of Marseilles and it's history. The game contains a board with a detailed map of Marseilles, cards in two different colours and 5 pawns.
Simple role & move cycling games published in Belgium in the 1950's. The 43x34cm. board shows a track with some obstacles on several squares. The first to reach the finish with his pawn wins.

The English Civil war is a Card game for 2 players simulating the British Civil Wars 1642-1649. One player is the Parliamentarians and the other is the Royalists. Simultaneous turns. The nature of confrontations are determined by whether players choose to attack or defend. Players accumulate troops counters and fight each other for territory tokens. Battles are resolved with dice and card play. Flavor is provided by the cards. A free copy of the rules is available at: 
http://www.angelfire.com/games2/warpspawn/TECW.html

Quod is an abstract strategy game played on an 11-by-11 grid of spaces with the four corner spaces removed.  Players alternate placing pieces, called quods, into empty spaces.  A player wins by placing four quods that form the four corners of a square.  The square can be any size and any orientation (in other words, the square can be "tilted").

In addition, each player has a small number of pieces, called quasars, that are used purely for blocking.

Quod was invented by G. Keith Stills in 1979, while he was at college, and was popularized by Scientific American in March 1996.

Many computer implementations exist, such as
http://www.geocities.com/SiliconValley/Heights/2220/quod.html
and
http://wizard.ae.krakow.pl/~jb/Quod/

boardgame: Mit Knorr Peterli auf zur Tour de Suisse
description: Promotional cycling game released in the '50s by Knorr about the Tour of Switzerland.

Using the same movement rules of standard Road Cycling Tour, this version provides a simplified set of track segments while also applying an advanced rule that is an optional variation in the standard game. This means each player’s cumulative selection for the special movement category (energy bursts for breakaway and sprint actions) must fit within a maximum budget restriction.

Note that the current written version of the rules relies upon a prior understanding of the standard game (most importantly regarding rules of movement). It does not provide a stand-alone description.

Dice are not used, here. Each player controls a deck of 48 cards for all movement, and each card contains information to serve multiple purposes. Players can divide the cards as they wish, selecting some for special movement skills (bidding for breakaway and sprint) while the remainder constitutes a random-draw stack that will be used for basic movement (ordinary counts of zero through 4).
family: Versions & Editions: Travel Versions of Non-Travel Games

description: The Course de la Paix was the most important cycling race behind the iron curtain. It started in 1948 and is still running. This is a Czech game from 1991. Unique for a cycling game is that it feature hexagons. The board is big (90x60cm.) and reversible. Each team consists of 4 wooden riders (20 in total since it can be played with up to 5 people).

A very simple man-to-man card game that appeared in White Dwarf 130.  Basically it takes combat books and turns them into decks of cards.  Players representing knights may take either attack or defense cards for hands of 6 cards, then battle it out.  If they survive a "hand," cards are replaced and play continues until only one remains.

A single route/multiple route game. Played on a square board with multiple concentric rings. The players have two counters - one for their base, which scores points for whenever the ball runs over it. The other counter is for their workman, whose job it is to run around and make changes to the ring. If the ball runs over the workman, they are returned to base. The ball travels clockwise around the ring and players can build intersections or branches on each ring to divert the ball to other rings, to change the ball type or even split up or combine balls.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: Murder Mystery Party: Murder in Transylvania
description:
Who killed Sonya Right?

Welcome to Castle Gladjorhere, home of the world’s one and only Transvampire with a soul! Visitors from all walks of life have come to witness the thrills and chills of this genuine Transylvanian house of horror, all of which will be revealed by the resident tour guide Sonya Right. But shortly after dinner Sonya is found in her room with two small puncture wounds in her neck. Host your own party to find out who sucked the life out of her!

The suspects :
COUNT GLADJORHERE – Tranvestite Vampire (he’ll bite and excite!)
SHEILA SUCKLE – Countess Gladjorhere (she drinks every last drop!)
HUMP ZEALOT – The Count’s manservant (he’s always on the job!)
ELLIE GANTT – International catwalk model (she’s purr-fect!)
MR FRANKNBEANZ – The Count’s chef (don’t ask for his secret ingredients!)
BRIDE OF FRANKNBEANZ – His wife (she’s always in stitches!)
PROFESSOR GRAF ENBERG – Academic (his theories always hit the spot!)
HEIDI SEEK – Local journalist (she’s always exposing things!)

Contents :
DVD with 4 scenes, background music CD, invitations, host instructions, character profiles for the 8 suspects, costume suggestions, menu suggestions, clues, name tages, solution to the murder.

From the box:

You Name It! the thrilling new board game that's the life of the party from Hollywood, California to Moose Jaw, Saskatchewan.  The fun springs from the players' inventiveness in concocting their own imaginative clues.  There's no limit to the number of players or the amount of fun that can be had playing this exciting game.

You Name It! is a team game where players take turns guessing and calling out clues to identities of famous people in five categories:  History, Screen, Music, Arts & Literature, and Sports.  Teams take sixty second turns guessing as many names as they can.  The names are both up-to-the minute and old as time itself.  Clues may include descriptions of the person, their accomplishments who they married, who they killed, etc. - or a syllable by syllable breakdown of the name itself.

If you're looking for an exciting fun-filled evening with a group of friends: You Name It!, we've got it!

From the back of the box:

It's a real fashion emergency as everybody's favorite girls with a passion for fashion, the Bratz, find themselves in a crazy race to see who can put together the hottest fashion first! Create the perfect look to make heads turn all night! The first girl to complete her outfit and make it home first WINS the game & gets TOP fashion honors!

Game includes: Game Board, Dice, 16 Player Pieces

Charge! appeared in White Dwarf issue 115.  The game combines Citadel Combat Cards with Citadel Miniatures to create a very simple combat game or, using Games Workshops' Dungeon Lairs, a very simple dungeon crawl game.

Zog Off! Ya Thievin' Lil Git! appeared in White Dwarf #117.  It was a clever use of the bits from Trolls in the Pantry along with some cut outs from the magazine.  The goal for each gobbo is to collect sets of food and get them into the pantry.  The trolls are trying to collect ALL the food and get it into the troll cave.
Simple children's roll-and-move game.  Think Snakes and Ladders only spiraling outward trying to avoid the spiders while escaping the web.

Northern Wars is a Card game that depicts the conflicts in Northern Europe circa 13th-15th Centuries AD. 12 unique Major powers available each with their own special ability. Players accumulate Army counters and Territory Tokens and they draw from Battle Decks and Event Decks. A complete copy of the rules is available for free at: 
http://www.angelfire.com/games2/warpspawn/NW.html

Lancaster & York is a card game simulation of the War of the Roses in England 1455-1485. Players accumulate troops and noble supporters by recruitment and intrigue. The losing side attempts to start a revolt to bring the King to battle. Winner of the battle becomes the new king and starts chopping off Heads. Players share a common deck. Win by remaining King for 5 turns or killing off 5 enemy Heirs. A complete copy of the rules and cards is available for free at: http://www.angelfire.com/games2/warpspawn/LAY.html
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: Reconquista is a wargame recounting the fighting in Spain circa 1050-1450. Players build up Armies and conquer provinces on a map of the Iberian Peninsula from each other. Players have standard unit counter sets. The game uses an Event deck and Battle Deck. Battles are resolved using a combination of dice work and card play. Raiding rules and events give a feel for the period.

boardgame: Vuelta Ciclista: el Juego con Kelme
description:
Beautiful Spanish game released at the end of the '90s and  endorsed by the Kelme cycling team. The game has three different levels of play, including one in which you manage your team buying riders according to your budget.

The game has three gorgeous boards in which you can play the Vuelta, Giro or Tour. Each of these races consists of three stages plus a time trial. (Except for the basic level, in which you use dice, the time trial consists of Trivial Pursuit-like questions.)

Each team consists of three cyclists (a leader, a climber and a rouleur) and this game has everything a cycling game should have (mountains, intermediate sprints, drawing from other riders; this one also has falls, punctures, exhaustions... even an antidoping test).
Rare Italian track cycling game published in the 1930's. The game included eight over sized cardboard cyclists and a board with an oval track on it.

French vintage cycling game published in the '50s. It is about 2 famous classic cycling races: Paris-Roubaix and Paris-Tours. The game consists of two parts. The first part is played on the green part of the board, and it is a dice game. The sprint of the race is played on the  velodrome (blue) part of the board and uses the aleatory record.

An aleatory record (also known as "multitrack record", "magic record" or "puzzle record") is one that has some separate tracks cut in exact step with each other, so that upon lowering the needle the track is selected at random. On the game's record, the beginning of the narration is the same in all tracks, and just the result of the sprint changes. Each side has 10 different tracks with every cyclist having its chance to win, naturally. Every time you play the record you get a different result, so you'd better listen carefully who won the race!

A French 1930s game, of which there were four versions. The first version only had 6 riders (one per team, no rules for team racing). Later versions had twelve riders (two per team). The cheaper versions had cardboard riders, the "deluxe" version had metal riders.  Veldiv, short for Vélodrome d'Hiver (winter velodrome), was a very popular cycling track in Paris.

Vintage simple British cycling game released in the mid to late 30's and named after the longest possible trajectory in Great Britain. The game is a race from the bottom of England to the top of the mainland of Scotland with progress made by the roll of a dice. Players have to abide by the instructions on the board which may aid or hinder progress.

The game is based on the Lands End to John O'Groats cycling competition which is not strictly a race but a time trial competition. The cyclist pictured on the game box is Sir Hubert Opperman (known as Oppy), considered as one of Australia greatest sporting heros in the 1920's and 30's. In 1934 is set a new record from Lands End to John O'Groats cutting more than 4 hours of the previous time.

At least 2 versions of the game box exist with a smaller rectangular box and a larger more square box. Both versions have the same game board layout and rules and a photo of Hubert Opperman on the game box, although the photos differ.

The game rules indicate that the game is included in a series of games that were produced by the National Games Company and may imply this game was also manufactured by National games. No reference to National games is on the board or box but playing pieces are similar in style to boxed National Games and National games did produce many of their games in 2 sizes.
Themed Cycling game (Donald Duck theme) released by a company from Berlin in 1998. Basically it's a simple roll & move game with card board riders.

Travel around the board buying your favorite landmarks from the movie Shrek 2.  The game is over when one player runs out of money.

For 2 - 4 players Ages 5 and up
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Puss in Boots
Versions & Editions: Versions with IP after the original release

description:
El Joc de l'Especulador is a card game based in other games like Mafia, Werewolf, etc. In this edition the action takes place in a city where corrupted businessmen want to take control of the city by buying the citizen's estates.

The game is published under a Creative Commons license, and was distributed for free from the web page of the editor. Check the links for the instructions and cards!

The Fraggle Trap Game is based on the characters from Fraggle Rock, a children's television series from the 1980's. The series was created by Jim Henson, primarily featuring a cast of Muppet creatures called Fraggles.
Fraggles are always hungry - the object of the game is to feed your Fraggles with as many radishes as possible by collecting them from the Gorg's radish patch & bringing them to your corner.

An entirely different game from MB's 'Fraggle Rock'.

Fantastic memories !!!

Dance your cares away, 
Worry's for another day.
Let the music play,
Down at Fraggle Rock !

Work your cares away,
Dancing's for another day.
Let the Fraggles play,
We're Gobo, Mokey, Wembley, Boober, Red.

Dance your cares away,
Worry's for another day.
Let the music play,
Down at Fraggle Rock !

based on description from Angel Fire Site:

Two player card game based on the Time Quartet series of books by Madeleine L’Engle.
One player is the side of Good. 
The other player is the side of Evil.

SETUP
Each player begins in possession of 3 Balance Tokens.

TURN SEQUENCE
Each turn has 10 phases:
Destiny Phase I
Fate Phase I
Challenge Phase I
Destiny Phase II
Fate Phase II
Challenge Phase II
Destiny Phase III
Fate Phase III
Challenge Phase III
Test Phase

DESTINY PHASES
Each player draws 7 cards from his deck.
If a Deck runs out, shuffle the discard and draw from it.

VICTORY
The First player to be in control of all 6 Balance Tokens is the winner.

The players are brought into the year 1558 and face each other as citizens and students. With strategy and a little luck they try to push through their interests in the blossoming university town of Jena.
Designer Prof. Ingo Althöfer developed “1558 - Hanfrieds inheritance” exclusively for the Jenaer university's 450 year anniversary. A short game, not only for the break between two lectures. Printed on ecological hemp cloth. Unusually packed in a silver aluminum cylinder. Limited Edition.
   
The special reason for using hemp cloth for the board was a word joke (hemp = Hanf in German): Hanfried == Han(f)fried == Hanf-Fried. The price of the game was exactly 15.58 Euro, motivated by the year 1558 when Jena University was founded.
 Information from the designer: I realized one very personal copy with wooden board (instead of hemp cloth) where the (neutral) Hanfried piece is a real silver coin. Two pictures of this item are found in the picture gallery.

This game offers at least for the fan of the Munich comedian genuine cult potential. In funny small dialog , which originate partially from the feather/spring from Bully Herbig, a search term is replaced in the text by the word “Bully”. While a player reads out the texts, the others must try to guess the term. This guessing takes place into four steps, trying to encircle the term a bit in each round. Amazing what (wrong) associations some first references may lead to.
While this first part of the game is strictly played in sequence, as soon as someone says the correct answer the game changes into chaotic action.
The so-called riddle master must try to catch with a cup the figures of the others, which stand in the center of the game board. They try to pull fast enough the cord and thus theirs figures of the board, otherwise point loss.
This odd mixture of knowledge and dexterity, has even something from the parodic humor of the Bully films.
Two long-proved mechanisms are connected here in new and unusual way to an entertaining game.

Introducing... The sound making, second-hand drawing, tongue twisting, face making, people directing, acting out, gut busting, laugh so hard that you cry, one of a kind, gotta have it, more fun than you ever had playing with your friends and family hoot of a charades game!

Simple word game in which players create silly sentences from phrases on cards.  Example:

I'm always in for

Hunting buffalo

At a wedding

Leading to outrageous fun at the turn of the century.
Simple party game, whoever answers the most riddles wins.  Example: What word is that to which, if you add a syllable, will make is shorter?  Answer: Short.

(From the publishers web site)
Primera Batalla, the Spanish Civil War supplement for Disposable Heroes and Coffin for Seven Brothers.

This book contains information on the various factions, and the artillery, vehicles, and aircraft they employed.

From the publisher: "It’s the bride’s last chance to play before the fateful day and there’s no better way to kick off her special party than The Bachelorette Party Game, the ultimate party game for wild women only.  But beware!  This game contains some very personal questions that are guaranteed to reveal everything you never wanted to know about your best girlfriends… and yourself."

Players roll and move around a board and answer personal questions which are very adult in nature.  Includes specially shaped game board, Game booklet with over 150 questions, pawns, die, and 54 "Beefcake" cards.

The object of The Picnic Game is to be the first player to set their place and gather all the food.   Players take turns spinning a spinner and collecting the items they need for the picnic (including a main dish, a vegetable, a fruit, a drink, a dessert, a plate, a napkin & utensils, and a picnic blanket.  There is something of an order in which the items need to be collected, but if the spinner stops on the ants, a player must give back an item.  The pieces of the game are made of laminated chipboard and the artwork is very appealing for younger children.

The Game of Psych-Out is a personality/psychological themed party game.  Players answer a series of questions designed to evoke revealing information about their personalities and then try to guess which other players provided the answers to the questions.

Gimme A Ruling is a golf-themed sports trivia/question and answer game that focuses on the rules of golf.  The game contains 550 questions that present actual on-the-course situations and challenges the players to provide the correct ruling.  (The situations, questions and rulings presented on the game cards are taken directly from the book, Decisions on the Rules of Golf, as published by the USGA together with the Royal and Ancient of St. Andrews, Scotland.) The winner of the game is the player with the lowest score after 18 holes/questions.

Game includes: 550 question/answer cards, 3 20-sided dice: 1 Category, 1 Birdie, 1 Bogey, 2 official score pads, and 2 golf pencils

Kerala is a race game from Asia.  Two or three players move their five pieces around a course, dictated by the throws of five cowrie shells.  The board consists of a square perimeter track and three "runways" approaching a corner, through which pieces enter and exit the board.  The object of the game is to be the first to move one's pieces onto the board via a runway, around the course to the centre, around the course again and bear off via the runway.

60-card deck featuring the outstanding achievements and brief biographical history of each President of the United States from Washington to Pierce.

The object of the game is to obtain complete books (set of 4 presidents) by calling for cards from other players.  When a player gets a complete book of 4, he lays it aside; when all the cards have been gathered into books, the game is over and the player with the most books wins.

Each player chooses a character, each of which are double-sided.  One side is BIG in which case you move forward, and the other side is SMALL, in which case you move backwards.
Players move up the board similar to Snakes & Ladders but with the added excitement of extras like the Looking Glass & the Jabberwocky square.

Contents

1 x Playing Board
6 x Double-sided counters
1 x Looking glass counter
1 x Die
1 x Die cup
Rules in box lid ( English, French & Dutch)

From the publisher's web site:
The board game Terminated was the brainchild of the experiences of its creator.  After more than a decade of employment in different companies and industries, different departments and sectors of the businesses, from agriculture to software production, I realized that at the end of the day, one common factor was evident; the desire for career advancement and the fruits and privileges that accompanied the advancement.   To me, everything seemed to revolve around this common factor, Advancement; the economy, lifestyle and life in general all seemed to be a game where the rules were already written and I had to follow them.

With this understanding in mind, I embarked on the arduous task of arranging it all on paper and it wasn’t long before I had a complete and playable game.  The circular design of the game was chosen to emphasize the revolving aspect of the game.  I had four gas stations to remind us of both the need for gas and the high gas prices.  I included childcare; a common denominator in our families and most other successful and complete families.  Since we all have to pay taxes, I included a slot for the IRS on the game.  It was abundantly clear that since I got paid once every pay period, then one complete revolution around the board was equivalent to one complete pay period.  Domestic utilities were also included in the game for this was an everyday necessity.

There were several things I could have included in the game such as food and health insurance but I ran out of room.  However, I did include vacation, a common phenomenon at work, whether earned or incidental such as sick time or penalty (suspensions).  The Superior Court was included to provide a platform for one to forward his/ her grievances and concerns and to provide a platform for one to mount their best defense whenever the circumstance arose.

For disciplinary purpose and as an instrument of the constant penalty awaiting the lawbreakers, I included the jail slot on the game.  I also included the INS slot on the game due to the fact that there are numerous undocumented immigrants who masquerade as legal entities hence the need for consistently proving ones eligibility for employment.  As much as I despise paying the high insurance premiums, the state law requires that we maintain auto insurance at all times.  In an effort to keep the game fun and entertaining, I included the mailbox and email as tools to deliver messages, promotions, bonus, and even penalties.

The eight departments, Shipping and Receiving, Production, Assembly, Customer Service, Packaging, Maintenance, Quality Assurance and Management were chosen because I had personal experience in them and I felt that they were crucial to any manufacturing enterprise.  Other departments such as accounting, advertising and marketing though important to a company were not included because I figured that some companies were able to operate profitably by either doubling or outsourcing these departments.
Plays 18 holes of golf with cards.

Michael Graves retools the classic game  with a new look. Includes 12 hotels, 32 houses and 6 Michael Graves Monopoly tokens. Tokens include desk clock, desk lamp, suitcase, teapot, blender, and, toaster. Houses and Hotels are made of rubber and the board has a silvery prismatic finish. The money is semi see-through (almost like a parchment paper). All of the pieces store neatly in a plastic cast that slides into its own carrying case. The game fully stored measures 14 inches length x 8.5 inches width x 3.5 inches height. Great for travel!
A game designed to teach proper social interaction, players connected by a "phone" pose questions that are answered by phrases on the game cards.

An early trivia game, each card has questions about three men who share the first letter of their last names.  For instance one card has questions on Shakespeare, Stanley, and Scott.  Typical question: R-A famous Dutch artist born 1607, died 1669.  Answer: Rembrandt.  (You knew that, right?)

A game done by Ravensburger as a merchandising game for the Handelskammer Hamburg. Ages 12 and up
Consists of
1 board
200 cards
200 stocks
315 playing money
1 die
some wooden game pieces

This game war released approx. in the mid 1960s by Verlag Hoppenstedt from Darmstadt/Germany as a merchandising game for the Dresder Bank AG. The name Concentra is the name of a big German investment fund and the game leads players to learn about investing and divesting with stocks.

Up to 8 players move around the board three times. The player with the most money wins the game. Movement is determined by a die. In the first round, if a player gets on a green space, he gets the corresponding bond. At the roll of a 6 he gets a Concentra-Fund unit. In the second and third round, a player who gets on a green space has to pay the owner of the corresponding bond and a dividend for the Concentra-Fund. At the end of the game the dividend is divided among the fund owners.

Phil Barker's Horse, Foot, Gun is a set of tabletop rules that is based on the same system as De Bellis Antiquitatis: Quick Play Wargame Rules with Army Lists for Ancient and Medieval Battles and its successors. It covers the period from 1701 to 1915.

Re-implements:

    De Bellis Antiquitatis: Quick Play Wargame Rules with Army Lists for Ancient and Medieval Battles
    De Bellis Multitudinis: Wargames Rules for Ancient and Medieval Battles – 3000 BC to 1500 AD
    Hordes of the Things
    De Bellis Renationis
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Hand of Fate Fortune Telling Game
description: A simple trivia game with three kinds of questions: identify the picture, put a series of related historic events in chronological order, and true or false.  The "hand of fate" is the spinner that points to a question on the board.  The game does not actually seem to have any relation to fortune telling.

In this game each player represents an enterprising transporter.  The object of the game is to be the first to earn either E500,000 in the short game, or E1,000,000 in the standard game.

Players earn money by transporting cargo in their trucks between European cities.  You can invest the money earned, buy more trucks or bigger ones, or increase the radius of your deliveries.

Actions available each turn depend upon the roll of the dice.  Actions include taking a chance card, taking a cargo card and auctioning it, driving the trucks or refueling.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Be the first to bury all of your Burying pieces!

Lift a Crown to bury one of your pieces.  Play moves clockwise and each player does the same.  If you uncover a Burying piece, add it to your supply.  Revolve the tray as far as you like during your own turn.  Bury all of your Burying pieces and be the winner!

Each player takes a score sheet.  High roller starts.  Players are asked questions corresponding to the die number rolled each turn, scoring one point if correct.  A bonus question may be asked if available.  If a player answers 6 questions in a row correctly, a bonus of 2 is scored.  The game ends when one player scores in all 6 rows.  Highest score wins.

There are three variants that can be played.

Game 1, as individuals, players take it in turns to be the question master for one card each.  Highest score wins.

Game 2, as individuals, each player rolls a die and is asked the question on the card by the player to the right.  Play passes to the right.  Highest score after an agreed number of rounds wins.

Game 3, in two equal teams, each player in the team will receive a question and decide whether to answer individually, (scoring two points,) or to confer, (scoring one point.)  The team with the highest score after an agreed number of rounds wins.

The teachers game consists of four different, all slightly silly, but entertaining games, based around school room subjects.  The aim of the game is to score more points on your School Report Card than any other pupil.'

English questions involve completing a phrase using an adjective beginning with a particular letter without repeating a word already used or hesitating for more than 10 seconds.

Geography questions involve naming a country, town, river, or state that begins with the last letter of the name of the country on the question card.

Maths questions involve arriving at a given number using four other numbers also given and multiplying, adding, subtracting, or dividing.

Art questions involve choosing a word from a card and communicating the word by drawing on a pad.

A very easy game about the Battle of Britain, included in the central pages of "The Battle of Britain Project Book" printed for the Royal Air Force Museum in the 50th anniversary of the event. On a squared map representing Northern France, the Channel and Southern England, the German player moves 10 bombers and 15 fighters against the 25 fighters of the RAF. Aim of the German is to destroy all the 11 British airfields and then do a successful raid against London.

The players are farm owners in the rural United States of the 1960s.  By growing crowing crops, raising cattle, selling grain, and efficiently managing resources, farm owners strive to have the wealthiest (or least in debt) farm at the end of three years.

The game is presented as a farm simulator, designed to help farmers learn more about the business of farming and train them to make successful business decisions.  Many of the instructions are actually advice that could be applied towards running a real farm, and the box cover advertises, "Test Farm Plans, Teach Better Management, Learn Farming Methods".
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Players take turns to pick a card, and play continues clockwise.  One of the three words is chosen by the active player who reads it out.  All write down an ingenious, unlikely or just plain silly definition.

The active player reads out all the definitions including the correct one.  All other players decide what they think is the correct definition.  Each player who chooses the correct definition scores a point.  Authors of fictional definitions that are chosen also receive a point for each player who chose it.

The winner is the player with the most points after twenty rounds.

Your opponent has placed a treasure under the pictures. Your job is to find out where it is!
 By making clever questions you get clues where to find the treasure. But it's not so simple the opponent can only answer yes or no to your questions and the amount of questions is also limited.

Similar to Secret Square with a different player count and slightly different rules.

The Flemish have beaten the French in a battle that took place on 11 July 1302 in the neighborhood of Kortrijk (Belgium). Groeninghe Velt brings you back to the time of crossbows, cavalry and man to man fights.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
A rally through Europe. Each player drives his car from Geneva to Paris. On their trip, players need to answer questions on car technique. When questions are answered incorrectly, their car is broke and needs help from the road-service cars.

Educational game on car technique

boardgame: Column, Line, and Square: Battle Manual
description:
Column Line And Square (CLS) is a game of war set in the period of Napoleon (1808-1815). It was written by "The 30mm Midwestern Napoleonic Wargamers Confederation." Most of the research and rule-writing was by Fred Vietmeyer, with additional input by Dave Mort and Hugh Reynolds.

CLS-II (aka the second edition) was photocopied typed text, about 100 pages. In addition, and almost mandatory at our games, was the addendum, the "3R" (Reviewed Referee Rulings) which documented the 'official' resolution of most rules disputes. There were also 'Optional Rules', 'Potential, Optional Rules', and 'Local Optional Rules'. I've got two three-ring binders given over to the rules and add-ons (and I quit keeping the add-ons long before we broke up).

CLS-III, also called The Battle Book, was a commercially available distillation of the above into about 100 pages. I can't remember who published it - it's out of print and unavailable. Fred still holds the copyright, but I doubt that it will ever see the light of day again.

I played CLS for about 20 years, from the mid '60s to the mid '80s; we broke up as a group in 1987 or 1988.

The rule set is a grand tactical set, using a 20 to 1 ratio (roughly) based on roster and unit strengths in the Spanish battles. The normal game was for 7 turns, using map marking and simultaneous movement. The typical command for a player would be a regiment of cavalry, two regiments of infantry, and a battery - about 220 to 280 castings. The basic map and maneuver units were the battalion and the squadron, with light infantry as an exception, run as either a formed battalion or as individual platoons of skirmishers (two, three, or four castings to the platoon, depending upon nationality).

--description from http://theminiaturespage.com/rules/nap/cls.html

Typical Q&A card game for children.

From the box:

"Ever wanted to be an evil genius, but lacked that degree in nuclear warping from a shady South American university?  Now is your chance!  Gather your body parts, chemicals and duct tape, stick your brain in a jar, and get to work with your deformed helper on that mind control beam you've wanted since high school gym class!"

Ballbuster: A Game of Skill for Two Players

Object: The first player to form a row of six of his/her marbles is the winner.

A fast-paced game of skill and chance for two players.

Ballbuster features an original red plastic 3 dimensional game-board with special chutes to transport marbles between players.

Players begin the game with a set of marbles and a pair of dice. Rolling the dice in turn, players place marbles strategically to either make a row or block the other player.

The game can be won in three rolls of the dice, or it can last up to 20 minutes.

Goldmine Trails - A Wood Building Game

"The Fun of a game... The challenge of a construction kit... The reward of accomplishment... All in one set."

Players roll the die, then add, subtract or move the wooden trail-building pieces accordingly.  The object is to construct a trail on each turn so that the gold rolls successfully from the entrance of the trail to the red miners' pan at the end of the trail.  The first player to collect 10 gold pieces wins!

Like all the Battle Box series, the American Civil War is an entry level game designed to bridge the gap between board gaming and miniature gaming for the young general in training.

The game comes with a game board, 20 detailed playing figures, dice, one page rules, and turn track/marker.  It is a straight forward game with simple mechanics that allow quick and amusing play anywhere anytime.

Players start with $1,000,000 to buy Condominiums at exorbitant prices.  Players attempt to buy the properties offering the highest return on their investment, and to own an entire block in order to double their rental income.

Players may gamble at the racetrack.  They receive money each time they pass the employment office.  They pay a fine or collect a bonus each time they pass the management office or the boardroom.  They hit the jackpot when they land in the Country Club.

The game involves luck and management of finances.

Published in Scarborough, Ontario, Canada.

Based on the book by John Bunyan, players move through life dodging sins and other dangers, like the Castle of Giant Despair, while trying to make it across the River of Death to the Celestial City.

The King of Cash is the first player to buy the "big six" assets: house, car, boat, furniture, audio visual equipment and clothing.

Try your luck and win cash by playing "rawlette", "play & win" and "dice & win".

Waddingtons Word of Mouth: A quick-witted word game of imagination and bluff.

Word of Mouth is a modern variation of an old game in which players select obscure words from the dictionary and try to make up bluff definitions.  In Word of Mouth, the fun has been enhanced as players must place their best on the one definition they feel fits the "official" definition.  The first player to fill his/her home base with betting chips wins, so that the name of the game is to come up with the most believable sounding definition, be it fact of fiction.

The beauty of this game is that it isn't necessarily for high-brows.  If a player's vocabulary knowledge is shaky but his imagination and bluffing skills are tops, he could easily win the game by persuading the other players to pass their betting chips his way.

boardgame: Our Town Sparkles / Social Responsibility Game
description:
These are a couple of games, made in Singapore, which come together in the same box. The objectives of these two games are to inculcate good social behaviors and habits.

The eponymous Our Town Sparkles is a simple roll and move boardgame. The first player to get to the end of the track by exact count wins. The Social Responsibility Game has a circular track (in the sense that you go round and round the track) and players follow instructions on the board where their pieces land. There are Social Responsibility Quotient (SRQ) cards worth up to 20 SRQ points. Players can also earn and lose money depending on their social behavior. There are red penalty cards, drawn when a player lands on a 'Penalty Square', and green cards that a player picks up after landing on a 'Good Social Habit Square'. The game ends when a player runs out of money, or there are no more SRQ point cards. The player with the most SRQ points wins. A curious feature of the game is the role of Social Responsibility Judege (SRJ) - the SRJ administers all penalties, rewards or awards.

Graphics and illustrations by Rolling Art Pte Ltd.
The year of publication is based on a discount coupon (for Popular Bookstore) that came with the game and was valid only till October 2005.

A version of this game, called Tampines Our Pride, combining the Social Responsibility Game and Tampines Our Pride Game (a roll-and-move game, like the Our Town Sparkles Game) was created for the Tampines Town Council. The differences are in the roll-and-move game (different track and events) and the Social Responsibility Game has some slightly different artwork - for example, there's a picture of the Tampines Changkat Community Club on the board. The board itself is made from cardboard and not as thick as the mounted board used in Our Town Sparkles.

1,000 Drinking Games combines rounds of classic and new drinking games with off-the-wall plot twists and a variety of drink assignments. If the other players are satisfied that the cards are in the correct sequence the player may take an additional card.
The first player to correctly sequence six cards is the winner.

From the Manufacturer:

It’s 1943, and the only way for the Allies to carry the war to the Germans is from the air.  But strategic bombing is an experiment;  can they succeed where no one else has, or will the Luftwaffe’s air defenses cause the USAAF to fail, as every other bombing campaign to date has?

This decision-rich, tense Print N’ Play game puts you in charge of either the US bombing campaign or the aerial defense of the Reich.  Not only do you have to select and achieve your military objectives;  you also must maintain enough support for your campaign to keep resources flowing to you.  Will you support ground offensives? Build currently available aircraft or invest in technology? Order your fighters to stick close to the bombers  or sweep the sky for interceptors?  Attack immediately with what’s in the air, or combine your fighter groups?  Defend each target as it’s attacked or wait and launch a huge strike against one raid?   The decisions are all yours.
