description: Rules for combat using bladed weapons. All known blades are listed or will match one listed. This is single figure combat. Most players can handle 4 figures at one time. Great for tournaments.

From Publisher's site

Rules for single figure combat 1780 to 1880. Especially the ACW and Indian Wars. Various weapons are available to choose from. There are rules for a block army version. Fantasy figures for this period are available, especially or the Nappy wars. Army lists are included.

From publishers site.

Take command of one of the five actual subs during the ACW. Each has its own special way of sinking the target. This solo game is quick, fun and habit forming. The example is the record board for your sub. Warning do not order if you hate losing. The chance to win is the same as it was in real life: 25%.
Great game to introduce older kids to gaming.
The Glorious Cause is an introductory game on the American Revolution on a strategic level. It has an area movement map, a one-page set of rules and low number of counters.

Urbs & Civitas introduces the fundamental concepts of street addressing in the urban context all the while demonstrating the importance of its derived benefits: revenue generation, crime reduction, increased commerce & tourism, improved public health & safety, and good governance.  Integrating street addressing as the foundational tool in the urban management process, Urbs & Civitas then progresses by assigning much importance to continued infrastructure development.  Game play emphasizes strong player interaction and management skills by balancing revenue generation versus maintenance costs and promotes the advantages of street addressing through ongoing civic improvement.

From the Mindware site:

Tick, Tock—Quick, Find a Place for That Block!

Put your spatial skills to the test! Fit the correct block on the structure and hit the stop button before time runs out—move too slowly, and the base vibrates, setting off an avalanche of brightly colored pieces. Words, music and lights add urgency and get kids excited to play game after game. Includes 24 blocks, electronic building base with three levels of difficulty, volume control and auto shut-off feature. Requires three AA batteries (not included).

Ages 6 and up

Children's boardgame featuring the star of the Welsh cartoon classic, Ivor the Engine, created by Oliver Postgate. The first person to reach the engine shed wins. Some squares have access to Branch Lines that make you jump ahead or fall behind.

boardgame: Ivor the Engine: The Flower Game
description:
Children's boardgame featuring the stars of the Welsh cartoon classic, Ivor the Engine, created by Oliver Postgate. Pick up three flowers for the flower show and return home to win.

Players may travel around the board in any direction they choose except backward. Land on a flower square to pick up a flower, land on a rectangle to pick up a card, or land on a circle to pick up a penny. Pennies can either modify a roll of the dice or 3 may be used to buy a flower.

Scans of the colourful components featuring elements of Ivor's adventures are available at: 
[url]http://www.smallfilms.co.uk/ivor/games.htm[/url]

Similar to WarGods of Ægyptus, Wargods of Olympus is a fantasy miniatures battles game with a quasi-historical theme. This time the theme is ancient Greece with players building and painting their own miniature armies of Spartans, Titans, Wendigos or other denizens of Greek Mythology.

Slimetrail is a two-player, abstract game played on a rectangular grid (or a rhombus made of hexagons, as in Hex). Each player takes turns moving a piece around the board. The piece can be moved one space, and may not re-visit a visited cell. Two goal cells are located at diagonally opposite corners of the rectangle (or in the obtuse corners of the rhombus). You win if either player moves the piece into your goal cell.

The rules at Game Cabinet state that "the piece starts on any mutually agreed upon square". But since this is a finite game, with enough computing power it can be determined which player has the winning strategy depending on the initial placement, so in principle no cell would be mutually agreeable. In practice, the Pie Rule can be used.

Space aliens captured and cloned a janitor named Herb to play a new game...

Herbs is a fun and simple strategy game where players make their moves simultaneously. It's like a simulated arcade game, which makes players rely more on good thinking than quick reflexes. Success in the game will depend on carefully devising your plans while leaving plenty of room for the unexpected.

Object
Each player will control two Herbs attempting to simply score the most points using 200 Action Points. Points will be scored by eliminating stones or by forming certain scoring patterns with the stones. Destroying an opponent's Herb also scores points.

extracted from the Game Cabinet listing for the rules for this game:

This is a strategy game with simple rules. The complexity of play could be compared to that of checkers.

The game requires a standard checker board and eighteen markers for the game. A set of markers for one player consists of a PIPER and eight creatures. You'll need two sets, one for each player, in different colors. The creatures' markers are two-sided. One side of the marker shows the creature asleep, and the other shows it attacking. Not surprisingly, these creatures are referred to as "SLEEPERS" and "ATTACKERS" depending on which side is up.

The Object
Be the first to move your PIPER across the swamp or to eliminate your opponent's PIPER.

Winning the Game
If a player manages to get his or her PIPER across the swamp to the land where the opponent started, that player wins the game.

A player also wins if he or she eliminates the PIPER belonging to the opponent.

For purposes of winning, it is assumed that both PIPERS could stand on one of the land squares at the same time. In other words, a player can't prevent a win by planting his PIPER on the land and refusing to move it!

Test your knowledge of Coronation Street as you journey to Roy's Rolls, Underworld, the Corner Shop and, of course, the Rovers Return, while watching clips and answering questions about one of television's most beloved soaps.

How much do you remember about Stan and Hilda or Jack and Vera? What about the love triangle between Ken, Deirdre and Mike or the one between Mike, Linda and Mike's son, Mark? How much do you really know about Roy and his transsexual wife Hayley, Tracy's mystery baby or the Weatherfield One? Find out as you compete for chips and trophies in this game that will prove just how "street smart" you really are.

This interactive DVD board game contains more than 700 questions across 6 different categories using clips, stills and story lines from this popular British soap opera.  Question categories include : Families, Workplace, Romance, Rovers, Feuds and Friendship.  Includes 12 Pub Quiz 'Beermats', featuring questions about the characters in the soap.

Procession is an abstract strategy game of trying to move a master piece (queen) across a chess board along routes projected horizontally and vertically from 5 setup pieces (pawns).

Full rules are at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=55

An abstract game where the players place markers on a chess board in sequence, where they can only place makers that are horizontally or vertically in line with their existing marker(s) without interrupt by other markers, and not placed such that they are on any line between a pair of existing markers of the same color.  Players are trying to form a box of their colour, or if they can't, have the longest lines of their color.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=57

This is an abstract strategy game played with dice and a chess board.  You are trying to move your dice across the chess board (in a fixed zig-zag pattern) before your opponent has move their dice across the board in the opposite pattern.  The twist comes that after you have rolled and moved a die (which can be your own OR an opponents), it is placed on the board with the rolled number up, and if you land on a die, you move again based on the number showing, but forwards if it is your opponents die, but backwards if your own.

It has feeling of inspiration from a lot of traditional/classic board/chase games, including (obviously) Ur, but also Backgammon and Parcheesi.

The Enron Game.

This game is a simulation of Enron and other corporate malfeasance type situations.  Players can either roll dice to (honestly) make profits or state a profit level - but at the cost of "audit points" which increase the risk that an audit may reveal the malfeasance.

Full rules and description at: https://web.archive.org/web/20070505132014/http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=36

boardgame: International Grand Prix Magnetic Road Race Game
description:
"Completely self-contained... just remove cover and PLAY.  Magnetic cars stick to special game board."  - From front of box

Spin and move racing game with magnetic cars that stick to the track.

An abstract game played on two chessboards and with stackable reversible counters.  The boards are seen as flip-sides of each other (like sides of a coin), one player starts setup in the center of one side, the other in the center of the other side, with the goal being to have only your markers on the other player's side.  Pieces can move farther and jump more adjacent pieces if they are stacked (up to 3 high).  If pieces are jumped over they are either converted to the other color or eliminated, depending on how many pieces have been jumped over.

Full rules at: https://web.archive.org/web/20070504174755/http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=24

The first chapter is the game. It covers all aspects of naval warfare during the American Civil War. Ramming, mines,
subs are also covered. The game is historical with one slight twist. It was written for what the ships were designed to do, not what they actually did. In this light two very powerful and equal fleets set sail. The players are the difference that decides the outcome.

The other chapters provide historical data to enrich the game. The original game has grown to be a one stop info source for the vessels of the ACW. For those without ship models.
110 different ships are provided on the cards on the CD. 90 vessels are drawn in black and white in Chapter 7. It also contains ACW photos of many of the ships. The game is design for all scales. Only four rules are different for the larger models, mainly distances.

"The Weider Game - it's more than a game!"
An educational fitness game based on dice, trivia, and physical challenges.  Game play is divided into two parts.

Events
The game begins with players trying to win medals in a series of events based mainly on dice rolls.  

    Weight Lifting - up to 3 dice rolls to gain the most points
    Walking - dice roll race with some values given special meanings
    Long Jump - simultaneous distance choice, identical numbers are canceled
    High Jump - dice rolls for numbers higher than an incremental value
    Sprint - dice roll race in turns



Weider Marathon
In the Weider Marathon players roll dice and move along the path.  On trivia squares, players answer educational questions about nutrition, anatomy, and sports.  Surprise squares often have the player trying to complete some physical task; some easy, some not.  Medals are used to fulfill a difficult question or challenge.  A player who retains a medal to the end does not have to finish with an exact dice roll.

This is an abstract game that kind of resembles the old old video game "Snake" -- where you moved your snake around the screen eating blocks, and each block you ate lengthened your tail, making it harder to move.

But, your moving around a set of circular rings, collecting blocks that have different scoring values, in competition with everyone else collecting blocks that will grow your snake, and trying to end at the centre square with the most valuable snake you can get.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=18

Node ball is an abstracted sports game, where you're trying to bounce one of three balls in the game through an opponents goal area.  You have several pieces that can move in varying ways to "kick" the ball, which then roll as far as the kicking piece moved in the opposite direction.  The game continues until all balls have scored, and the player with the most points wins.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=29

Online Play


    Super Duper Games

Played on a 13x13 Go board with go stones and 2 special stones each ("tigers"), the game is mostly about moving stones such that they bump opponents stones, with the hope to have them move past your tigers where they will get eaten.  The winner is first to eat the opponents tiger or eat all the opponents stones, or immobilize the opponents tiger.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=34

Online Play


    Super Duper Games

You're racing giant monsters after a little rabbit -- first to catch the rabbit wins.  Of course, you're a giant monster -- you can attack the other giant monsters, too.  Each monster has custom abilities, and a variable number of actions depending on its "heart rate", which starts at 1, and goes up as things happen.  You select as many dice as you have heart rate for different categories like attack, defense, or movement (simultaneously) then roll and evaluate.  So, the higher the heart rate, the better -- except if your heart rate exceeds 10, you explode and lose the race.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=26

This game is similar to the Flash game Proximity (available many places, for example: http://www.mathsisfun.com/games/proximity.html ).  Players roll a hand of 5 dice, then each in turn place them on a chess board.  If a die is placed adjacent to dice of the same player, those dice are promoted in value.  If placed adjacent to dice of another player, those dice are converted to the placing players color and raised one in value.  Several winning conditions are given, including most points, most area, and other options based on these.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=23

Online Play


    Super Duper Games

Tris combines some of the ideas of Tetris and Connect 4, with the further idea of pushing stones and them falling.  It is played on a chess board, and players alternate either placing stones or moving them.  Place stones can be placed on either edge, or a at the top of a column of stones from an edge.  Stones can be moved one space left or right into an unoccupied space, and if it is unsupported, they "fall" to the bottom.  Four stones in a row of your color are "captured" and removed from the board, the remaining stones "fall", and if more 4-in-a-rows result, this recurses.  Stones in a full row are locked.  Winner is first to capture 12 of their own pieces, or to fill their side of the board with locked rows.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=37

Planecrash is a game played with Magic: The Gathering cards, though it has almost nothing to do with that game.  Players select a deck of cards of various colors (color determined based on M:TG rules), shuffle it, then play from the shuffled deck.  The place a card on one of three piles based on color relationships, with the chance to capture a pile or create a particular combination of colors on top of the three piles.  Creating the correct combination, or collecting the most cards, results in a win.

Full rules at: https://web.archive.org/web/20070504074239/http://www.luchacabra.com:80/?p=30

Cech zlodějů) ("Thieves Guild") is a fast-paced card game where you become a thief among thieves. The "winner" is the player who is most successful in robbing the others. You can't trust anyone here, the police are corrupt and the other well-known companies in town never sleep either. They are constantly watching you and will use every opportunity to get your money back, even with interest. For these cases, it is always useful to have a bribe or a well-guarded safe in your pocket.

In each round, a player starts by buying cards. He can buy one or more cards from the supply pack, for which he pays with his gold. The first card costs one gold, if the player wants more cards, it is always more expensive. Gold used to buy cards creates a bank next to the supply deck. It is empty at the beginning of the game. If the card says "remove" the gold, it means to put it in the bank. After buying the cards, the player goes to loot.

During the Loot phase, a player rolls the dice twice. The first roll determines how many gold he can loot that round. If no opponent reacts to this throw, the player throws a second time. The second roll determines who gets robbed that turn. The number of points rolled is the distance of the opponent to be robbed. The player whose turn it is decides whether to count to the left or to the right. If the roll equals the number of players, the roller, he cannot rob anyone.

The game ends when one player loses all their gold. The player with the most gold wins.

Mirrorwing is a game inspired by Chess but with some interesting twists.  All pieces are, essentially, pawns ("villagers") at the start, but they can be stacked -- at which time they become a "dragonrider", with the movement based on the bottom piece ("dragon") and the attack/capture (which is done without moving to the capture location, unlike Chess) based on the top piece ("rider").  As an added twist, any piece that moves over the center line of the board flips over, reversing the dragon and rider abilities.  Winner is the last player to have an un-enhanced (villager) piece remaining.

Full rules: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=28

This is an abstract strategy game where players are rolling place dice onto a 4x4 chess-board like area.  One player has light dice and the other dark -- and similarly there are light and dark squares on the board.  The player with the highest score in their dice on the opposite toned squares wins.  The trick comes in that the dice are placed with a particular horizontal or vertical orientation, and that when a dice is placed, adjacent dice may be converted if the facing side of the new die is higher in value than the facing side of the original die.  Also, to give a bit more strategic depth, players may store up to 3 dice that they don't like the rolls on -- and may use one of these instead of rolling on any turn.  But, they must use all the stored dice by game end.

Pomota -- for POstMOdern TActics is an abstract set of miniatures-like rules, using a chessboard as the playing field and (usually, though not required) chess pieces as the miniatures.  It provides a set of rules for movement, combat, knock-out and elimination, and a points system for both evaluating wins and using the variable and specialized powers of the pieces.

Two possible "armies" are supplied, but it is clear that the idea is that this could be quite extensible.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=33

This is an abstract game played with a chess board and Icehouse Pieces where the object is to get your pieces to the opposite corner of the board as quickly as possible.  It has some of the feel of Chinese Checkers, but with the added complications of the possibility of your opponent being able to immobilize your pieces, and you being able to carry your immobilized pieces, and movement rates and jumping restrictions based on the size of the pieces involved.

Plastic Glory is, essentially, a simple miniatures rules system -- except it isn't for miniatures.  You're fighting with plastic toy soldiers, they're marching across the tabletop, climbing over books, ducking around a Cheetos bag, etc.  They move in inches, real inches -- not scale to something.  They have capabilities based on the posture of the plastic figure -- standing, crouching, or prone.

Full rules at: https://web.archive.org/web/20070504014109/http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=41

Coatl is a race to get your pawns to the center of a spiral before your opponent(s), by rolling better.  But, there are a couple twists -- the simple one is that landing on an opponent captures it, sending it back to the start.  But, landing on your own piece allows you to combine them at which point they get enhanced powers, with a choice of what power to take.  This combination is where the strategy comes in.

Each player has a business card, in fact it should be a USA business card since zip-codes are mentioned in the rules.  Each player will select some dice, and roll them -- trying to get sets.  They can augment their sets by crossing out numbers on their business card, and they damage other players by knocking numbers on their opponents business cards.  You're KOed if you have no numbers left, last card "standing" wins.

Full rules at: http://www.luchacabra.com/?p=40
Join Baloo and all his pals on a high-flying adventure.  Collect the most cargo before reaching Higher for Hire.

Do you know what Canada's motto is? Or how long it takes for the tide to go out at the Bay of Fundy? Where did Percé Rock get its name? What is the provincial flower of Alberta?

These are all questions that you will find the answers to in the game TRAILBLAZER. This game is produced by the Trans Canada Trail Foundation in order to raise funds for the world's longest recreational trail.

TRAILBLAZER is a four player board game that is a great way for families to spend time together. The questions are trivia based and each question card has five questions. The player chooses the difficulty level (1-5) and then the question is read. Points are given according to the difficulty of the question. There are a wide variety of topics covered in the game such as geography, history, flora, fauna, and transportation. There are also adventure and treasure cards.

description:
This small game deals with the Soviet attack to liberate Kiev, Ukraine in November-December 1943 and the subsequent counterattack by Balck's 48th Panzerkorps (1st, 7th, 19th, 25th, 1st SS Leibstandarte, 2nd SS Das Reich panzerdivisions and the 68th infantry division) against the 3rd Guard Tank army of Rybalko, the 38th and 60th armies, on Zhitomir, Korosten and the Malin pocket. After a quick Soviet initial advance overwhelmed the German defenders and liberated Kiev, the German counterattack with fresh reinforcements coming from other theaters of operations, destroyed several Soviet units, but could not regain much of the lost territory and was too weak to reach Kiev.

11 X 17 inches map,  64 counters, divisional level for the Germans plus the 506th Tiger battalion and corps level for the Russians.

The board comprises five linked holes and each of two players has two counters.  In quick-fire fashion, each player is allowed to move one of their counters to the vacant hole along the available grid lines - if they can't, they've lost - no jumping allowed.

The puzzle can actually be folded over to provide a pocket-size block (containing the 4 pegs) for travel / storage.

Guide a species from the earliest beginnings to the height of the evolutionary ladder.

Brief Description:

Players begin with their animal only having a single body part, such as a torso, and must evolve to gain additional appendages and abilities.

Each species has a set of stats that will increase as the game progresses and they gain new abilities (such as Predator).

Each player begins with a single adult of their species. You have a limited number of actions to split between hunting for food, birthing new offspring, changing the current environment, or claiming an effect tile which will give their species a new ability once they can afford to put the tile into play. 
  
If a player's species is strong enough they can also attack one of the other players and try to slow their progress.

Whichever species has evolved the most by the end of the game wins.

Terrain affects combat through additions to or subtractions from the attack or defense factor. The Combat Results Table is bloody enough for a battle that was fought for the most part without the fiddly flanking moves that produce ZOC-kills.  The Norman arrow supply and Saxon Hand-held missiles rules add flavor to a bloody shoving match.

As a pirate, it is your objective to capture enemy or neutral vessels and islands, thus obtaining gold to outfit or improve your own ship. The ultimate goal is to conquer the "Island of Pirates", which gives this game its name.

To achieve the goal, the players can explore the seas surrounding the fabled island, always having at least one watchful eye of the look out at the opponents.

At the beginning, every ship is outfitted with five cannons of a given firepower. Both numbers can be increased using upgrades. Chronology Junior: A Card Game for All Time

Players build a time line of cards. Each card contains a event and the year in which it occurred. Players begin with one card each, which is placed face up in front of them. On her turn, a player is read an event from a new card; the player must then indicate the position on her own time line where the card should be placed. If she's correct she places the card in her own time line; if not, the next player gets an opportunity to win the card. The first player with seven cards wins.

Cards from other Chronology games can be added to increase the number of combinations and the level of challenge.

description:
Swarm is a turn based abstract strategy game where opponents strive to take over the Hive, or board by eliminating other swarms.  Game pieces move across a number of hexagonal spaces determined by the number of pieces in the stack (called a swarm), and stack and unstack upon landing on specific spaces.
Opponents are eliminated by landing on their spaces, or "stinging" them.  2-player, 3-player, and 6-player boards are currently available.  There are many board variations, but only three are currently in print.  There are also a variety of spaces which have different properties to enhance game strategy.

"Watch the mystery ball magically select the correct answer. Over 100 questions on history, sports, science, entertainment and the world around us. Scoring board for competition play."

A two-player trivia game in which the object is to be the first player to answer ten questions correctly.

The game consists of a plastic board with an oval area to place the question/answer sheets and two scoring boards which are used to track how many correct answers each player has given.

The "mystery ball" is clear plastic dome mounted on a black base. At the edge of the base is a round hole or window which allows one to look through the base and onto the question/answer sheet. The mystery ball is placed on the question side of the board and turned to reveal one of the eight questions through the window in the base. The player then chooses an answer from among the eight answers. The mystery ball is then moved to the answer side of the board and it automatically (and mysteriously) turns to reveal the correct answer through the window in the base.

The question/answer sheets are double sided, providing eight questions in various categories, such as Space and Space Travel, Popular Music, Movies, Science and Inventors, Astrological Pairs, The World's Mostest, Where in the World, General Knowledge, Facts on Wheels, The Arts, Sports, History, Geography, Legends and Myths, and Who Is.

Taken from the Instructions:

The object of Flying Finnegan is to set up the game so that the Tank opens and "Flying Finnegan" lands in the Water Tank. To win the game, you must be the first one to do this four times.

Push the button
If you have set everything just right, the Flag Pole will raise, Flying Freddie will climb down the Flag Pole and release the marble which will roll down and hit the Tank Latch which will open the Tank. The five marbles will roll into the Marble Catch.

At the same time, the "POW" Flag will have hit the Flying Finnegan Sign causing Flying Flo and Flying Joe to whirl and twirl down the Spiral and hit the Boom Hammer which knocks the Marble into the bucket thereby moving the Long Hammer which releases, yes, Flying Finnegan!!! who will be launched through the air into....the Water Tank which will close with a ring of the bell!!!

A big vacuum cleaner has sent lots of monsters in the cosmos. You must gather them in families in order to score points.
During the game, cards are drawn to form two columns. The cards can have monsters on them, or various bonuses/penalties. During your turn you can choose to take a whole column in your hand, but you can only do that four times. You then must draw two new cards to extend the columns.
The columns will grow during play until someone picks one up and starts a new one.

At the end of the game, you must arrange the cards you picked up in order to have majorities for each monster type. If you do you will score points, if you don't you will loose points.

from Game Cabinet rules:

Use the same board and pieces as for International Chess (also known as English Chess or Western Chess).
Start with an empty board.

One side starts first on any square, after which play continues in turn. A player cannot pass his turn.

A legal move consists of one of the following:

placing one's own piece on an unoccupied square. The color of the square is immaterial. 
moving a piece as per the International Chess rules with the exception of pawns. A pawn piece moves and captures like a King piece, that is one step in any direction. No promotion when a pawn reaches an edge of the board. 
capturing an opponent's piece by moving one's own piece to that square. 
The side who first captures all the opponent's pieces wins the game. If neither side can capture all the other side's pieces, and both sides agree to a draw then it is a draw.

Other possible variations: The size of the board, the number or types of pieces can be different from the International Chess set. To play handicap games, players can take unequal number of pieces or can start with initial placement of pieces such as pawns.

A domino game with letters and pictures. You play usually like dominoes but you can also : put a letter on a picture if you can spell a word beginning with this letter in the domain of the picture. For example, after a picture of a town, you can place a "T" as tower, a "S" as street, etc. But you can't place "P" for Paris or "S" for Santiago. You can only use words about the subject of the picture.

There are also attacks and parades.

Content: 2 x 72 dominoes, 2 bags, 5 supports, 1 rule.

description: Shambattle is a old fashioned toy soldier battle game. It is the first miniature game published in the United States. It is played on hand drawn maps with toy soldiers and is intended to be played by children.

Lode Runner is based on the old Broderbund Software computer game of the same name (Lode Runner (1983)), and was in fact designed by a Broderbund employee.

The game contains eight different game boards of varying difficulty level - one of these is used for 2-4 players and two are recommended for 5-6 players, although the rulebook also shows configurations of four boards. ​Each player controls a runner and a guard and tries to have their runner collect as much gold as possible while eluding the other players' guards. Runners can dig much as in the computer game using hole tiles; players start with a few tiles and acquire more when they pick up gold. Guards attack other players' runners.

Once all gold has been collected the runners escape from the top of the playing area, with additional gold awarded to the first three to exit.

Chosen is a Bible-based trading card game for kids, adults and families. Chosen is fun to play and an educational Bible game. Collect your favorite Bible characters' cards, read their stories and play against your friends to see whether you are a Chosen player!

Chosen has three different rule levels so that beginners and casual players can quickly and easily learn how to play this Bible game, while more advanced players can challenge themselves with the advanced rules.  The design of the Chosen cards and rules allows players to use the same cards in an easy to play beginners Bible game, when using beginner rules, or in an intellectually challenging  adventure Bible game when using expert rules.  Collect and trade cards with friends to build a strong deck and to collect your favorite Bible characters.
This game is a Hungarian variant of Clue.
A Hungarian clone of Cluedo.

Each team plays in turn, proceeding in a clockwise direction. One player from the team takes a card from the box & throws the two dice. They then have one minute to communicate a category printed on the card to the other members of their team, by drawing or miming different things in that category until a member of the team guesses the category.

It is important to remember that players are trying to communicate a category, rather than specific things. For this reason, it is worth waiting for the player drawing or miming to draw or mime two things or more to make sure of the category being communicated. For instance, if a player draws a glass full of drink, they may be trying to communicate 'Drinks! - but if they then draw an ice cream, it is more likely to be Cold Things !

Pictionary with a twist - great party fun !

This is yet another Monopoly clone. The publisher Lokaletspelet I Skandinavien AB did a lot of this games as promotions for different cities in the 1980s.

description: A simple fast play rules set for the Napoleonic era based upon the very popular De Bellis Antiquitatis (DBA). In its most basic format, about a dozen units on each side contest across a 2 foot by 2 foot. While bells & whistles are held to a minimum enough Napoleonic chrome exists to give this game a interesting flavor of the time --- Vistula Lancers, British rifles, Spanish guerrillas are differentiated as is the very slow Austrian army. Includes very usable army lists. KISR is following this up with sets for the American Civil War (DBACW) & WWII (Rommel's Battles). description:

Be the big winner in this Stone Age race! Answer questions by matching dividens and divisors through 9's. Watch out for the low-flying pterodactyls

Prehistoric Times:
Race a dinosaur through a prehistoric obstacle course! Dodge tar pits, volcanoes, and saber-toothed tigers. As you play you will sharpen your multiplication skills through 9's

Step up to the pat and hit a home run! Round numbers to the nearest tens, hundreds, and thousands. Spin the spinner to get on base and score points. Everyone can win!

"Can you achieve high inflation, huge unemployment, gigantic debt, and lead the country to the brink of disaster? Great! You're Top Banana!"
In Banana Republic, players attempt to outdo each other in controlling banana republics. The object is twofold: 1. to create the worst governed nation, and 2. become the richest player (in bananas!).
The entire game is themed around everything related to bananas. Players moving around the banana-shaped board may take "banana chance" cards (gaining or losing bananas), answer banana-related trivia questions, choose 'Economy Cards' and bet bananas on their chance of having the worst economic performance in a chosen area (such as inflation, unemployment, etc), bet bananas on having the worst banana republican leader (eg worst Treasurer, worst General) by allotted scores (in order to collect a set of government cards), land on a 5-banana "Bananza", lose bananas in the banana bank, and withdraw other players' bananas from the bank.
The winner (Top Banana) is the player who banana bankrupts all other players.

NB: Although the playing time is stated as 10 minutes, it can often last several hours.

Nike and Cooper divided their games between two main categories, "wargames" and "thematics". This was one of the first games published by them in the latter one, along with "La fuga de Colditz" or "Asalto al banco de Inglaterra".
Set in the Cold War era, players enter East Berlin in order to find a personality who wishes to pass to the other part of the Wall. They have to find out who he is while evading military police and other hazards. Besides, one of the players is really a mole working for the Russians...
The game is inspired by a tale by writer Arthur W. Landsbury.

boardgame: Blow by Blow: Pakistan Invades India 1965
description:
This is a wargame about the pivotal battle of Assal Uttar 1965 which was one of the largest tank battles after World war two.

The Pakistanis launched an offensive to cut off the supplies to the city of Amritsar in the Punjab which was vital to tilting the war in Pakistan's favor.

The Pakistani offensive was spearheaded by its Elite 1st Armored division comprised of M47 and M48 Patton and M24 Chafee tanks.

The Indian defenders had the 4th Mountain Division backed up by Sherman and AMX tanks but had the advantage of plentiful recoilless rifles, defending in poor terrain(sugar cane fields and rice paddies) as well as reinforcements in the form of the Indian 2nd armored brigade composed of Centurion tanks.

The battle was pivotal and led to the loss of many Pakistani tanks.

The area of the battle is referred to as Patton Nagar which means Patton city due to the large number of Patton tanks captured and destroyed during the battle.

Contains:
• One 11" x 17" map
• Set of 280 ½" playing pieces representing ground combat units.
• One rulebook
• Charts and tables
Subtitled Lights Up Your Party, based on a 1950's tv show, players answer questions by connecting electric leads. When the answer is correct, a light turns on.

Speedy Gonzales is an original race game, where chance has a rather large part, but also tactics are important. The game is played in two phases. In the first phase, the players set their water-gourds - small tokens - along the route. In the second phase, the race itself, each player on his or her turn is confronted with a difficult choice -- either drink one or more water-gourds, and then roll a die for as many gourds drunk (with distribution - 0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4) and advance the number indicated, or advance one space without using a water-gourd. Players can collect water-gourds by landing on spaces with them, but cannot carry more than three at the same time. For those not far-sighted enough to plan for the future, some wells are available along the way.

Monopoly-ish board game loosely based on the investment strategies of the Rochester Fund of New York. Play as either a fund manager or a politician. Buy municipal bonds, build, and collect tax-free income.

description: Travel around Broome County, New York in this roll-and-move family game. Comes with a six part game board, instructions, dice, 11 game pieces, money, 48 "surprise" cards, 120 travel cards, 60 coupons, and 12 shopping lists.

During the 2 billion year time period before the evolution of man, many different alien species, races, and entities have made Earth their home. Of these, there are six groups who stand out as being the most numerous and most powerful. These groups of colonizers include:
1. The Elder Things
2. Cthulhu and his Spawn 
3. The Flying Polyps
4. The Great Race of Yith
5. Mi-go, the Fungi from Yuggoth
6. The Serpent People
All of these races fought each other for dominance on the planet. Often they would summon beings from other places, times, and dimensions to help them fight their Wars.
Elder Things and Outer Gods is a card and dice game where the various races (players) try to eliminate each other from existence. A free copy of the rules is available at:

Ump-Rite is not a statistical replay.

One player rolls two six-sided dice. The dice are read as a two digit number with the lower number being the first digit.

One player checks for a result on the page which relates to the current on base situation. However, a player can challenge the result by looking in a second book of tables called the Blue Book.

For 2 or more players. Ages 4 - 8

In ACW Solitaire, the player plays the Union forces in the American Civil War of 1861-65. The aim is to defeat a simulated Confederate opponent using a combination of strategies.

Units include named Generals, Armies, Forts, River Gunboats, Naval Fleets and different Indian Tribes. The map is province based, including ports and the Mississippi river.

Union victory depends on a series of objectives being achieved, such as cutting the Mississippi and an effective Naval Blockade. It is unlikely the Confederates will win, but it is possible

The game has been intensively play tested and should take about an hour to play to completion - offering an exciting and stimulating experience of directing the strategy of the civil war.

There are a series of optional rules to allow players to investigate different aspects of the war, including a 2 player version.

The game is a "print and play" game available for US$5 from wargamedownloads.com

In The Game of Triangles, tiles are played so the numbers on each side match the numbers on any adjoining tiles.

Players begin their turn by declaring what number they will play on.  They continue to place the triangular tiles until they have no remaining tiles with that number that can be placed.  Tiles are then drawn to bring the total back up to 5.  Scoring is based on the numbers on the placed tile, with multipliers for placements involving two or three adjoining sides.

description:
Masterpieces - The Family game created by children.

In Masterpieces players attempt to guess what young children have drawn.
The game consists of 560 cards. The face of each card displays a framed drawing - the vast majority of the drawings have been drawn by children under the age of eight. The reverse shows the value of the drawing, the answer, name and age of the artist. A small quantity of the cards are "Forgeries" which have been drawn by famous adults.
The first player (or team) to collect £100,000,000 worth of "Masterpieces" (by correctly identifying the subject of the drawings) wins the game!
A trivia game based on Colorado.  Categories include 'Personalities', 'History', 'On the Rocks', and more.

Up A Gum Tree - A game for monkeys!
Throw the dice and move your monkey up the tree. Make use of the creepers, but watch out for the snakes! First to get their monkeys to the top wins the game.

This game is essentially a 3D 'Snakes and Ladders' with a number of subtle changes; each player has two monkeys, both of which must reach the top of the tree to win, each player's section of the tree has two creepers (which are the 'ladders'), but most importantly the pair of snakes on your section of the tree can be moved! Each player races his own pair of monkeys up the tree whilst attempting to avoid the two patrolling snakes, should a monkey and a snake end up on the same space, the monkey falls and has to start the climb over again. On your turn you can roll the dice and either move one of your own monkeys, or any snake (either to clear a route for your own monkeys or attempt to knock an opponents monkey back to the start).

Animal Journey -  A rescue Adventure Game-travel the world with animal heroes and help save endangered animals. Animal Journey is a memory Adventure game that helps children develop matching skills, learn animal habitats and names, develop social skills, by following the rules and taking turns. No reading or numbers involved. For 2-4 players, Ages 5 and up

Children's game published by Whitman based on a Disney story featuring Donald Duck, Mickey Mouse, Goofy, and Pluto.

Each player starts with six matching pieces laid face up in front of them.  A pool of path tiles, plus Escape and Lost playing pieces are placed face down in the center.  In turn, each player draws a tile from the pool.  The first tile must be a path tile and is placed face up in the center of the table.  After that, drawn path cards must be connected to a path card already played.

If the Escape tile is turned up, the player instead places one of their character cards on the path.  They are played as a path card but become a dead end.  The Escape tile is returned to the pool and mixed in after use.

If the Lost tile is turned up, the player instead removes one of their character cards on the path (if they have one) and returns the tile to the pool after use.

The first player to get rid of their six pieces wins.  If all paths are blocked before this happens, the player with the most characters used wins.

Contents:
34 Playing Pieces
66 Path Cards

1833NE (called 1834 in prototype) is an 18xx game set in the Northeastern United States.  The scale is significantly smaller than many 18xx games and depicts regional railroading, where constantly changing outside forces buffet the players. 1833NE includes both standard and takeover versions.

The standard game is most like 1846 (and therefore has a linear stock market, multiple types of trains available at each phase, and paying for most track lays), but adds ferries, small cities, and interconnection bonuses, as well as using an auction -- not a draft -- to distribute the private companies.

The takeover game adds 5 variable start railroads and takeovers, by which one railroad can acquire another one, increasing its train limit (which is reduced from the standard game).

As a bonus, 1833NE includes the 1846 mini-expansion (included in second edition 1846) for first edition 1846 owners.

Board game based on the Little Colonel series of books and the movie starring Shirley Temple. Comes with 4 playing pieces, board, 8 figures with metal stands, dice, a shaker for the dice, and instructions printed on the inside of the box.

This is a two player abstract strategy game that includes a unique custodian trap/freeze move. In Crossing Stonehenge, two druids and their armies, battle to cross from one side of Stonehenge to the other.

The game was initially made as a set of rules for the game Stonehenge.

So ein Eiertanz!is a simple card laying game, where players have to place eggs and worms in line of sight of their chicken, in order to gain points. Various special cards (scrambled eggs, lakes,...) can be used to either destroy eggs of the opponents or block line of sight. More cards included in the set (e.g. easter eggs) can be added to the basic card set for more interesting options during gameplay.

Subtitled: *The Far Out Strategy Game*
"Blast Off Through the Orbiting Track to a Galaxy of Fun in this Exciting Space Race Game"

Published 1977 by Israel's Orda Industries, Orbito features an interesting board with a rotating ring, a bunch of stumps to hold the spaceship pegs, and 4 colored 'space station' areas in each of the 4 corners. Additional components are plastic 'spaceships', 4 each of 4 colors, and a 6-sided die.

Players attempt to move all their spaceships from his or her space station, through the launching track, and across the board to the diagonally opposite station.  Players take turns rolling a 6-sided die then rotating the center ring and moving 1 piece the number shown on the die.  The first player to get all his/her ships to the other side wins.
  
Presumed to be out of print.
The players take their ducklings for a swim. During their outing, they dive to collect water plants. The lucky duck that collects the most water plants wins the game.

Wolkenkinder is a dice game for children three and up.  The players assume the role of children, who live up in the clouds, called the wolkenkinder.  The wolkenkinder like to play their favorite new game- dropping raindrops into a rain barrel.  The game is made more challenging by the umbrellas that block the holes in the rain barrel.  The first wolkenkinder to drop all their raindrops into the rain barrel wins the game.

You are a Night-club DJ under orders from the Club Manager to keep the clubbers happy. The Club has many rooms (one per player), each playing a different genre of music. Each player has a board that depicts their ‘room’, which is split into 3 main areas; Bar Area, Dancefloor and Stage. Also on a player’s board are 2 record decks on either side of a sound mixer.

The manager will visit your room from time to time and you will receive points for clubbers on the Dancefloor and for those on the Stage.
At the end of the night the DJ with the most points is the winner.

Be the first player or team to collect 6 Bible coins by answering questions correctly. Includes 500 cards with 2000 questions.

The box can easily be mistaken for a box of chocolates. Which is of course where the name of the game pitches in. 
During the game the cards allow you to choose from 6 different options: to give definitions of difficult words, to judge over historical facts, to complete proverbs, to act out a word, to solve riddles, thereby going through several steps, and to improvise a story. 
The game is good for about half an hour of acting, tale telling and guessing.

Help Jimmy Gourd find his cheeseburger, Bob the Tomato retrieve his hat, Larry the Cucumber fetch his duck, or Junior Asparagus search for his ball.  Remember where the 4 items for each character are on the game board. The first player to collect all 4 of their character's items and return to the center of the board, WINS!

Simple roll and move game from IDEAL. The Nurses asks you to follow the hectic careers of nurses Liz Thorpe and Gail Lucas.  This game comes from the television show of the same name that was on during the 1960's which featured Shirl Conway as Liz Thorpe and Zina Bethune as Gail Lucas.

Rex Morgan MD Game is from the Sunday Funnies Series of games put out by Ideal in 1972. The game is for 2 to 4 players Ages 7 to 14. Object is to roll the dice and then "Race around to emergencies with America's favorite doctor".

Kate Smith, the famous singer and radio star from the 1940's (she also sang "God Bless America" at Philadelphia Flyer Hockey Games during the 1970's), is on the cover of this war-time game that reeks of patriotism. It was produced by Toy Creations Inc. of New York, and a signed dedication inside the box lid promised to donate all royalties to charity.

The game comes with 144 thick cardboard tiles: 48 states divided into four color-coded regions; 48 state capitols; 30 primary industries; 10 "favorite sons"; and 8 American flags (wild cards). There are four cardboard racks for holding each player's tiles (neatly designed to create red-and-white stripes when laid sideways in the box next to the blue-starred covered rules).

The tiles are shuffled face down on the table between the players. Each player draws a hand of 12 tiles to start. Each in turn then draws a tile and then discards a tile (the same or another). As in mah-jong, the player must announce the discard. The next player may pick up either the discard or a blind tile, but any player may claim the discard if it completes a partial set. He must reveal enough tiles to prove the set (e.g. two other states of the same color), thus revealing part of his strategy, but play then passes to the seizing player's left, with any in between missing their turns.

There are eight potential scoring combinations. The easiest, scoring 5, is a hand with three states in each of the four regions. The most difficult, scoring 30, requires three states each with their capitols, one of their industries and a favorite son. (Since some industries have only a single tile, and only 10 states have favorite sons, this can be very difficult indeed.) However, the 8 American flag tiles can be used as wild cards.

If a player needs information (such as a state's favorite son or industry), he may ask questions out loud, and pays 5 into a bonus pot for each question. As soon as a player completes a winning hand, he calls "America" and collects the value of the hand from each player as well as anything in the bonus pot. Cardboard discs in four colors are supplied, with their values to be assigned by the players.

This expansion for Memoir '44 includes eight painted airplanes and the air rules needed to fly them from the Battle of Britain to the Battle of Midway, from the frigid air of Kursk to the balmy skies of Malta, and deep into Nazi territory.

Also included in the pack is a complete compilation of all 64 official Memoir '44 scenarios published to date, including revisions to many of them - taking advantage of the numerous expansions now available for the game system.

The Air Pack is further rounded off with a complete 120 card deck describing every single terrain, special rules, troop type and nation introduced since the game's launch at the 60-year anniversary of D-Day.

Part of the Memoir '44 series.

It comes with a suitable pirate themed game board and pieces including pirate ships with sails that are knocked off when hit. The game differs from the classic game by adding two special weapons. A game similar to Operation where players attempt to retrieve stolen treasures from a giant without waking him up by using a pair of tweezers and not touching the edges where the treasure is stored.  Ages 6 and up.

EMS Challenge - the game that challenges emergency medical professionals! EMS Challenge is a take on the trivia board game intended to be an enjoyable method of reviewing information pertaining to professionals in the pre-hospital care field.

On your turn you must answer a trivia question from one of four categories: definitions, medical terminology, anatomy and physiology, or miscellaneous. A correct answer entitles you to roll the die and advance.

Special squares mix up play slightly including "pick your category" spaces, "player to the right picks your category" spaces, and time trials which are small tasks with a time limit (i.e. roll a 1 in five seconds) and give small rewards (advance one space)

The game contains 400 question cards, 4 tokens, a die, a game board, and instructions.

The system shares basing and many mechanics with the DBx series.

Square Play is an abstract strategy game played on a board of inset squares.  Each player takes turns placing one of seven tokens in the hope of creating a square with four tokens.  If they fail in that effort, players take turns moving a token to create either a square with four tokens or a straight line with three, whereupon they can remove an opponent's token.

Play continues until either a square is formed or until a player is eliminated due to having only 2 tokens left on the board.

The player then works to overcome various challenges at the carnival, defeat other players, and collect three pieces that will make up the "Ultimate Weapon," which leads to victory.

This game simulates an Italian soccer tournament. The players play each other moving their soccer team markers on the board spaces using a spinner. The spaces of the board represent events occurring in a typical soccer game that give the chance to score a goal: goal kicks, corner kicks, penalties, and so on. The game box includes cards and markers of the 30 best Italian soccer teams of the '70s, goal markers and a goalkeeper figure to be used on penalty kicks.

As the year 2015 came to a close it was clear the world was in a new trend. Instead of fighting wars with their own conventional forces, nations found it easier to pass the burden of conflict along to private contractors. They could be expensive, but a mercenary force was less politically liable than a country and more expendable. The best survived, and went on to glory and profit. The most famous and sought after are the mercenary pilots.

The leader of a Mercenary Air Squadron has many responsibilities. The fortunes of his or her firm depend on taking the right jobs for his pilots, outfitting with the right equipment, and keeping those birds flying. One month a mercenary squadron could be flying combat operations over the Pacific Ocean for Japan, and turn around and join the highest bidder against their former employer. Nothing is personal, except for rivalries amongst mercenary squadrons, which tend to lead to heated exchanges beyond that specified in a contract. Your prestige and fortune are at stake. Pilots, man your planes and your pocketbooks!

Expanded by:

    Mercenary Air Squadron World War II: Mediterranean Theater of Operations

description:
Miniatures wargaming without the paperwork.

The On the Counter (OtC) system is a framework for creating miniature style wargames without the miniatures, and without the tedious record keeping.  It eliminates the need for status sheets, army rosters and hit location tables by placing everything you need directly on the counter.

The OtC system lets you spend your time thinking about playing the game instead of managing it.  The basic set comes with basic rules, plus counters and special rules for four different genres of combat: Mecha, Starships, World War One Dogfights and World War Two company scale combat.

The On the Counter system is released under a Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial-Share Alike 3.0 License Creative Commons License.

The archive includes a counter template for GIMP that you can use to create your own counters.  Go ahead and make your own genres, scenarios and counters.

boardgame: Wherry: The Elizabethan Thames River Game
description:
(from the rulebook explanation)

Wherry is a game of Watermanship on the Shakespearean Thames.  In the merry but sometimes threatening ambiance of 16th century Elizabethan London, players of this game will re-enact the working lifestyle of a Thames Waterman (Wherryman).  They will pick up passengers in their Wherries, from the various Landing Stairs situated along the riverbank and while rowing them to their destination, they will have to deal with the prevailing conditions of the tide, weather and other river hazards.  They must also try to avoid colliding with other Wherries or with the Royal Barge and must give careful thought to the many chances and choices they will have during their voyaging up and down the river Thames.
From the box, "An amusing game in which players compete to make the l-o-n-g-e-s-t snake."  Ages 3 and up

A leader secretly selects a word then players take turns guessing its letters, yet the game expands upon the familiar Hangman concept.

The leader’s word must contain the letters shown on three cards drawn from a deck containing letters of the alphabet.  A player’s guess must state both a letter and the numerical position within the word.  The leader will declare the guess to be “Right”, “Wrong”, or “Almost”, where the latter indicates a valid letter but not at that position.

Printed score sheets allow players to track the progress of all guesses. Individual players score a point for each “Right” guess, and get another turn.  The leader scores points for all the “Wrong” and “Almost” guesses.  The role of leader passes for multiple rounds.

Open ended number of players, can be executed as a party game.

From the manufacturer:

Little ones will enjoy the Flintstones Stone Age Memory Game! You don't need to be a prehistoric scientist to build your own Stone Age Television, Rock Guitar, or Baby Carriage. Each of the 12 inventions in the game is split into 4 cards. Collect them all and you can keep the invention. Play with your friends to see who can collect the most inventions, or use the special rules to play by yourself. For 1 or more players. Ages 4 - 8.

From the manufacturer:

Calling all Junior Detectives!! Using real detective gadgets, solve a new mystery every time you play The Detective Kit Game. Create a clue card and decode secret code words. Write, and reveal, hidden messages with an invisible ink pen. Search the Ski Shop, Zoo and Aquarium for clues that will verify your hunches. Follow the footprints on the trail and use your magnifying glass to compare matching thumb prints. Collect the correct clues, and then race back to the Detective's office to win the game! For 2 to 4 Players. Ages 8 and up.

Players Race to be the first one to find all three keys in a designated area and bring them back to the electronic treasure chest. The keys are hidden beforehand by an extra non-playing person. When the Chest recognizes that a player has put in all three different keys it will open and play victory music.

Star Fleet Battle Volume II replaces expansions 1, 2 & 3. This boxed set includes revised rules and the standard ship display sheet included with the game are the original design not the later Commander SSDs. It includes 3 solitaire missions, 6 campaigns, and 21 other scenarios.

Four races are included with this expansion: The Hydrans, Andromedans, Lyrans and Wyn Star Cluster.  Also included are the Commanders Rulebook Volume II, 324 die-cut playing pieces and the 32 page SSD booklet containing 150 starship designs.

The weapons introduced in this boxed set are the same as expansions 1, 2, & 3.  Including the F type plasma, Tractor-Repulsor Beams, Gating Phasers, Fusion Beams and others.

This is a dexterity/skill game for children involving the placement of ladybug pieces on the leaves of a palm tree.  The palm tree leaves (fronds?) have various colored circles on them in rows from closest to the trunk to farthest away from the trunk.  The tree leaves have some degree of movement, and if too much weight is placed on them, they will dip or bend down.  Players take turn rolling a dice with various colored sides, and then placing a ladybug piece on a matching colored circle on a leaf.  The goal of the game is to get rid of all your ladybugs, but if your placement causes ladybugs to fall, those pieces become yours.  The game continues until one player is out of ladybugs. The leaves have different lengths and there are three sizes of ladybugs (small, medium and large), so there is some definite strategy about which ladybug gets placed where.  The tree, leaves, and ladybugs are all nicely formed and painted wooden pieces.
Monkey Bowling comes with 6 3" brightly painted wooden monkeys and 1 wooden ball.  A fun game for children (and adults!)  Play is just what you would expect - Set 'em up, knock 'em down!

It's all good, clean fun - at least that's the intention. But you never know what might slip out when you're playing Innuendo. If someone starts talking about things that sometimes don't rise, just what are they referring to ? Yorkshire puddings? Share prices ?

Well what were you thinking of ?!

The object of this card game is to collect as many pairs as possible. The game, first published in 1916, has cards with objects on them, a gavel, and some coins. At the start of the game the cards are dealt and the coins distributed evenly. One player takes the first turn at being auctioneer. He takes up the gavel and declares an item for sale from his cards. The trick is the auctioneer can only describe the item by it's number of legs. Many different items have the same number of legs, so players have to bid on the chance that they are getting the item they need, without really knowing it. The purchaser of the card becomes the new auctioneer. As players earn pairs they set them aside and are assigned a value. When all cards are exhausted the winner is the one with the most value in pairs and in coins.

Spanish Fury, Voyage! is a STAND ALONE game of Corsair activity in the Caribbean from 1550 to 1585. The Corsair player (or players) enters the region with ships packed with either Trade Good or troops (usually both) in hopes of turning a profit by hook or by crook. The Spanish Empire player must see that he prevents the Corsairs from succeeding (and discouraging others) while at the same time running the Caribbean efficiently so as to not lose his Vice-royalty (is that a word?).

FREE game to download here: http://perfectcaptain.50megs.com/sfvoyage.html

Alte Spiele -- Games of Old -- Jeux d'Autrefois
1975 Calendar

This was a 1975 promotional calendar from printing press manufacturer Roland Offset. it contained six roll-and-move style games from the 1800s.

From the first page:
"Present-day games have contemporary goals: The imagination envisions planetary flight and the psycho tests of the psychologist are dinner table discussion. In this context, who would like to hazard his fortune on a throw of dice? Even in cases where chance is permitted, workaday personal and occupational practices persist - the neighbor is shunted aside or his property gloatingly purchased at auction...

All of this was not evident at the beginning of our rushing century. All one wanted to do at the end of the day was to relax. Television, radio, record player and cinema were unknown. The first motorists purchased their fuel at the apothecary and the operator of a magic-lantern illuminated his coloured glass slides with a kerosene lamp. The village squares were still the site of public wells, although some well-to-do people already had gaslight. Geese were allowed to graze the entire summer. Skiing was just beginning to be recognized as a sport. A race track for horses in the city was a novelty. And a locomotive engineer was admired by all the boys: 'What a man!'

Games of old reflect this world of the small adventures, where it was an event if a train derailed or even when a fowl flew its coop. Then in those days games were modern and exciting. Even for their producers..."

January/February - "The Ski Race"
March/April - "The Tramway"
May/June - "We Take The Train"
July/August - "The Goose Game"
September/October - "The Automobile Race"
November/December - "Commerce And Trade"

boardgame: Monopoly: St. Louis Cardinals Collector's Edition
description:
This is Monopoly but based on the St. Louis Cardinals MLB baseball team.
It contains 6 pewter peices.
Published at a time When Mark McGwire was on the team.
Phonics based word game for Ages 6 and up.
Role and Move game based on the Looney Tunes Characters Sylvester and Tweety.

From the Box (all spellings and grammar are identical to the box text):

The Alaskan Gold Rush

A Game of High Adventure

Is the rousing new board game that combines the excitement of poker with the incredible adventures of the Alaskan Frontier.

In the 1890's thousands of greenhorns flooded the Port of Skagway looking for riches beyond their wildest dreams. However, charging bull moose, ferocious Grizzly bear, raging rivers, ice flows and frost bite (sic) await all those who quest for gold. Only the most experienced gold-panners emerged from the wilderness unscathed with gold in their pockets. These SOURDOUGHS were the toughest of the tough, and would literally risk an entire year's earnings on the flip of a card in a poker hand.

Would you be willing to bet everything you have on a bluff, or will you play it safe. This is a game of strategy, luck and skill, where the game dramatically changes with the personality of the players.

The easy to play game includes:

1 Board of Alaska (the game track is superimposed on a satellite picture)
1 Deck of standard playing cards
100 Silver and Gold coins (Poker Chips)
100 $1 Dollar Bills
6 Playing pieces
1 Die (d6)
27 Adventure cards (15 gold, 12 silver)
6 I.O.U. Cards (4 gold, 2 silver)
Rules

The game begins as a simple roll-and-move mechanic with various spaces giving rewards or penalties. There are also spaces that can trigger a round of poker between the players - winner take all monies bet. The players must accumulate wealth enough to buy transportation to the gold fields and then find the motherlode (Big Vein). Once there, a final hand of poker is played and the person at the mine must have $500,000 remaining after the hand.

The components are of high quality and it presents an interesting twist on a mundane night of poker.

boardgame: Wine Tour: The Napa Valley Game
description:
From the Instruction book:

Object: Tour the Napa Valley wine country starting from San Francisco, collecting wine point cards by visiting wineries along the way. In addition, Case Cards are collected from the burgundy-colored wineries at the ends of the side roads, and a bumper sticker is collected upon landing directly on Calistoga. The first person to return to San Francisco with 500 points (or a pre-determined number), at least two case cards (which are worth points and are included in the total) and a Calistoga bumper sticker, wins the game.

Equipment:

4 Decks of city cards
1 die
4 Wooden player tokens (in the shape of wine bottles)
1 deck of case cards
Bumper Stickers
Wine Point cards
Balloon spinner

The games looks as if it were produced to advertise the wineries of the Napa Valley. Over half of the instruction booklet is filled with the names and addresses of wineries that supported the game. Not much decision making here, even the intersections on the pathways have instructions on which way the tokens must move. The components are of a nice quality and even comes with a cloth bag to hold the wine bottle pawns.

Dice game of American football.  Features 1 kicking die; 2 offense dice (1 run; 1 pass); 2 defense dice (1 run defense; 1 pass defense).  Players roll off to determine results of plays, keeping track of field position mentally or on paper.  Follows rules of football.
Players roll special fairway dice and putting die to determine shot location and number of strokes for a round of golf.

JIHAD

Strategy game about the "holy war".
Each player plays a God and tries to convert countries to his beliefs. He has to win over 5 countries to his beliefs to both end and win the game.

Game Information

The aim is to build up the most money by buying or selling an intriguing range of every day items; sounds easy ? Well the twist is, that a roll of the die will mean you have to buy & sell in the value of a different year each time (ranging from 1950-1995) !

Imagine, buying a microwave oven at 1970's price (£259) & selling it in 1995 (£90) !

Not so easy eh !

Contents

100 x Goodie cards

1 x Set play money

6 x Playing pieces

1 x Regular die

1 x Year die

boardgame: Teenage Mutant Hero Turtles: Jacht op Shredder
description:
You are one of the four hero (not ninja) turtles. 12 cards are placed on the table in a 4*4 square without the corners. Each turtle starts in a different corner. You throw the dice and may move your turtle on the cards in any direction.
When you land on a card, you turn it over. If the card shows a pizza, you get a pizza-card. When you land on an opponent (Bebop, Rock Steady, Krang, Shredder), you'll have to fight him. You throw the dice, you may add pizza-points, and when you have more than indicated on the card, you win the card. 
The first to conquer the four bad guys, wins the game.

description: In RGB - a barbaric game played with Icehouse pieces on a chessboard - you're trying to kill your opponent's children. (You were warned it was barbaric!) You control an army of RGBs - alien creatures with three distinct layers of thought - and they lose one layer (and an ability) if they are attacked. For example, children must remain next to a piece which still has the green sustenance layer, or the child starves. Children can be created if the spare pieces exist, but only by one male and one female, both with the blue 'reproduction' layer. And attacks can only be carried out by pieces with the red destruction layer. RGB bears a few similarities to Chess, but not as many as you might think.

Experienced explorers can play a second, even more challenging game.  Using a set of clue cards, you can launch a fast paced battle requiring quick thinking and even quicker answers!

FOURSITE™ is a game that stretches the intellect, tests your ability to multi-strategise and provides a different set of challenges every time you play it. From the publisher's webpage:

Battleship Captain is a simulation game depicting ship vs ship combat in the era of the battleship.  The game covers warships produced in the pre-dreadnought (1890-1906), dreadnought (1906-1919), and World War II (1920-1945) eras, providing gamers the chance to replay a virtually unlimited number of historical or hypothetical scenarios occurring during the six decade "golden age" of the battleship.
    What's Included:  Over ONE THOUSAND individually rated warships (light cruiser size and larger) from over twenty of the world's navies, from the three eras represented in the game, on twenty-two counter sheets; 150 generic destroyer and merchant ship counters; 56-page comprehensive rulebook containing standard instructions applicable to each major period, plus separate chapters with "era specific" rules, Ship Rating Index, designer's notes, over thirty scenarios; scenario guidelines folder; over 300 color game markers and playing gauges; and three Reference Cards.  This is a full, complete game... nothing else is  needed to recreate surface combat over the entire period!
   Battleship Captain is a "miniatures" game system with scenarios played on the tabletop or floor, using the rules and ship counters provided.  (You may freely substitute metal miniatures if you wish.)   The game emphasized playability, as players maneuver their ships and try to defeat the enemy.  The counters (measuring 2.5" x 5/8ths inch) are printed in black ink on different colored cardstock, and need to be mounted and separated prior to play.  Game scale is seven turns per hour, and 1000 yards per inch.  Ships are rated, right on their counter, in the following categories:  name, nationality, ship type, year, flotation, stern-broadside-bow fire ratings, movement, armor, and nine "special characteristics" ratings, plus an overhead scale view of the ship's deck.

Players try to peg out by rolling dice combinations that match the different lanes on the wooden, cribbage-like pegging board. For instance, the peg in your 6 lane moves one space closer to home with every 6 you have rolled on the dice. Once you peg out on a given lane, you now score victory points every time you roll that number. Opportunities exist to bump your opponent back by landing on him, or to have your roll count double, depending on your peg position.

But first, each time you roll the dice, you have to set aside any pair of dice that add up to seven! If the first throw doesn't look good enough, you may take one re-roll.

Diver Down is a boardgame about Scuba Diver Exploration, as well as can be done in what is known as a Eurogame. 
The game relies heavily on the use of money cards for purchasing supplies needed to dive on sites around the world. Treasures are found at each site, and if a player bids highest, they become the expedition leader and can bring up the best treasures. All other players will get some lesser treasures. Only the treasures taken by the expedition leader will be able to be taken to the museum and score points. There are three museum tracks, one for each: gold, pottery, and jewelry; worth 4,3,2 points respectively. The player with the most points after 5 rounds will be declared the winner and gain the respect of their fellow divers.

description:
A gripping duel between two hungry crocodiles and a bold "oarsman".

One player steers two green crocodiles, the other player owns a red rowing boat (wooden playing pieces). Both start opposite of each other in the middle of a 4 by 8 "river" board. The boat player secretly chooses one card (printed with the values 1 to 4) to select the column on the board he wishes to move to. The crocodile player has to guess this column and place his crocodiles on two spaces to block the boat. If the crocodile player was right, the boat has to retreat one row into the direction of "its" river bank, if the other player succeeded in choosing a crocodile-free space, he moves one step to the river bank of the other player.
Whoever reaches the opposite bank first is the winner.
No tactics involved, just blind guessing.

Was available as a big box with nicely designed components first, later followed by a small version (1988) with tiny pieces and (flimsy) cardboard river (with lots of air in the box).

Dugout Dreams is a baseball simulation that used one 20-sided die and two 10-sided dice. The game comes with 17 pitching charts on 5 pages, a color playing field on which to keep track of outs and base runners, score sheets, and various charts, including: fly outs, hit & run, hitting direction for left- and right-handed batters, runner advancement, ground outs, hold/steal, errors, and steals. Teams are available from various seasons, including some recent ones and 1926, 1948, and 1971. Players' ratings are listed on 8.5 x 11" card stock, one page for each team. (There are no individual cards in this game.) Ratings are based on actual performance from these respective seasons.

To play, roll a d20 and cross-reference the result with the Batting Rating on Player's Batting listing with that of the Pitcher's Chart, where the following results are found: blank (an out), W (a possible walk), or H (a hit). Each of these results requires an additional 2-4 rolls of two d10 dice to resolve a given play.

Pitchers are rated for Start/Relief effectiveness, walks, strike outs, home runs yielded, ground balls, fly balls, stamina, hold ability, and fielding.

Batters are rated in the following categories: Batting, HR, 1B, 2B, 3B, W, K, Fielding, Range/Arm, SAC, BS (base stealing) and BR (base running).

Your elite force of MechWarriors drives these juggernauts into battle, proudly holding your faction’s flag high, intent on expanding the power and glory of your realm. At their beck and call are the support units of armored vehicles, power armored infantry, aerospace fighters and more, wielded by a MechWarrior's skillful command to aid him in ultimate victory. The product of more than twenty years of gaming experience, Total Warfare presents the rules of the Classic BattleTech game system as never before. For the first time, all the rules for various units that have a direct impact on the deadly battlefields of the thirty-first century appear in a unified rules set: from BattleMechs to ProtoMechs, Combat Vehicles to Support Vehicles, infantry to aerospace fighters and DropShips. Interwoven and meticulously updated, Total Warfare provides the most detailed and comprehensive rules set published to date for BattleTech—the perfect companion for standard tournament play.

Total Warfare is the single-source rulebook for people who play Classic BattleTech. It is not intended to teach new players the game, but rather to serve as a reference work for people who know the game, while introducing more technologies and expansive rules than appear in the basic box set. The introductory game in the BattleTech line is the Classic BattleTech Introductory Box Set. New players should pick up that product before diving into this one. Construction rules for the various units presented in Total Warfare can be found in Classic BattleTech TechManual.
