Aim: To be the richest to travel around the world. When one of the players run out of money, the game is over. The winner will be the one with the most money and property.

Players choose a coloured plane to represent themselves, and start out with a specific sum of money. They take turns to spin the arrow dice for the number of squares that their planes may fly on the board. Players have the option to build their house on the country squares they land on, but they can do this for free if they spin the arrow dice and it lands on "Free" (located on a different circle from the one used for movement). If players land on a space which already has property built, rent is to be paid to the house owner.

The game is similar in some ways to Monopoly, but with more options. In addition, it plays more quickly than Monopoly due to the endgame condition which requires only one player to run out of money before the winner is determined.

In Party Time the players play in two teams. One player draws a card and has to explain/describe, draw, pantomime or knead up to three words/items. Depending on how many items his/her team guesses the team's pawn may advance 0, 1, 3 or 6 steps along the board. On the cards you have clusters of three terms per action to be taken. The action a player has to take (explain, draw, pantomime or knead) is decided on the field the pawn is situated on.
You also have a flash symbol on the board. Whenever a pawn starts its move on the flash symbol, this goup must take an special card and one player has to do whatever is written on the card (e.g. "non-sense" like explain terms using a certain slang, stand and one leg and put off your shoes and socks, keep your breath for 90 seconds...).

Tactics is a landmark game in that it was the first cardboard-counter commercial board wargame that was intended (albeit with limited success) to accurately simulate warfare and command.

The game was self-published by Maryland National Guard infantryman Charles S. Roberts, who would go on to found of Avalon Hill Games. The original edition of Tactics was published by the Avalon Game company, with a print run of approximately 2000. Roberts sold Tactics by mail order out of a garage in Avalon, Maryland.

The game portrayed a modern day strategic conflict between the forces of two hypothetical countries. The opposing units included infantry, armor, and airborne forces. The game was played on a mapboard which used a square grid rather than the hex-based style map which would later became preferred in the board wargaming industry. However, many of the conventions and mechanics established in Tactics still live on, in one form or another, in many of the wargames published today.

Based on his success with Tactics, Roberts decided to expand his operation and in 1958 he formed Avalon Hill Games. The initial line-up of games from Avalon Hill included Tactics II, a revised version of Roberts' original wargame. Avalon Hill would go on to publish a 25th Anniversary edition of Tactics in 1983, which represented a modified version of the original game in that it used a prototype mapboard which had not previously been released.

Other than a few graphical changes there are some functional changes from the Tactics map to the Tactics II map:

Going over the 1954 Tactics and 1958 Tactics II boards square by square the only functional changes are:
 - The squares around the edge of the map for 1958 now have numbers 1 to 43 from North to South and 1 to 55 West to East.
 - Cities for 1958 had unique numbers added. 
No numbers are present on the 1954 board in the cities or the grid.
 - There is no map compass on the mapboard label like there is in Tactics II
 - The mountain pass in the large mountain range on the board label edge is not present on the 1954 map. Also there is only one single clear square, same place as in Tactics II, 7 squares in from the Blue coastal city near the map label in that mountain range. There are none of those longer dead end corridors in the big mountain range like on the Tactics II map.

Re-implemented by:

    Tactics II
    Tactics (25th Anniversary Edition) (25th Anniversary Edition)

boardgame: Bolos and Krags: The Philippine American War 1899-1902
description:
(from the designer:)

On June 12, 1898. Filipino revolutionary forces under Emilio Aguinaldo declared proclaimed independence of the Philippine islands from the colonial rule of Spain. The declaration of independence however was not recognized by the United States of America and Spain since the Spanish government ceded the Philipines to the USA in the aftermath of the 1898 Treaty of Paris which formally ended the Spanish American war (April 25 to August 12, 1898). Tensions already existed between both sides due to conflicting movements of independence and colonization further aggravated by misunderstandings on both sides and feelings of betrayal on the Filipino side. The tensions escalated between the former allies on February 4, 1899 when a Filipino soldier was shot by an American soldier (William W. Grayson) in Manila. Fighting soon erupted in Manila and culminated in an official Filipino declaration of war by the Malolos congress on June 2, 1899. The war would last 3 bloody years and would see a short conventional war followed by a long guerilla war which would be a prelude of things to come in Vietnam 60 years later.

---

"Bolos & Krags" is a simulation of the Philippine American War 1899-1902 which saw 4 years of brutal guerilla war resulting in 4,000 American soldiers and a million and a half Filipino dead. The war influenced Mao Tse Tung to refine his people's war guerilla doctrine which would haunt the U.S. in Vietnam and Iraq many decades later.

Area impulse action movement system.  280 counters, 12 pages of rules, area map covers all of Luzon. units are leaders of regiment sized battlegroups which you equip with companies in a system similiar to Death In the Trenches/CSA. Victory centers on Political will (like Byzantium Reborn/Battle For China). Combat is the NO CRT wescom system from Death in the Trenches/CSA. Has Strategic chits(from Storm Over Taierzhuang) to give both sides a wealth of strategic options. Lots of random events.

The game shows the political and military dimensions of the war. 16 bimonthly turns. In a historical, easy to play and learn, balanced and interactive format. Playable within one evening. Interesting situation as the U.S. with military supremacy tries to fight a guerilla war with determined but poorly equipped Philippine Republic insurgencts in one of the most roughest terrain in the world.

This game includes:
Mark Twain, Bandit hunts, guerilla warfare, atrocities, Sun Yat Sen, Boxer Rebellion, Philippine Constabulary, President Mckinley, 1900 U.S. presidential elections, epidemics, Mao Tse Tung (Ok, a cameo in the random events), General Joe Wheeler, typhoon, hearts and minds and more...

This game is probably the first wargame on the Philippine American war 1899-1902 and this game will shed much needed light on a obscure part of American history.

There are 140 cards divided into two decks of 70 cards each. The first deck contains lighter questions such as, "If our family inherited a million dollars, I would like for us to _______." Most of the questions in the first deck are about families in general and not about individual members of the family. The second deck is composed of more specific questions, such as describing your feelings about other family members or explaining how you felt when a family-member hurt your feelings. Other cards allow the player to talk about any subject they choose.

Players take turns drawing cards and answering the questions on them in a few sentences. Most of the rules are considered as guidelines, such as the "hint" to keep your answers fairly short, but the Rule of Silence during another player's answer must be observed.

As with other games in the Ungame series, there is no winner or loser. The object of the game is to spend quality time with your family and learn more about the thought and feelings of those closest to you.

boardgame: Dios Patria y Rey The Carlists Wars, 1833-1840
description:
Alea Magazine #32.

Dios Patria Rey (For God, Country and King) is a system simulating several battles of the First Carlist War of 1833-1840.

The Carlist Wars in Spain were the last major European civil wars in which pretenders fought to establish their claim to a throne. Several times during the period from 1833 to 1876 the Carlists — followers of Infante Carlos (later Carlos V) and his descendants — rallied to the cry of "God, Country, and King" and fought for the cause of Spanish tradition (Legitimism and Catholicism) against the liberalism, and later the republicanism, of the Spanish governments of the day.

Dios Patria Rey uses a beefed up and revised Libertadores system (Soldados y Estrategia n. 10) to simulate eight of the most decisive battles of this war: Arquijas, Luchana, Oriamendi, Barbastro, Maella and the 1st and 2nd battles of Perecamps.

The game features the several foreign units which fought in Spain in 1833-40 including the British, Portuguese and Belgian legions and the French Foreign Legion. There were also French, Prussian Austrian and Russian Absolutist fighting on the Carlist side. The Carlist cause was defeated, and the Carlist pretender died in exile in the Austrian Empire. He is buried at the San Giusto Martire cathedral in Trieste, now in Northern Italy.

Start - players are dealt cards, the number depending on how many players, and the remaining cards are left in a face down stack.

Object of game - to get rid of all ones cards by laying cards so as to continue either the red main line or yellow branch line. Players either play one card or take a card from the remaining stack. The general idea is like a game of dominoes. At the end of a game players count up the points on cards still in hand.

The Winner is the player with least [or no] points counting against him.

Ah Fruits!

Albert the farmer is a very old man who can't really go around is farmland to pick is fruits like he used to.
So instead of letting is fruits rot on the ground, he gathered a few of his animal friends to help him out with his tedious job.

But if you give a basket to a duck, a dog, a pig, a turtle and a hedgehog, confusion is bound to happen!

With all of their good will and dedication, our little friends can't stop bickering and start little competitions between themselves to see who will gather the most fruits!

What is the Tosenkyo?


To-sen-kyo means throwing-fan-amusement. To contest your skill with your rival, aim the target, toss the fan, and then refer scoring chart for victory points (VP). Although its elegant image made some fantasy like "game of Heian Era from a millennium ago", it is only legend. According to evidences of manuscripts, it might be flourished from middle of Edo era (about 1770s).

Skilled veteran makes victory points constantly. Beginner may hit big score luckily. Because of this easily enjoyability for everyone, it won popularity once among commoners.


    Difference of Tosenkyo from Darts


As same throwing game, Tosenkyo is frequently compared with Darts. The most significant deference of Tosenkyo from Darts is way of scoring. VP of Darts are gained from stabbed area directly. But in Tosenkyo, after hitting fan dropped the target down from the Base box, VP would be determined between distance and over/under positions of fan and target (and sometimes Base of target). Beginner cannot win almost against veteran in Darts. But in Tosenkyo, scoring system has some happening factors. Some beginner may hit big lucky score and win against constant veteran. This makes the game unexpectedly thrilling and exciting.

In fact, adequate proportion of luck factor against cool rule is the joyous essence of Tosenkyo.

Page: http://www.tosenkyo.net/whats-e.htm

The fifth major expansion to Carcassonne, Abbey & Mayor adds 3 new wooden piece types, 6 abbey tiles, and 12 landscape tiles. The Abbey tiles act as Cloisters but do not have to match adjacent tiles and they complete adjacent features when placed. Mayors can be placed only in cities, with their strength determined by the number of pennants in the city. Barns allow players to score fields during the game rather than just at the end. Wagons are placed on roads, cities, or cloisters, and can move to an adjacent open feature when their current feature is completed.

Part of the Carcassonne series.

Expands:

    Carcassonne

Spreadbet is a trivia/economic/wagering boardgame that uses the mechanics of the growing phenomenon of 'spread betting'.   Each round, one player takes his or her turn to set their estimate of the answer to a trivia question like 'the height of the Eiffel Tower', and pays out or collects from the other players after they have gone 'higher' or 'lower'.

In this game players act as delivery drivers for DAF trucks, driving them through Europe to where they're required.  Sometimes, in addition to delivering the truck, there may be a cargo to deliver on the way.  Each time a successful delivery is made, the player gets a Delivery Bill.  The winner of the game is the first to get a complete set of Delivery Bills.

This is principally a roll and move game, but players can use the die roll to move 1, 2 or 3 trucks, as long as the total moves equals the number on the die.

game board
die
24 card holders
18 truck cards
6 road blocks
36 route cards
36 cargo cards
6 service tickets
18 devlivery bills
rule book
publishers: DAF Divisional Parts Marketing Services Department

description: Each player has 10 numbered disks and 10 numbered slots (Channels) on his base that each disk goes into.  The board is  a grid pattern.  In the middle of the board is the scoring base.  The scoring base as 10 numbered slots like each players base.  The scoring base also has four channels to move disks through.  Each turn a player may move one of his disks one place; forward, backward, left or right.  Each player is trying to get his numbered disk in the same numbered scoring slot on the scoring base.  Each scoring slot only holds one disk.  Players may use their disks to block slots and channels.  When all scoring slots are filled players add up the total of a each players respective scoring slots with the highest winning.

Muffin the Mule was a children's television program made by the BBC through the late '40s and the '50s. This game displays characters and events from the television show.

The game is basically "Strip Jack Naked" or "Beggar My Neighbour" with players playing the top card from their deck to a pile in the centre of the table. If a numbered card is played then the next player must pay cards in succession equal to the number on the card. If while paying out that player turns over a numbered card then the next player must now pay for that card. If a player pays cards to match the required number without revealing a numbered card themselves the player who player the numbered card collects the entire stack from the centre of the table and adds it to the bottom of their own pile.

This game has the addition of 2 Peregrine the Penguin cards. When one of these is revealed the first player to shout "Muffin" instantly wins the centre pile.

Play continues until one player has all of the cards.

boardgame: Krimi Total Fall 6: Der verfluchte Schatz der Piraten
description:
The 6th in the KRIMI Total series. This time the setting is Broken Island in the Caribbean and players are trying to find the long lost treasure of Broken Island. But as the keeper of the treasure has been found dead, a curse now lays over the island. What happened?

Released: Essen 2007. Ages 16 and up.

The Wargame system is devoted to battles of WWII (1939-1945).

It models tactical level of combat operations. A corresponding card represents each military formation. They may be infantry, armor, artillery and aircraft units, or even special troops (e.g. Guerrillas). As a rule a card depicts certain military unit, usually company or platoon. Game parameters of unit cards are adjusted to reflect not only their power but also tactical capabilities in terms of the game mechanics. Resource cards reflect the economic powers of each side, which enable them to build and maintain armies and to take military actions.

Diabelski Krąg is a tactical game in which (in it's basic version) two players compete to either defend or kill a infantile princess of a fantasy kingdom untill the dawn for 3 nights (time is randomly simulated after each turn). The "good side" are four powerful mages, while the "evil side" are eight demons.

Though tactical, the game is pretty random, since the demons change their statistics every turn due to the changing phases of the moon and stars. Demons can change their status, there are 8 stages of it, from 100% material (they can be hurt and they can fight) to 100% spiritual (they can fight only using psychic attack, but can pass through the walls, etc.). Demons can use fireplaces to move, cast a curse, use pentagrams and holes to hell. Mages can use garlick, Torch of Ithilien's Temple, Ilih's Weed, wand, salt, Magic Book, Elvish Drop, pray and exorcise.

One of the last games by Sfera.

In this free game that is part of the German magazine Spielbox (number 3/2007), all players rush from start to finish. But at the end of the game it's not the fastest the winner but the player with most coins.
Each player starts with 12 coins. Every player has always three cards with values of 1/2/3 to move his pawn accordingly.
The first player chooses a card, all other player try to guess what value he has chosen. This first player will move his pawn according to the value of the card but the other players have to compare their guesses.
Were they right, then they have to move one field further than that first player. The first player has also to give them a coin. 
A player that guessed wrong only moves one field ahead ignoring the card he has played.
The First player changes every move around the players. The game ends with the first player reaching the finish field.

How does a MechWarrior interface with a BattleMech? How does a ProtoMech really work? When was the Gauss rifle or the Particle Projector Cannon developed? A must have resource for any BattleTech aficionado!

description:
Players take control of a primal human race and try to be the first to evolve from Australopithecus to Homo Sapiens.

The map is divided to different tiles and each one has a number on it. Active player throws the dice and populations in that tile multiplies and possibly gains a power card. The idea is to multiply & try to spread ones race to the far corners of the Earth. By discarding power cards (instead of playing the effect written into them) players can develop their muscles so that they can drive their rivals away from the fertile forests or generous jungles. Or alternatively they can develop their food diet and outbreed their rivals and beat them by sheer numbers. At least that way they can better afford the occasional bestial attack or an epidemic disease. However, player's can't hold on to their good locations forever because every time they want to evolve to the next level only a small fraction of their population can make the leap while the rest continue to populate the area.

Jeepers Leapers is a children's game suitable for Ages 3 - 6.  
"Catch the most frogs before they leap off their lily pads!"

Foam frogs are placed randomly in the holes on a rotating board (the lily pads).  A pair of dice with coloured sides are rolled and all the players dive to grab frogs that match the colours on the dice.  For example, if the colours red and blue are rolled, then either blue frogs on red lily pads or red frogs on blue lily pads can be collected.  Players are awarded point tokens for the frogs they hold, and the frogs are returned to the pond.  The board is then rotated so each frog is now on a different coloured lily pad.

Projex (Projective Hex) is a Hex-like abstract strategy game: two players take turns to occupy hexagonal cells, competing to build a chain of occupied cells across the board.

For Projex, the hex grid wraps around at the edges to form a projective plane.  Six pentagons (or three squares) must be included among the hexagons for the whole plane to be tiled.

A winning chain is any loop which encircles the projective plane, in the sense that it is not topologically equivalent to a trivial loop.  As with Hex and Y, the game has the mathematically appealing property that when the board is full, exactly one player will have a winning chain.

				
				
					References
     Designer's post to sci.math describing the game
     Description at gamerz.net

The Emperor’s Arena is where legends are born.  Lured by the promise of all the treasure one can carry from the Emperor’s Vault, heroes thrust themselves into combat with the Emperor’s fiercest monsters, the most diabolical of tricks, and the most blood-thirsty competitors.  To those who can survive an hour in the arena, a life of fame and fortune awaits.

In The Emperor’s Arena, you and your opponents take control of rival teams of heroes battling for fame and fortune within the Emperor’s Arena.  As time progresses, the Emperor will release increasingly dangerous opponents into the ring, requiring you to decide how much punishment your active hero can take before tagging to the next team member.  Fame is gained from slaying monsters and attacking other players.  The player whose team has gained the most fame by the end of the game wins.

Follow up to 5 Alive with new special cards and a different way to get lives.

Players take turns playing cards and adding up the total - you want to take the total over 21 so you gain a life. First Player with 5 lives wins!

Trail Drive by the Aarco Playing Card Company of Chicago, New York, Los Angeles and Toronto. From the box; Round up your herd and hit the trail with your Chuckwagon, Cowboys and Longhorns in this exciting new game of the West. Watch out for rustlers, pardner, good luck on your TRAIL DRIVE.

The game includes 40 new Western Cards and a rule card.  For boys and girls Ages 6 - 12

Rules: 
How to Play Trail Drive

THE GAME: Try to play as many cards as you can each turn to build a long Trail Drive with a Chuckwagon, Cowboys and Cows, laying your cards in a row on the table in front of you. A Rustler might stop you, but a Sheriff gets you started again.
THE DEAL: If four or more play, deal 4 cards apiece, then play that round, deal 4 more and play again, continuing to deal and play until the deck is used up. If three play, deal 5 cards apiece each round. If two play, deal 6.
THE PLAY: After each round is dealt, beginning at left of the dealer you each play what you can from your hand. The first three cards must be 1 Chuckwagon and 2 Cowboys in any order; then you add cows, the more the better. Discard the rest of your hand, face down. After each round the discards are added to the bottom of the deck. You may play a Rustler card to stop anyone’s Trail Drive when it has cows. Then no more Cows may be added, until a Sheriff is played on the Rustler. You may start a new Trail Drive, when your first Drive is rustled (with 1 Chuckwagon, 2 Cowboys, then Cows, as before), but you may not play on more than one Trail Drive in one turn. Therefore, you may not play a Sheriff card and start a new Drive in the same turn.
When Deck is used up, whether or not it comes out even, the dealer calls, “Last Round.” On the last round, each player passes to the next player the cards he can’t use, instead of discarding.
THE WINNER: When all possible plays have been made, you each take out all the Rustlers, plus two Cows for each Rustler. Count the rest. Whoever has the most wins.

What If? is a fun question and answer game designed by Diane Rogers and dated 1968.  What If is the hypothetical question game for teens and adults. Game comes with a couple of pamphlets with questions to be asked and a set of rules on how to play the game rules. The game box is 8-1/4 x 10-1/4 x 1-1/2.
Game originally published in the British comic book 2000 AD, issues #8-11. Based on the comic Flesh were rangers from the future travel in time to kill dinosaurs for meat.

From the back of the box:

It's the classic Magic 8 Ball with a twist!
The Magic 8 Ball now includes a ticking timer for a ridiculously unpredictable party game. Consult the Magic 8 Ball and race to the finish line by competing in over 70 wild, physical and mental challenges.
Complete the challenge and hand the Magic 8 Ball to the next player. Now it's their turn to do the same. Lose a challenge? Forfeit a token.
Mind-numbing hilarity that will have you rolling on the floor, literally.
But hold on to your tokens. The "Great Eight Potentate" in round two dictates the price players must pay to win back lost tokens.

" Match*A*Book "

Any two books in order are considered a pair. 
For example: if Luke is captured, it would take either Mark or John to make a pair. "

" Match*A*Verse " 
" The object of the game is to match halves of Bible verses. "
This is a children's game themed on dinosaurs.  Players roll their dice and compete to move their set of 4 dinosaurs to safety.

Packaged in the form of a thin, staple bound book with 5 dice, Spooky Math Games is a collection of 13 games, each with a strong mathematical slant, and each with a kid-friendly 'spooky' theme.  Many of the games require additional components, be it a deck of cards, counters, a hand drawn board, or a combination of all the above.

A common mechanic used in many of the games has players roll a number of dice and then perform addition, subtraction, multiplication and division on the results in order to reach a target number.

The included dice are black with white pips, with a square of bones runs along the perimeter of the faces.  4 of the dice have a standard configuration, while the fifth depicts a cartoon skull in place of the '6', and has additional meaning in some of the games.

The games presented in the book are:


    Spider Legs
    Pirate's Watch
    Picking Through The Pumpkin Patch
    Ghoul's Place
    Haunted House
    Ghost's Grave
    Sitting on the Picket Fence
    Broomsticks Up!
    The Devil's Arithmetic
    Skeletons in Your Closet
    Witches' Brew
    Lucky 13
    Stomping Through The Graveyard


Sequel:

    Spooky Scary Math Games

boardgame: NASCAR: Chase for the Nextel Cup 2007
description:
From the press release:

The new for 2007 game features drivers who qualified for The 2007 Chase for the NASCAR Nextel Cup, which is NASCAR’s version of playoffs. The 12 drivers who are highlighted in the game along with their race cars are: Jimmie Johnson, Jeff Gordon, Tony Stewart, Carl Edwards, Kurt Busch, Denny Hamlin, Martin Truex Jr., Matt Kenseth, Kyle Busch, Jeff Burton, Kevin Harvick and Clint Bowyer. The drivers were determined Sept. 8 after the Chevy Rock and Roll 400 race at Richmond International Speedway.

Players race around the racetrack game board, trying to be the first to cross the finish line for the 10 races that make up The Chase. They collect chips for the races they win, and the player with the most race chips at the end of the game is the winner.

Along the way, players try to avoid the Crash Zone and Pit Road but zoom forward when they land on a Drafting Advantage space or play a Pass Card. The game board also has several Chase Challenge spaces, which might help lucky players take the lead or avoid a crash. Players who aren’t so lucky might be warned about changing track conditions or a tire that’s losing air.

No reading is required and dinosaurs rule in this dino-domino matching game for kids ages three to eight. It includes twenty-eight extra thick colorful dominoes and represents yet another fun way to improve matching skills.  Children can learn color-matching, social interaction, recognition and concentration skills.  These Dino Dominoes also help kids pronounce the dinosaur names.

Ages 3 - 8

Igels 2: Waschtag is an expansion to Igels: Das Kartenspiel and adds two new groups of animals: raccoons and birds. The fight for food is even more difficult now with 5 or 6 payers. But new munition is added as well.

"Bambus Spiele No 2" 
This book offers a glimpse into the boardgame scene of Northern Ireland, Finland, Poland, Israel, Madagascar, Japan, Thailand, Singapore, Australia, USA (extra chapter about Hawaii) and Brazil. 
It also offers rules of local games and games from Michael Schacht, Günter Cornett, Rudi Hoffman, Joli Quentin Kansil and others.

Themaword is a word game: random letters are placed on the gaming table, the players draw a category card and must find a word in the corresponding category, containing only letters found on the gaming table.

Categories include: domestic animals, verbs, colors, food, vegetables, ...

NI-SHI-KI -Shouden- is a pocket sized version of NI-SHI-KI: The Sliding Samurai Card Chess meant for head to head combat.

NI-SHI-KI -Shouden- contains 13 Warlord cards, which are all new and different from the large version (examples; the powerful Tokugawa Ieyasu and Shimazu Yoshihiro).

Originally published in the British 2000 AD comic. Play Ro-jaws, Mek-quake, Hammer-stein or Dr. Feeley-Good in a simple roll-and-move game. The first player to reach Greasy Gracie's Robo-Bar is the winner.

Subtitled: 5000 years of dice and dicing
This 192-page book covers the entire history of dice in many cultures.  It summarizes the many dice that have been found in sites of antiquity around the world.  The history then moves into various efforts to outlaw or tax gambling with dice.  There's also a chapter on dice cheating and another on modern dice.  The book is peppered with interesting stories and legends that revolve around dice.  If you ever wondered why many bone dice are hollow, it's explained.  If you want to know where the best museum exhibits of dice are, it's also covered.  The origins and evolution of many dice games are covered as well.

The book is heavily illustrated with drawings and photographs.

The copy I read was a complimentary copy published under the auspices of Dice & Games Ltd and translated from the original Dutch.

This is a children's version of Malefiz aka Barricade. You only have 2 pawns instead of five. There are only 4 barricades instead of 11. And the board has fewer fields.  If your pawn is taken by another and this happens on the top  half of the board, you don't have to start all over again. In that case you may start again in the middle of the board. There are also secure places on the board. If you are on a blue field with a drawing of Pluto, you're safe from attacks of other players. Barricades can not be placed on the blue fields. There are even simpler rules (with one pawn) for children between four and six.

Though it is somewhat confusing, Ravensburger produced a Spongbob version of both Junior Malefiz and Barricade, the latter is a version of Malefiz.  These two different items used the same cover artwork but differed on the board layout, rules, and numbers of figures.

360° Air Hockey with sound FX.
Fast flipper action for 2-4 Players. Ages 5 and up
It' just like air hockey.The object is to try and score in your opponents goal. The first one to score 10 goals first wins!

4 Player:
Place puck in center and let the fun begin! First person to 10 wins! Every time you shoot the puck into your own goal you lose a point. Just keep score on the score marker by sliding it over the number of pucks you have shot into an opponent's goal.

description: This is a twist on the usual game in which participants compete in a race while they balance an egg on a spoon.  In this one, 10 different items are given to 2 teams of children.  You can try to stack as much items on your balancing sticks as you want whenever it is your turn to race, but you risk slower speed, and if any of the items fall, it is back to the starting point.  But if you succeed in bringing a stack of items from the start to finish, then you can leave the items at the end point and go back to the starting point to pick up some more or pass to the next player in your team.  The team that completes all the items first wins.

A small 2-player abstract. The gameboard is a Q and on each space there are 2 stones. Stones have one of 4 colours and are neutral. Players take turns to remove stones from the board and place them in one line in front of them. Getting can only be done from either and of the Q, Placing can also be done to either and of the cue in front of the players.
The objective is to avoid stones with a colour not anotherwise in the cue.

A little roll-and-move shoot-'em-up game using a Treehouse set of icehouse pieces, Martian Coasters, and a 6-sided die.

Players use stacked nests of pyramids to represent a Zamboni with a giant laser on top - the mechanics are simple, run around the board, shoot at your opponent until you're the last one on the field.

Since the pieces are nested, each time your Zamboni is hit, another bit comes off, leaving a more vulnerable core piece to be shot at the next time. After three hits, you're out!

rules are here - http://icehousegames.org/wiki/index.php?title=Zamboni_Wars

"Exciting Tracks!"

Each player represents one of six large mammals found in the Finnish nature (wolf, lynx, elk, bear, wolverine and rabbit). From the starting area, paths of circles lead to a picture of each animal. The game is divided in two phases; in phase I, players try to collect the footprints of their chosen animal, being occasionally hindered by Wind or Snow. Phase II starts as soon as one player has collected all 14 footprints of their animal, and in this phase the players use their collected footprints as "stepping stones" to reach the picture of their animal on the board; naturally, in this phase those who managed to collect more prints in phase I have a better chance of success. Winner is the first player to reach their goal.

This is a reworking of a similarly themed game by Peliko's preceding publisher Paletti, WWF Taktinen Luontopeli, using very similar components and game board.

5 Finnish marks (about 1.30€ in 2020) from the sale of each copy were donated to Lastenklinikoiden Kummit RY, a Finnish charity raising funds in support of five university hospitals providing a clinic for children.

Published with the new Peliko logo already, while the Finnish mark is still in use; therefore, this is from sometime in the 1990's.

The first expansion for the of the Aeronautica Imperialis, an aircraft miniatures game set in the Warhammer 40,000 (Third Edition) universe. It is a 128 page hardcover book that expands the game and features new aircraft, scenarios and rules. It also includes hobby material, a variant campaign system  and a new campaign narrative on the Typha-IV conflict, depicting a desperate air war fought between the Imperium and the Tau Empire.

Excel Saga Boardgame! is a simple roll-and-move game included with the US DVD release of the anime series Excel Saga (Volume 5).  Players choose between six characters represented by included circular punch-out tokens.  Players then roll dice to advance along the board's path in typical roll-and-move fashion.  There are numerous spaces with special instructions, such as playing rock-paper-scissors to advance, rolling a certain number, or singing the series' theme song.

boardgame: How to Host a Kids Party: Safari Adventure Party
description:
A children's party game (presumably a birthday party) with a unifying theme (safari) and a loose deduction element, a la adult How to Host a Murder parties.  In this game, the children are attempting to rescue an animal by playing games and listening to a cassette tape.

Contents:
- cassette tape
- shopping/gathering list
- 16 activity (game) cards
- 12 safari postcard invitations
- animal footprint clues
- safari poster sticker game (stick-the-panda = pin-the-tail-on-the-donkey)
- 12 jumping frogs
- 48 safari cards
- small jungle animal
- party favors: 12 each of compasses, magnifying glasses, puzzles

This is one of three HtHaKP themed games.

An unusual game where players take turns trying to place magnetic bees onto magnetic spots on the face of Sally the sunflower. If the polarity is correct the bee sticks, if not Sally turns her head away.

From the manufacturer:
"Will Sally turn her face away or let your bumblebees land? Sally the Sunflower shows children the wonder of magnets all through the fun of play! Watch their enjoyment as they try to make a positive connection by landing their bumblebees onto Sally's face! The first to get all their bumblebees onto Sally's face wins!"

Evolution is the first expansion to the Hordes miniatures game, which in turn is a completely compatible sister game to Warmachine.  The book adds new units for each of the four Hordes factions, and new neutral Minions.  Also included are painting guides, campaign rules and compiled errata and clarifications for the main rulebook.

The book itself is 192 pages in full color available in both soft cover and hardcover versions.

Expands

    Hordes

From the box:
"His Coming - More than just a game!"

This is simply a roll-and-move and question answering game with a heavy Christian theme.
Game comes complete with board, instructions, pawns, cards and a bible.

Ages 8 and up

From the box:
"A hooker of a party game... fun and laughs for the entire family! Similar to the party game Twister but you couple yourself up with hooks! Ages 8 to adult."

Players use elastic bands around arms and legs to pass hooks from one player to another.
Players move their mother hens around the barnyard, trying to gather their chicks. Mothers are connected to chicks by magnetic attraction (in the base of their pieces).

In this trivia game featuring Oshkosh, WI, you move around a board featuring businesses from the city and answer questions relating to it.  You need to visit the businesses (which are all real Oshkosh establishments) to collected Product & Service cards in order to win. It was developed in the 1980's by David A. Colbert.

Mfg suggested Ages 7 - 9
The game consists of 36 domino tiles.
One half of the tile contains a digit between 1 and 9 and the other part a picture of a number of fruits, animals or objects that are well known to every child.
The tiles are shuffled and dealt. The aim is to play the tiles one by one and then trying to match the digit and the number of objects of the already played tiles on the table.
The tiles are color coded to make it easier for the youngest children.
This is a nice little self-published game by John Deere. Essentially a spin-and-move race game with a farming theme.

The name of the game translates into English as Skillful Flyers and it's about birds. Actually there are three different games in the box that are all playable with the same set of components. The games are of different difficulty levels and it's intended that as the child grows the game(s) can grow together with him. The goal of all the three games is to collect a specified set of 15 different animals.

One of the scarce editions from the so-called Pepys "Double Feature Games".

As with most of the Pepys games there are cards (50), but added is a game mat of paper (enforced with raw canvas on the back) and 6 cardboard speedway riders on wooden stands.

Aim of the game is to round the course the quickest with your rider, trying to block your opponent, just like in real speedway.

Players use their different cards (a hand of 7) to funnel out the best combinations, as the cards have different movement value which can be combined with other cards to more advantage.

Before game starts, players have to agree the number of track rounds they play (4 is the number in real speedway).

Star Trek Battle Manual was Zocchi's first foray into space combat games. It is very similar to his later Alien Space Battle Manual and Star Fleet Battle Manual, with good reason. Zocchi did not have a license to publish a Star Trek game when this came out, and in addition to the title the game uses images of ships from the series (the Enterprise is on the cover), not to mention the names and pictures of the characters. Paramount objected and Zocchi was forced to recall the game from stores. A few copies had already been sold, which are the ones occasionally seen today.

The following year, Zocchi released Alien Space Battle Manual, with all the Star Trek references replaced with ones he invented. When he obtained a license from Franz Josef Designs (allowing him to use ship designs and names such as "Klingon"), he published Star Fleet Battle Manual.

In this game, optional rules are given for the special abilities of some of the Enterprise crew - these of course do not appear in the later games.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Front of Box:

"The Exciting new game invented especially for bubblegum card collectors.

"Experience the fun of card collecting and trading, card auctions, flipping contests, attic finds and much, much more.

A game the whole family can enjoy.  May be played by 2 to 6 players."

Back of Box:

"Now you can have the fun of putting together your own collection of Tobacco Cards, Goudeys, Bowmans, Rookie Cards of your favorite super stars and other rare cards.  Build your collection through trading, card auctions and flipping contests.  Each card is worth points.  You win the game by collecting cards totaling 1,500 points.  you get a starter collection of star cards, common cards and collector's money but then it's up to you.  However, beware of drawing those special cards.  They're full of surprises!  Just when you are about to win you may have to give up your best star card.

"An easy-to-play game for baseball card collectors of all ages.

"A great family game that can be played by from 2 to 6 collectors.

"An exciting and fast moving game of skill, luck and fun for beginning and advanced collectors.  New collectors will pick up hobby terminology and learn to recognize names of rare cards.  Seasoned collectors will find that the game recreates real life card collecting experiences.

"Here is what players have to say: 'Scott's Baseball Card Game is different every time it is played'...'If you like baseball card collecting, you'll love this game'...'There has never been a game like it.'"

Address on back of box is Scott's Baseball Cards, P.O. Box 12424, Salem, OR 97309

Subtitled "The City Of London Send-Up Game", In The Money allows players to choose a city career and try to make it to the top as a banker, lawyer, stockbroker, accountant or property dealer.

Players attempt to create a valuable team to help them get to the top - blackmail, stress, death, nepotism and luck all feature in this game.

From the Directions:
RANKO is a game for 2,3, or 4 players using a deck of 48 cards, each having a numeral.  Each RANKO player is dealt 8 cards which he must place in a rack in the order dealt.  Then, by replacing various cards in his rack he tries to get his cards ranked so that the numbers represented on the cards are in increasing order from front to rear.

Four different games are possible since there are four different decks of cards:
(a) Pink cards have numerals 1 to 48 in standard form: 1, 2, 3, 4,..., 46, 47, 48.
(b) Blue cards have numerals 1 to 48 in non-standard form: (36/36), (2^3/2^2), (9/3),..., (7^2-2), (2^4x3).
(c) Yellow cards have numerals from 1 to 48 in base five:
1five, 2five, 3five,..., 142five, 143five.
(d) White cards have numerals for the multiples of 4 from 4 to 192: 4, 8, 12, 16,..., 184, 188, 192."

Shredder has a terror plan to destroy the city's entire sewer system. He has filled all water reservoirs with deadly laser balls. His henchmen stand ready at the gates to let the laser balls roll deep into the sewer system and destroy the entire foundation of the city.

The mutant heroes must defeat Shredder at the Technodrome to save the city.

Contents:

     1 threedimensional Board
     4 Turtle Figures
     1 Shredder Figure
     6 Sewer Covers
     6 Enemies
     10 Laserballs
     1 sixsided Die
     1 foursided Die
     1 Rulebook
family: Comic Books: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles

boardgame: Which Way, Please? A Map Reference Game
description:
Which Way, Please? is a map reference game for children Ages 4 and up. It is designed to teach small children about the concept of grid maps. It consists of six collecting boards divided into nine grid squares, and 54 picture cards that magnify the village images on the collecting squares. The picture cards have grid references on their backs.

There are three games that can be played. The Solo Game asks the child to go through the picture cards one by one and match the grid references to the correct square on the right collecting board. When all cards are placed, the child then turns the cards over to see if the pictures correspond to those on the boards.

The Lotto Game doles out the collecting boards to each child. Each child in turn takes a card and shows it to everyone. If a player recognizes it as one that belongs on their board, that child gets the card. The first one to fill up the board is the winner.

In the Skill Game, the boards are connected to create a large village. The cards are turned up one by one, and the first child to call out the correct grid reference wins the card. The child with the most cards is the winner.

Racing Worms boldly states that it is "A Little one's First Game", with no reading skills required. Each player controls one flexible 3-D worm (complete with hat and eyes), with a peg at each end which fit into holes in the board. On your turn you spin a spinner which gives two numbers ranging from 1 to 3- you move the front peg one number and the back peg the other - subject of course to the bendiness of your worm! Each player advances along their own track from their plant pot to an apple tree. The tracks do crossover so worms do get in each other's way. First to the apple tree wins!

A basic childrens card game with the cartoon character Popeye as the theme.

It appears there are two Popeye Playing Card Game the other is Popeye Card Game. One set has the split cards the other is used for playing Animal Rummy. Most likely this should be listed as two separate games with the one falling under a rummy version.

boardgame: Shooters! Alias: The Hole in the Wall Game
description:
Description on the rules sheet:

A set of six finished wooden "pieces", one die, a set of 500 cards with 2,500 throughly fascinating facts on everything from Wiley Women to Ornery Outlaws, a box of "ammo", and a playing board.  All of the above are contained in a beautiful red box with the silver letters "SHOOTERS" on the front.

Be the first to get to the center of the badge and become "sheriff".  After successfully shooting it out with any remaining gunslingers, the sheriff then becomes "Marshal" and winner of the game.

Aero-Ball - It's loads of fun!"

The object here is to take turns tossing a marble. Depending on where it lands gives you the score, and you move your marker along the game board to see who reaches the finish line first

This is a really cool looking spin-and-move football game with the added twist of "you call the play" that influences the outcome of the spin.

Simple components include:
box w/ instruction inside lid
2 player chits
football field w/ attached spinner

The art deco look to the game really sets it a part.

A music lyric quiz game for individual or team play. Players can be required to either recall the lines of their favorite songs, or to perform them, or describe song titles in charades or doodles. Examples:

- What are guilty and have no rhythm in the chorus of George Michael's Careless Whisper? 
- In a song by Take That, which seven words follow: Whatever I said, whatever I did, I didn't mean it... ? 
- Sing any lyric from different songs equal to the number just rolled on the black dice containing the word Really 
- Charade: Tie Me Kangaroo Down Sport

Opinion: As the publisher are UK company, the song choices probably contain a UK bias.

Hockey Action is a game of strategy and chance. The winner is the one who will scores the most goals by using the players in a strategic way, while benefiting from some elements of chance that the different cards might provide.

This game also provides you to be a coach because it's you who places your own players to fool your opponent. It provides you to be a hockey player by drawing the pass cards to get a shot on goal. You're also the goaltender by keeping secret your weak spot from your opponent.

Even if you don't know the rules of hockey, this game will gives you much pleasure. It's very easy to play and you'll be able soon to even beat experts on the matter.

Box says this is an "Exciting Tabletop Baseball Game". Hidden pitching controls, pitch a fastball, slowball, high ball or sinker. Bat the ball with spring activated bat. Play and score like a real baseball game.

Fast-Action Games created many "quick-play" sports simulations where the outcome of a contest could be decided with just one or two dice rolls.  These games made it easy to play an entire series of games in a matter of just a few minutes.

This game covers professional baseball (Major League).  Like all of the team sports games in this line, a few simple numbers, devised from team statistics, enable the player to recreate an entire season in a short period of time.

Fast-Action Games created many "quick-play" sports simulations where the outcome of a contest could be decided with just one or two dice rolls.  These games made it easy to play an entire series of games in a matter of just a few minutes.

Game OPTIONS include placing bets, timing your races & more! Complete game costs only $15 postpaid.

Fast-Action Games created many "quick-play" sports simulations where the outcome of a contest could be decided with just one or two dice rolls.  These games made it easy to play an entire series of games in a matter of just a few minutes.

This game covers horse racing, something the designer knows quite a bit about, having created supplements to Avalon Hill's Win Place & Show game.

Bingorello is a game for seniors (60+), but for their families as well. A game that consists of 90 cards with 5 different flowers and a number. By rolling a die (NOT included in the game), it is decided which cards can be given to your opponent or turned around. First player to lose all his/her cards wins.

Released: Essen 2007.

Crime Wave is the latest expansion for Heroes Incorporated. The Super Villains turn the tables on the Heroes in an attempt to overrun Megalopolis! New Heroes join the fight, but is it too late to save the city?

Contents:


     2 new Heroes with brand new powers
     4 new Super Villains
     20 new Research Cards, including new cards which allow you to draw and play more cards without using Action Markers
     8 Event Cards, which alter the balance of power each round
     Game pieces for a 5th player 


This expansion is slated for a 2009 release.

In the box are 6 distinct math-orientated board games.  They teach such concepts as addition, subtraction, place value, counting 1 to 100, colour and shape, and time.

The board games are specifically designed for year 1 and 2.

Included within are:
Addition Adder
Colour Shape Sheriff (which is a Candyland Variant)
Time Travel 
and 3 others.

The game is the successor of the Global Air Race game from the same company.
Game components are an 8 inch metal globe with large numbered and colour-coded base with integrated two-dimensional spinner, 4 coloured magnetic jet planes, 16 flight plan and 32 chance and a large lot of fuel cards, play money and a transparent distance finder.

You have to round the globe first, starting in New York in eastern direction. On the way you have to land on 4 pre-dealt cities depicted on the flight plan cards. You encounter hazards and have to refuel your plane.

In order to move you whirl the whole globe (hence the name of the game), and the two-dimensional spinner at the globe base connected to the globe shows on the inside what chance cards and hazards are encountered and if and how much you can refuel. On the outside is shown how many hours (points)you can fly and how much fuel is consumed.

And there is also a colour code depicted on the globe base, which adapts to the various countries spread all over the "world".
You have to take all these informations into account to work out the best route with the help of the transparent distance finder depicting the travel distances point-to-point.

There are also some extra advantages to gain achieving some goals during your travels (such as crossing the equator).
Vintage horse racing game

boardgame: Little Benny: The Eager Beaver Game
description: Appears to be a typical children's roll and move type game. Benny and his beaver family try not to get eaten by wolves or killed by humans. (from Alvin's Vintage Board Games)

Inspired by the 1936 tour of the Million Dollar Rexall train. The 12-car train, the longest in the World at that time, visited every state in the US and many points in Canada in 8 months, covering over 30,000 miles.

This is a spin-and-move children's race game billed as "Exciting, Amusing And educational". The race starts and ends in Boston, Mass.

Up to eight people can play as individuals. Eighteen people can play as partners. Each person (or partnership) is randomly assigned an occupation: manufacturer, planter, jeweler, broker, inventor, doctor, lawyer or publisher. Each player begins the game with a wad of cash. Each player will move one’s own playing piece along the game board’s track. Players move along the gameboard according to a pair special dice, which, together, yield a random number from, zero to six. Along the way, each player must buy all six sections of an assigned possession as a victory condition.

The gameboard is divided into six sections. No player may advance to the next section until all the players have completed the previous section:
Paths of Fortune - Each player moves along a separate path using only one die.  Players may pay and collect money from each other. Each player has a guide card, which says what to pay or collect and from whom as one advances along the path.
The Trading Area - Four trains, four planes, four ships and four buses are placed on random Purchase spaces in this area. Here players can buy transportation for use in the next section for price determined by a spinner or barter with other players.
Transportation Department - If a player owns the method of transportation on the space landed there is no penalty. If the player does not own the method of the transportation landed on that player must pay all the players who possess that vehicle. The penalty escalates with the number of vehicles of the designated type owned.
The Jamboree - Whenever two or more players land on the same space, all the players on that space spin the spinner and the player who gets the lowest number pays the penalty the space assigns.
‘Round and ‘Round Circle - If a player has yet to own all six parts on the assigned possession, the player must buy the remaining parts at double the price spun on the spinner. Advancing in this section means paying other players because of their occupation or because one lacks the transportation mode the space asks for. Players continue in this section until rolling doubles.
The Pay Off - Players sell their possessions back to the bank for whatever space on which they land.

When all the players have reached the end of The Pay Off section, each type of vehicle is sold back to the bank for values as determined by the spinner. Bank loans are paid back at 50% interest. The player with the most money wins.
This spin-and-move game, with players moving their colored pawns around a winding track.  The spinner features a picture of an old car losing its fender.

The Pixie War between the men of Hopville and the men of Skipville.  Board features a yellow and blue checkerboard with pictures of Pixies on many of the boxes.  It includes 12 red and 12 black markers and a die.

You Only Dice Twice was created for a two-player dice game design competition.  The hardest thing to include in most dice games is some kind of player interaction, so that was a major design goal.

Players race to be the first to close out the most and most valuable number categories.  Each turn, you roll six dice, but you can only use three of them for yourself--you have to let your opponent use the other three!

The only things needed to play are six ordinary dice, two pencils, and the provided score sheets.

The object of the game is to steal grain from the other player's miller.  Whoever steals the most grain wins.

The board has a red and black track along the outside of the board.
This game involves spinning a spinner and collecting cards for various animals until someone has all of the cards.

This Gabriel-Nealey Co. game is played with smooth 7/8" plastic chips on a 15x15 grid. The published game board has squares that are about 15/16" each side. The object of the game is to be the first to get 5 chips in a row, horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It is the same game as Go-Moku.

While the edges of the game board are decorated with various configurations of chips, these are basic patterns from which the game may be won. Gabriel-Nealey Co. also published the 60-page booklet "Onino Basic Plays" by Henmar Ruskin, copyright 1942.

Play starts by placing random tiles (there are 52 and they match the 52 cards in a standard deck, but with no suits) in the corners of the board, then alternate taking turns placing tiles to one side of the board to score the cards/tiles as a Cribbage hand. You can play up to 4 tiles to get a bonus and score all the rows and columns you play tiles into.

Contains:

    52 tiles
    drawstring bag
    4 tile racks
    game board
    rules

In Angel in the Attic, players take the part of the Willowby family, who must search the house to find the missing pieces of the nativity set on Christmas morning. The children in the family liked to play with the nativity set so the pieces are scattered throughout the house. Players must use logic to correctly discover which piece was the last to be found, which room it was found in, and who found it.

This is a Christian Family game, which uses a mechanic similar to that of Clue (the cards players try to identify are placed in an envelope under the board), but without the murder theme. There are variations provided in the rules to make it more or less challenging depending on the ages of the children involved.

The game features a miniature nativity set and distinctive art on the board, box, and player tokens. The game also came with an offer from the publishers to order new custom drawn player tokens of your family based on a photo you mailed in.
Popeye's Game is a tiddly-winks variation, where the object is to propel your tiddly-wink through a hoop featuring Popeye characters or items.

This roll-and-move game features a winding yellow track on top of a green (grassy) background.  It's "a jolly game for 2, 3, or 4 players." From the Powerhouse Museum "The game is significant because it uses scenarios familiar to Australian country motorists during the first half of the twentieth century, when Australian roads were largely poor, gravel sections and steep hills made conditions treacherous, and cars were unreliable. The poor road conditions sometimes saw cars needing major repairs to axles and springs. Another very Australian obstacle on the road was a flock of sheep. The game reflects the poor state of the roads and the uncertainty of arriving at your destination without some mishap occurring. The aim of the game is to progress from the petrol station at the 'start' and travel to the gate marked 'home' via 100 squares numbered 1 to 100. Players move from the start to finish as quickly as possible by taking it in turns to throw the dice. Along the way various motoring obstacles, calamities, and misadventures including punctures, collisions, wrong turns, road blocks, flocks of sheep, bridges under repair, gravel on the road, broken springs, steep hills and axle trouble must be negotiated. These all require the player to miss throws or return to the start.

boardgame: Spider-Man versus Doctor Octopus: Race to Rescue Board Game
description:
Doctor Octopus has captured Mary Jane.  He has left her on the top of a tall New York building and returned to his lab.  Spider-Man has set out to rescue Mary Jane, but Doctor Octopus is also on his way back.  Who will get to Mary Jane first?  The object is to be the first to get to Mary Jane.

To start the game, each player chooses a pawn (either Spider-Man or Doc Ock).  The Spider-Man player takes the Spider-Man cards, and the Doc Ock player takes Doc Ock cards.  The cards are used to move its player forward one or two spaces or to force the opponent to move back a space or lose a turn.  Players take turns turning over cards with the goal of moving his/her pawn closer to Mary Jane.

A game that combines Chess and cards.

This is how it is described at the game's website:
Chards TM uses a deck of 52 cards (Chards) with the normal suits: Hearts, Spades, Diamonds, and Clubs. Each player selects a favorite suit, places their King on the board and begins to play. Chards are added to the board during play anywhere on the board so the playing field—and challenge—is always changing.

Each Turn - Players do three things on their turn: Move a Chard, Turn (twist) a Chard, and Place a Chard from their stockpile. It's FUN! ... and Easy!

Chess characters include Kings, Queens, Bishops, Knights, Rooks and Pawns.

Card characters include Jacks and Jokers and Dukes (ever played with a deuce in cards?). Chards TM even has Archers and Wizards to give the game a special twist. And it’s the twists in the game that make it exciting!

There are no stalemates in Chards TM. Surprises are common so winners often come from behind. Are you clever or lucky? It takes a bit of both to win at Chards TM. That’s what makes it FUN!

Whether it’s family fun or party entertainment, Chards TM is a game that players want to play again and again. The FUN is enjoyed by all ages, be they adults or teens or kids. Have a tournament. Form a club. Use Chards TM as a fund raising event.

Most of all – Chards TM is a great way to have FUN!

Each player is supplied with a number card with the numbers 2 through 12 printed on it. Players start by placing a chip in the kitty or ante or some other predetermined amount. As each player rolls the dice they cover the corresponding number on their board much like bingo. If a 7 is thrown, the player loses a turn unless it is their first throw. In which case all players put a chip in the kitty and the player keeps throwing dice and covering numbers on their board or their neighbor's boards if he/she cannot use them until another 7 is thrown. The player who covers all their numbers wins and wins the chips in the kitty.

This is the Kainuu version of the Kaupunkipelit (City Games) series.

Unlike most games in the series, this one is not centered on a single city/town but rather a wider geographic area - in this case, the eastern-central Finland region of Kainuu, the capital of which is the town of Kajaani (and which is why there is no Kajaani version of the game).

Kaupunkipelit is a series of games, each of which is basically a light version of Monopoly localized to a city/region of Finland. The games promote local companies, councils, educational institutes etc. and were designed as a fund raising effort for local sports clubs.

The main differences between these games and Monopoly are:

-Instead of a "street" each square on the board represents a local business.
-There are no hotels or houses, but each square has two title deeds instead of just one, allowing two players to jointly own any location on the board.
-The majority of the event cards are trivia questions about the locality the game is set in.
-While the effects of the first two corner squares (Go and Jail in Monopoly) remain the same for every edition (Go and Stock Market Crash), the effect of the other two corners varies from edition to edition and is the only real difference between them (except for the trivia content, of course). They are typically also local businesses but without title deeds and thus no rent either, but some special effect instead, such as pass a turn or get an extra throw.

Mariposa is a children´s game about butterflies. In this tile laying game players form butterflies on flowers with 4 tiles. Players try to be the first to build four of these and place their butterflies on them - thereby winning the game.

A subset of the rules are available for free at:

description:
A blue plastic cookie jar is placed in the center of the table.  A deck of 40 round cards are dealt into one face-down stack per player.  There cards show a chocolate chip cookie on the back and one of several Sesame Street characters on the front.  Each player also gets two cardboard cookie chips with a unique border color.

Players count aloud and then simultaneously flip over the topmost card of their stack and then quickly scan all of the newly flipped cards for any that have matching characters on them.  If there are no pairs, then everyone counts and flips another card.  If a pair is spotted, the players must try to toss their cookie chips into the jar.  Whoever does so first, as determined by the bottommost chip, claims all of the flipped cards and adds them to the bottom of their stack.  The player that eventually claims all of the cookie cards wins.

International Game Series game from Multi-Man Publishing. It was removed from the MMP's pre-order list in July 2008.

Scaled at mostly regimental level, 4 kilometers a hex and with 4 days per turn, Decision in Normandy covers the period from the D-Day landings until the successful breakout from Normandy in late July of 1944.  Using a highly interactive game turn sequence with multiple movement and combat phases and reserve use, a firepower based CRT, and the use of situational morale states, Decision in Normandy offers a unique look at this very famous moment in World War Two.

It is all here – armor effects, breakthroughs, desperate defenses, naval gunfire, air power, artillery support, extensive England to beachhead subroutines, and more.  All in a seamless game system that still focuses on playability and elegance.

Decision in Normandy was originally published in 1981 by Epoch games of Japan.

Sequence of Play

German Player Turn:

    Weather
    1st Movement Phase
    1st Combat Phase
    1st Reorganization Phase
    Reaction Phase
    2nd Movement Phase
    2nd Combat Phase
    2nd Reorganization Phase


Allied Player Turn: The Allied Player Turn is similar to the German Player Turn, but replaces the Weather phase with Naval Gunfire and Air Attacks.

2 player aides

boardgame: Quest: A Family Home Evening Game
description:
Quest A Family Home Evening Game is a religious trivia game designed for members of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter Day Saints. It is designed for family members to play on "Family Home Evenings" but is also appropriate for other church gatherings.

Players begin at Earth and follow a "Strait and Narrow Path" (a phrase from Mormon scripture) to the Celestial Kingdom. Players advance by answering up to four religious questions per turn. Odd numbered questions are about faith/religious knowledge and even numbered questions are about Good Works. Each questions answered increases the number of spaces the player can move.

If a player answers a Faith question incorrectly, his or her turn is over. If he or she answers a Good Works question incorrectly, then the pawn is moved off the Strait and Narrow Path and onto a detour, which lengthens the route to heaven.

The first person to make it to the Celestial Kingdom is not the winner. Also, the rules encourage helping players that are falling behind. The game ends when all players arrive, thus emphasizing that Families should work together so that everyone makes it to heaven.
family: Religious: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

description:
Here is an interesting variant on the Battleship concept.  Each player has 2 boards side by side, one is on your left - this is where you place your ships secretly.  On the right is where you make your guesses, but when you make your guess, you push a Laser Missile Launcher - if you get a hit, some light misting water squirts the other player.  It also features sound effects to include explosions.

Like the classic Chinese checkers game board reconfigured as "Canadian checkers" for Canada's centennial in 1967, now there's Electronic Hydro Battle--an even more elaborate, tricked-out version of that other battleship-sinking game, which here shall remain nameless. Apparently it's no longer enough that an opponent calls out grid coordinates in an effort to find and sink the hidden fleet you've placed on your side of the board. With Electronic Hydro Battle, players guess the location of opponents' ships by inserting a wired "torpedo launcher" into the screen, switchboard-style. Missile sounds are heard; if you strike an opposing craft, you'll also hear a blast, followed by the spectacle of your victim getting sprayed with a squirt of water from the game's refillable tank! Whether this funky marriage of "Mighty Mo" and Gong Show represents a high-water mark of fun or a sign of low tide is up to you.

The object of Hydro Battle game is to be the first player to destroy all five of your opponent's ships. If the Launching Sound is followed by a Blast Sound that will also be followed with your opponent getting sprayed.

The game is played in two courses. In the first course (Duck), both of you, after playing to a trick, draw the top card from the stock to restore your hand to thirteen cards. In the second course (Soup), which begins when the stock runs out, you play your hands out to a finish.
    
You'll find it more profitable to win a middling number of tricks in both courses than many in one course and few in the other because you each calculate your final score for the whole deal by multiplying together your two scores for the individual courses. For example, if in the Duck course you took 6 tricks for 7 points, and in the Soup course you took 5 tricks for 8 points, your final score will be 7 x 8 = 56.

boardgame: Gliders from the Sky: The Fall of Eben Emael
description:
This is a game on the fall of the Belgian fortress of Eben Emael, May 10-11 1940, by a daring and unheard of, at time, glider attack made by less than 100 German paratroopers. It's a fast and furious solitaire game (you take the German side and try to make the best of it), that should be playable in less than an hour, once you master the game.

To try to make the victory harder to get, several variants and optional rules are provided, based on the historical situation and on several what if situations that could have happened on those fateful hours that cleared the way for the German initial smashing victory in the West.

For one or more players.
Play time is approx. 15-20 Mins. or more depending on how many players you have.

Set includes: one tin bowling lane (29.75"L X 6.25"W), 10 real wooden pins, two black marbles, and, one tin bowler figure.

You start the game by setting up the bowler on the end of the lane. His arm is pulled back behind a lever and the ball (marble) is placed on a stand in front of the bowlers right arm. Place the ten wooden pins on the ten markers. Now you aim and pull the trigger to release the bowlers arm and hopefully you've got yourself a strike! You score the game exactly the same as you would a real game.

A well-equipped game with a seldom used scenario: Kart Racing !

Before starting the game the players agree how many times they want to round the racecourse.

Each one starts with a different kart and tries to outwit the opponent upon grading up his race gear, either by card play or by investing money. The money has to be earned during the race.

You can do this by reaching different spaces on the racecourse or by playing one of the 50 action-cards at the right time, on top of throwing the right number on the die.

These cards allow weird actions, such as throwing a hay bale (come with the game- although in plastic...)in front of another cart or hauling it in with a lasso! For certain situations you can even earn a gold medal.

Winner is the player with the most points for the best kart, the most money, the most rounds in the lead, the best actions, for holding the gold medal etc.

Playing pieces include a Lighthouse, Cookie the Cow, Train, Cherry Pie, Yacht and Anchor.
publishers: Late for the Sky Production Company

description: Nimble Numbers is a partnership Tic-tac-Toe variant that uses rotating disks.  Additionally, besides just trying to get 3 in a row, you attempt to collect combinations of numbers to improve your scoring.

In this game you can replay the show Quiz Taxi where a well known tv host drives around in a taxi (he's the driver) and everybody who calls this certain taxi suddenly (and without them knowing)is a participant in a tv quiz show. The questions get asked on the way to the participant's original destination. If they fail, the taxi stops and they have to get out, if they don't fail more than three times (they have 2 jokers - a phone call and asking people on the street) and they get to their destination, they can either get the money or play at high risk to lose everything, or win twice as much.

"Empires  in Harm" is a strategic and diplomatic Napoleonic wargame for between seven to ten players (depending on the Campaign selected) that covers warfare in Europe during the 18th and early 19th centuries, expanding the legendary Australian Design Group/Avalon Hill board-wargame "Empires in Arms".

The military counters in the game generally represent Corps and Squadrons, with each army factor being equivalent to roughly 1000 to 3000 men and each “ship” equivalent to one ship of the line or a number of frigates & sloops.

Current version: 5.2
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
This official extension has been published in the French magazine "Des jeux sur un plateau" issue 40.

This extension includes John McNeal winter pack (character sheet plus 4 new items), Eva Kramer winter pack (Character sheet plus 3 new items), Ksiaz castel pathfinding resume, Ksiaz crypt pathfinding resume and Tesla base pathfinding resume.

Modern combat in a variety of theaters.  Uses same Warfighter rules system as The Guards and Movement to Contact.

Platoon-level combat uses step-loss damage system and 3 different combat tables (direct fire, assault combat, indirect fire).  Scenarios written to resemble US Army operations orders.  Modular geomorphic maps include both woodland and desert maps, varying urban/mountainous terrain.

Combat involves rolling dice based on unit's firepower value, subtracting target's armor, and looking up result on CRT - stun, flip, retreat, kill, no effect.  All damage applied simultaneously, so 'killed' units get their final shots.

Recon units have separate objectives to acquire/locate targets of intel value.

Additional rules cover engineers (obstacles & fortifications), scouts (stealth movement & recon by fire), leaders (special battlefield effects), and compatible with civilians-on-the-battlefield rules from The Guards.

3 die-cut countersheets (56/sheet, large 7/8in counters), rules, 4 d/s maps, reference sheets for each set of units, scenarios (see below), blank scenario sheets, cardstock admin counters (non-cut), errata sheet with some counter corrections.  US mechanized battalion compatible replacement for non-die-cut counters from Movement to Contact.

Units include:

    US
    Russian / Arab
    Israeli
    Arab Irregulars
    Partisan Irregulars
    Australian


5 scenarios in the box (others available online):

    US v Iraqi irregulars
    Arab v Israeli
    Russian v Chechen
    US v Balkan irregulars
    Australian v Partisan irregulars


All scenarios written as paired sheets, so each player has their own instructions that are unknown to the opponent.

"The game consists of 21 figures... one of Charlie McCarthy and four each of Edgar Bergen, Don Ameche, Dorothy Lamour, Nelson Eddy, and Robert Armbruster.  Also a spinner which determines play."

The spinner features a picture of the puppet Charlie McCarthy.

This game has a very unique theme: collecting scrap metal for the war effort.

The board features four scrap collection centers, four iron foundries, and paths among them.

This game's board has a maze of colored squares.  Players are attempting to go from one part of the board to another.  The theme of the game is related to Native Americans.

On the box the game is Top Pacheeza, but on the board the game is Top Pacheesa.

Hopsi the kangaroo is excited. It's about to go on holidays. The players help Hopsi to pack his bags. 
The game consists of 5 quadratic boards each showing items that Hopsi may need for its holiday such as a toothbrush or bathing suit.
Four of the boards belong to the players, the fifth is the one where Hopsi moves around.
The youngest player rolls the dice and moves Hopsi from one up to three spaces. He is allowed to mark the space (item) that he lands on on his map with a red chip.
The first player who achieves marking a row of five spaces (vertically, horizontally or diagonally) wins the game.

from the back of the box: "Have you ever wondered where the Weirdos gather?  Welcome to Weird U.S., where colorful characters like the Melon Head, Skunk Ape, La Llorona, Bunnyman, Frogboy and the Killer Clown journey to not so far off places like Midgetville, Crybaby Bridge, Danvers State Hospital and the Land of 2093 Milk Jugs in their attempts to collect travelers and be the last Weirdo standing!  Based on the stories from Weird U.S. by Mark Moran and Mark Sceurman.  From Weird books come Weird games."

Players move around the board trying to gather travelers while dodging being exposed by Mark and Mark and battling the other Weirdos.

The game is rated Ages 8 and up.

boardgame: Murder à la carte: The Brie, the Bullet & the Black Cat
description:
You have been invited to dinner at the Official Residence of the Deputy Mayor of Casablanca. The guest of honor was to have been France's greatest ever living mime artist - but he fails to appear...you've guessed it, he's been murdered...

Everyone is a suspect, but who is the murderer?

So who stars in this great adventure, obviously you, but you don't play yourself you take on the role of one of the following characters:

Hughes Le Grandebutte - Deputy Mayor of Casablanca The epitome of solid respectability
Edith Le Grandebutte - his wife, former dancer Married to Hughes - but not altogether happy - still yearn for your former life as a dancer
Nicole Le Grandbutte - their daughter 18 yr old daughter of above - would rather be in Paris than Casablanca - does not approve of her father working with the Germans
Otto Von Pinkelwurst - a Gestapo Officer, fanatical and possible mad Doesn't understand why he is in Africa rather than a senior staff post in Berlin
Kirk Ransom III - an American who runs Kirk's African Cafe in downtown Casablanca late 30's, doomed romantic hero nursing a broken heart and a bottle of whiskey. Suave, elegant and slightly battered.
Countess Bogov - an exiled Russian aristocrat, glamorous and mysterious. Passionate anti-communist; sympathetic with Germans
